{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
[Date]

Environmental Protection Agency
Office of Air and Radiation
[Docket ID: EPA-HQ-OAR-2025-0194]

Re: Comments on Proposed Regulatory Framework to Update Compliance and Oversight Mechanisms (EPA-HQ-OAR-2025-0194)

Dear Administrator Regan and EPA Staff,

My name is Robert Thompson, and I am writing to you today as a retired steelworker with decades of industrial exposure, now living with occupational lung disease on a fixed income near an industrial zone. I strongly support the Environmental Protection Agency's proposed regulatory framework to update compliance and oversight mechanisms (EPA-HQ-OAR-2025-0194). This rule is not an abstract policy for me; it is a vital step toward protecting the health and financial well-being of individuals like myself who have borne the irreversible consequences of inadequate air quality enforcement.

[BACKGROUND]
The proposed rule introduces a comprehensive overhaul of how regulated entities interact with the agency, focusing specifically on the modernization of reporting obligations, the standardization of documentation, the establishment of new implementation timelines, and the requirement for Cost Assessment and Operational Impact Analyses. These provisions are critically important for improving the agency's ability to monitor compliance and assess the effectiveness of environmental regulations, ultimately leading to cleaner air and healthier communities.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
My personal experience highlights why these proposed changes are so necessary.

First, regarding the **Cost Assessment Requirement**: I was diagnosed with occupational lung disease as a direct result of workplace air quality failures. I now manage my household on a fixed retirement income, where healthcare costs alone consume a disproportionate share of my monthly budget. According to various benchmarks, average monthly expenses for an individual can include $300–$500 or more in healthcare costs alone [1]. The agency's Cost Assessment Requirement, as currently framed, encourages regulated entities to submit data on their 'expected costs' of transitioning to new standards. This framing risks creating a one-sided cost record dominated by industry compliance costs, while the ongoing costs imposed on workers and retirees with occupational illness go uncounted. Every dollar industry saves by avoiding rigorous reporting is a cost I absorb in medical bills, reduced quality of life, and the inability to prove causation if I ever needed to seek legal remedy. The agency specifically asked about the expected costs of the proposed compliance requirements — and I submit that the analysis of costs is dangerously incomplete if it counts only industry compliance expenditures while ignoring the enormous, ongoing costs that inadequate monitoring imposes on workers and residents like me who bear the health consequences of under-reported emissions [3, 4]. I am also concerned that the Cost Assessment Requirement will generate a flood of industry-submitted cost data that the agency may use to justify extended timelines or weakened standards. For me, delay is not a neutral outcome. My lung disease is progressive. Every additional year of inadequate air quality enforcement is a year of accelerated health decline, increased medical expenditure, and reduced quality of life. For example, the financial consequences of early, health-driven retirement, a category I fall into, are permanent: lower lifetime Social Security benefits, reduced pension accrual, and decades of elevated healthcare costs on a diminished income [2].

Second, concerning the **Operational Impact Analysis requirement**: I wake up every morning managing breathing difficulties that are a direct consequence of decades of workplace and residential exposure to industrial air pollutants. On high-emission days near my neighborhood, my symptoms worsen measurably — I use my rescue inhaler more frequently, I cannot walk to the corner store, and I face out-of-pocket medical costs that strain my fixed retirement income. The current regulatory framework has never formally required the EPA to account for these day-to-day operational realities on my side of the fence. This proposal, if implemented with a resident-centered scope, would change that. The agency asked specifically about 'operational impacts of the proposed compliance requirements.' For a retired steelworker managing a household on Social Security and a modest pension, the operational reality is stark: I cannot hire an attorney to pursue a personal injury claim against a former employer, I cannot relocate away from polluted areas, and I cannot afford the out-of-pocket costs of worsening respiratory illness without financial strain. Bankrate's 2025 data, for instance, shows hidden homeownership costs averaging over $21,000 per year nationally [1]. On a fixed income, there is no margin to absorb these costs if my health deteriorates further due to continued inadequate air quality enforcement. The proposed rule's stronger compliance and documentation standards are, for me, a form of financial protection [3, 4].

Third, I strongly support the **Updated Documentation Standards**: I live on a fixed retirement income and have been tracking my respiratory symptoms against local air quality readings for years, trying to understand whether ongoing industrial emissions in my area are aggravating my occupational lung disease. Right now, the data I can access through AirNow and the Air Quality System (AQS) is reported in inconsistent formats by different monitoring entities, making it extremely difficult for an ordinary person without technical training or legal resources to draw meaningful connections between pollution spikes and my health episodes [5]. Standardized documentation formats would allow me — and patient advocates or community health workers helping people like me — to actually use this data.

Fourth, I strongly support the **New Implementation Timelines** and urge the agency to hold firm on their enforcement: I spent decades working in a steel mill where airborne particulate exposure was a daily reality. I now manage chronic respiratory illness on a fixed retirement income, relying on Medicare and limited savings to cover ongoing pulmonary treatments, specialist visits, and medications [6]. Every year that regulated entities are permitted to delay compliance with updated reporting and documentation standards is another year that the agency lacks the data it needs to identify facilities still exposing workers and communities to dangerous air pollution levels. For someone in my condition, worsening air quality is not an abstract policy concern — it is a direct threat to my remaining lung function and quality of life [7].

Fifth, I strongly support the **Revised Reporting Obligations**: I was diagnosed with occupational lung disease after decades of workplace exposure, and I now live on a fixed retirement income with no access to attorneys or environmental consultants. When I try to monitor whether the facility near my home is complying with air quality standards, I am confronted with inconsistent, fragmented data submissions that are nearly impossible for a layperson to interpret. The current patchwork of reporting formats means I must spend what little advocacy time and energy I have simply trying to understand whether a violation has occurred — time that could be spent actually holding polluters accountable [8]. Standardized reporting protocols would allow me and community groups I rely on to more efficiently identify non-compliance without expensive legal intermediaries [9].

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To ensure this rule achieves its full potential and truly serves the public interest, I offer the following specific recommendations:

1.  **Cost Assessment Requirement**: The agency should explicitly broaden the Cost Assessment Requirement to solicit data not only from regulated entities on their transition costs, but also from affected individuals — including retirees, workers with occupational illness, and low-income households — on the ongoing healthcare and quality-of-life costs they bear as a result of inadequate air quality protections. The agency should issue a supplemental data call or public survey targeting these populations, and should instruct its economic analysts to incorporate worker health cost data into the regulatory impact analysis as a benefit offset against industry compliance costs [1, 2].
2.  **Operational Impact Analysis**: The agency should explicitly define 'operational impacts' in the final rule to encompass impacts on nearby residential populations — including symptom management burdens, healthcare utilization costs, and lost daily functioning — not solely the compliance and reporting burdens on regulated facilities. The Operational Impact Analysis should require facilities to submit data on ambient air quality conditions in adjacent residential zones as part of their compliance documentation, so the agency can correlate facility operational changes with measurable community health outcomes [3, 4]. I urge the EPA to add a community health impact sub-section to the required Operational Impact Analysis template.
3.  **Updated Documentation Standards**: The EPA should ensure that the standardized documentation formats required under this rule include a publicly accessible, plain-language summary layer — not just machine-readable data for regulated entities. Specifically, the agency should require that AQS submissions and real-time AirNow data feeds use consistent field definitions, timestamps, and pollutant identifiers so that community members and health advocates can cross-reference industrial emission events with health symptom logs without needing to hire technical consultants [5]. The agency should also create a free, downloadable personal health correlation template aligned with the new standardized format.
4.  **New Implementation Timelines**: The agency should resist any industry pressure to extend the proposed compliance windows. If anything, EPA should consider establishing interim milestone reporting requirements within the implementation timeline so that regulated entities cannot simply wait until the final deadline to begin transitioning. The agency should also clarify that failure to meet interim milestones will trigger enforcement action, not merely additional extensions [6, 7]. This would ensure the modernized reporting framework begins generating actionable data as quickly as possible. Furthermore, I urge the EPA to reject any industry argument that transition costs justify weakening or delaying the new standards. The agency should establish a clear analytical standard in the final rule stating that industry-submitted cost data will be evaluated against the full spectrum of benefits, including avoided health costs, reduced occupational disease incidence, and the economic value of extended healthy life years for affected workers and retirees [1, 2]. The agency should set a rebuttable presumption that implementation timelines will not be extended based solely on industry cost submissions, absent a showing that the extension would not result in additional harm to populations with existing occupational or environmental illness. The agency should further require that any entity seeking an extended timeline demonstrate what interim protective measures it will implement during the delay period.
5.  **Revised Reporting Obligations**: The EPA should ensure that the revised reporting obligations include a public-facing data portal — similar to the 2024 Chemical Data Reporting resource portal already developed under TSCA — that translates standardized facility submissions into plain-language summaries accessible to non-expert community members [9]. The agency should explicitly require that submitted data be machine-readable and searchable by geographic location so that individuals like me can monitor local facilities without legal assistance [8].
6.  **Overall Cost-Benefit Analysis**: In responding to its own question about the costs of the proposed compliance requirements, the EPA should conduct a full social cost accounting that includes avoided healthcare costs, reduced emergency room visits, and improved productivity for residents near regulated facilities — not only industry compliance costs. Specifically, the agency should commission or cite existing health economics research quantifying the per-capita medical cost burden on low-income and retired residents living adjacent to industrial zones, and weigh that against facility reporting costs [3, 4]. The final rule's preamble should include this bilateral cost-benefit framing so the record is complete. I also urge the EPA to establish a low-barrier public comment mechanism (such as a simplified online form or toll-free phone line) so that retirees and individuals with limited resources can provide the lived-experience cost data the agency says it is seeking, without needing legal representation or technical expertise [1, 2].

[CONCLUSION]
This proposed rule is a critical step towards a more transparent, accountable, and protective air quality regulatory system. As someone living with the direct health and financial consequences of past pollution, I implore the EPA to prioritize the health and well-being of workers and communities over industry compliance costs. Stronger, well-enforced standards are the only mechanism available to people without legal or financial resources to seek individual relief.

Thank you for the opportunity to comment on this important proposal.

Sincerely,

Robert Thompson
Retired Steelworker

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The American Farm Bureau Federation (AFBF) is the nation's largest general farm organization, representing 6 million member families across the country. Our mission is to advocate for farmers and ranchers, promote the vitality of rural communities, and ensure a safe, abundant, and affordable food supply for all Americans. We write to express our strong opposition to the Environmental Protection Agency's (EPA) proposed regulatory framework, particularly its "Revised Reporting Obligations," "New Implementation Timelines," "Updated Documentation Standards," "Cost Assessment Requirement," and "Operational Impact Analysis" provisions, as they relate to agricultural operations.

AFBF recognizes the importance of modernizing compliance and oversight mechanisms. However, this proposal, as currently structured, fails to account for the unique operational realities, seasonal production cycles, and financial constraints of America's farms and ranches. Uniform compliance standards and timelines designed for industrial facilities will impose disproportionate and unsustainable burdens on agricultural operations, ultimately threatening the economic viability of family farms and the nation's food security.

[BACKGROUND]
EPA's proposal introduces a comprehensive overhaul of how regulated entities interact with the agency, focusing on the modernization of reporting obligations and the standardization of documentation. Key provisions include:
*   Revised Reporting Obligations: Requiring adherence to new protocols for submitting data.
*   New Implementation Timelines: Establishing specific windows for compliance.
*   Updated Documentation Standards: Mandating recordkeeping according to new, standardized formats.
*   Cost Assessment Requirement: Encouraging data submission on "expected costs."
*   Operational Impact Analysis: Requiring review of how changes affect "operational impacts."

While the PROPOSED RULE TEXT does not explicitly mention "agricultural emissions," the docket prefix EPA-HQ-OAR (Office of Air and Radiation) and supporting evidence such as EPA's reporting on "Agriculture GHG emissions" [5] and the Air Emissions Reporting System (CAERS) [7] confirm that agricultural emissions and their associated reporting are within the scope of this office's regulatory authority. AFBF's primary concern is that these provisions, if applied universally, will impose significant and unmanageable compliance costs and operational disruptions on agricultural operations, undermining their ability to produce food, fiber, and fuel.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
**1. Disproportionate Compliance Costs and Administrative Burden:**
The "Cost Assessment Requirement" and "Revised Reporting Obligations" will impose significant financial and administrative burdens on agricultural operations. Farm profit margins are notoriously thin, with USDA data consistently showing high volatility and that even modest increases in input costs can eliminate profitability. For a family farm operation grossing $500,000 annually with a 3-8% net margin, even $5,000-$10,000 in additional annual compliance costs represents a 10-20% reduction in net income [Stakeholder Point 4]. Research on data standardization in the dairy industry found that even basic compliance upgrades cost small farms between $5,000 and $10,000, while comprehensive overhauls for larger operations exceed $50,000 [Stakeholder Point 8]. These costs are not trivial against this backdrop.

AFBF represents 6 million member farm families, the majority of whom operate on thin profit margins with no dedicated compliance or regulatory affairs staff [Stakeholder Point 1]. Preparing credible "expected cost" data submissions requires hiring consultants, conducting operational audits, and projecting multi-year transition costs—tasks that are routine for large industrial emitters but financially prohibitive for small-scale farms [Stakeholder Point 1]. For example, a family-owned livestock operation potentially subject to EPA's air emissions monitoring framework would need to model ammonia, hydrogen sulfide, PM, and VOC transition costs under new documentation standards without any agency-provided cost modeling tools or templates [Stakeholder Point 1]. This creates a participation gap where large regulated entities with legal and compliance departments will dominate the comment record, while small farm voices are structurally excluded [Stakeholder Point 1].

Rural broadband limitations further compound this burden: many farm operations lack the reliable high-speed internet connectivity required to operate new digital reporting portals, meaning compliance may require capital investment in connectivity infrastructure that EPA has not accounted for in its cost analysis [Stakeholder Point 4]. EPA's own Compliance Monitoring Program budget increase of $50.9 million in FY 2024 [3] demonstrates the agency has substantial institutional resources to absorb modernization costs—resources that farm families simply do not have [Stakeholder Point 5]. Moreover, EPA's expanding reporting agenda—including proposed PFAS reporting additions to the Toxics Release Inventory and reconsideration of animal waste air emissions reporting under EPCRA—signals that this rule is not an isolated cost but part of a cumulative regulatory burden that will compound across farm operations [Stakeholder Point 4].

**2. Incompatible Documentation Standards and Duplicative Requirements:**
AFBF opposes the one-size-fits-all standardized documentation format proposed under "Updated Documentation Standards" because it fails to account for the diversity of agricultural operations and that less burdensome alternatives exist [Stakeholder Point 2]. American agriculture encompasses an extraordinary range of operation types—from 50-acre specialty crop farms to 50,000-acre grain operations, from single-family dairies to large-scale poultry integrators—each with distinct recordkeeping needs, existing software systems, and administrative capacities [Stakeholder Point 2].

The proposed standardized format would require many farms to abandon functional, existing recordkeeping systems (including IRS-compliant farm accounting records already maintained under established standards [Stakeholder Point 2]) and replace them with EPA-specific formats that duplicate rather than integrate with existing obligations. Forcing format standardization without interoperability requirements will create duplicative recordkeeping burdens—farms maintaining one system for tax and USDA purposes and a separate system for EPA compliance—effectively doubling administrative labor with no gain in underlying data quality [Stakeholder Point 2].

**3. Operational Disruptions and Inflexible Timelines:**
AFBF opposes the proposed "New Implementation Timelines" and "Operational Impact Analysis" requirements as they fail to account for the seasonal, weather-dependent nature of agricultural production cycles. Agricultural operations cannot pause or restructure reporting and documentation systems on a calendar-year regulatory timeline. Planting, growing, harvesting, and livestock management cycles dictate when farm operators have capacity to implement new administrative systems [Stakeholder Point 6, 7].

A transition deadline falling during spring planting (March–May) or fall harvest (September–November)—the two highest-labor, highest-financial-risk periods of the year—could force farm operators to choose between regulatory compliance and operational survival [Stakeholder Point 6, 7]. For example, a grain farmer operating on a 3-5% net profit margin who must simultaneously adopt new EPA documentation standards and manage a harvest faces compounding operational risk that a manufacturing facility on a fixed production schedule does not [Stakeholder Point 7]. EPA's own Regulatory Impact Analysis for the AERR revisions acknowledges the complexity of transitioning to systems like CAERS, yet the proposal does not provide sufficient lead time or resources for the agricultural sector's unique operational realities [6, 7].

Furthermore, AFBF's policy advocacy staff—who assist member families in understanding and responding to regulatory requirements—would face a significant reallocation of resources away from farm income programs and rural community development work toward compliance administration, directly undermining the organization's core mission [Stakeholder Point 6].

**4. Threat to Food Security and Rural Communities:**
The proposed compliance cost structure, lacking any safe harbor, de minimis exemption, or tiered compliance pathway for small agricultural operations, creates a regulatory structure that will accelerate farm consolidation and undermine rural community development—an unintended consequence the agency has not analyzed and which directly contradicts EPA's stated commitments to supporting small and rural communities [Stakeholder Point 3].

When compliance costs are uniform across entity size, small farms face a proportionally larger burden than large agricultural corporations. If a small livestock or crop operation cannot afford to generate the required cost documentation, cannot meet new reporting standards on the proposed timeline, or cannot absorb the administrative overhead of the new documentation framework, the rational economic response is to exit the market or sell to a larger operator [Stakeholder Point 3]. AFBF's member families in rural communities depend on the viability of small-scale farming not just as an income source but as the economic and social foundation of rural towns—supporting local suppliers, schools, and tax bases [Stakeholder Point 3]. EPA has separately announced $30 million to help small and rural communities protect water quality, demonstrating agency awareness of rural community vulnerability. Yet this rulemaking contains no parallel protection mechanism for small agricultural operators facing compliance cost burdens [Stakeholder Point 3].

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To mitigate these critical impacts and ensure that compliance modernization does not compromise the viability of U.S. agriculture, AFBF urges EPA to adopt the following recommendations:

**A. Establish Exemptions and Tiered Compliance Pathways for Agriculture:**
*   **Small Farm Exemption:** Exempt farms below a defined gross revenue threshold (e.g., $1 million annual gross receipts) or acreage threshold (e.g., 500 acres) from the standardized documentation and reporting mandates entirely [Stakeholder Point 3, 5, 8].
*   **Tiered Compliance Framework:** Create a tiered compliance framework that scales documentation and reporting requirements to the size and complexity of the regulated entity [Stakeholder Point 3, 5, 8].
*   **Self-Certification Pathway:** Implement a self-certification pathway for small farms as a lower-cost alternative to full documentation audits [Stakeholder Point 3].
*   *Applicable Provisions:* Revised Reporting Obligations, Updated Documentation Standards, Cost Assessment Requirement, Operational Impact Analysis.

**B. Develop Flexible and Interoperable Documentation Standards:**
*   **Outcomes-Based Standard:** Adopt an outcomes-based data integrity standard rather than a prescriptive format mandate [Stakeholder Point 2].
*   **Minimum Data Elements:** Define only the minimum data elements required for compliance verification rather than mandating a specific format or software platform [Stakeholder Point 2].
*   **Interoperability Standards:** Establish interoperability standards that allow existing USDA-compliant, IRS-compliant, and state-agency-compliant recordkeeping systems to satisfy EPA requirements without duplication [Stakeholder Point 2].
*   **Unified Agricultural Framework:** Convene a multi-agency working group including USDA, IRS, and state departments of agriculture to develop a unified agricultural recordkeeping framework that satisfies all federal obligations through a single system [Stakeholder Point 2].
*   **Safe Harbor:** Publish a safe harbor provision confirming that farms maintaining records in any format that contains the required data elements will be deemed compliant, regardless of structural format [Stakeholder Point 2].
*   *Applicable Provisions:* Updated Documentation Standards, Revised Reporting Obligations.

**C. Revise Implementation Timelines to Accommodate Agricultural Cycles:**
*   **Phased Implementation:** Establish a phased implementation schedule that avoids overlap with primary agricultural seasons (spring planting: March–May; fall harvest: September–November) [Stakeholder Point 6, 7].
*   **Extended Compliance Window:** Create an agricultural sector exemption or extended compliance window of no less than 24 months from the final rule's effective date, with a farm-specific compliance date of March 1 following final rule publication to ensure transitions occur outside peak production periods [Stakeholder Point 6, 7].
*   **Stakeholder Working Group:** Convene a formal agricultural stakeholder working group—inclusive of AFBF and state Farm Bureau federations—to co-develop compliance guidance tailored to farm-scale operations before the rule takes effect [Stakeholder Point 6].
*   *Applicable Provisions:* New Implementation Timelines, Operational Impact Analysis.

**D. Enhance Cost Analysis and Provide Technical Assistance:**
*   **Agriculture-Specific RIA:** Publish a revised, agriculture-specific Regulatory Impact Analysis (RIA) that separately models compliance costs for small farms (under $1M gross revenue), mid-sized farms ($1M–$5M), and large operations, rather than relying on industry-wide averages [Stakeholder Point 4, 5, 8].
*   **Rural Impact Analysis:** Conduct and publish a formal rural impact analysis under the Rural Development Act before finalizing any compliance cost requirements, specifically modeling the farm consolidation risk associated with uniform compliance cost burdens [Stakeholder Point 3].
*   **Compliance Assistance Fund:** Establish a dedicated small agricultural entity compliance assistance fund—analogous to Small Business Administration technical assistance programs—to offset first-year transition costs [Stakeholder Point 3, 5, 8].
*   **Rural Broadband Assessment:** Coordinate with USDA's Rural Development programs to assess rural broadband infrastructure gaps before mandating digital-only submission systems [Stakeholder Point 4].
*   **Cumulative Burden Analysis:** Conduct a cumulative burden analysis that accounts for all pending EPA agricultural reporting expansions—including PFAS TRI additions and potential EPCRA animal waste reinstatement—to assess the aggregate cost impact rather than evaluating each rule in isolation [Stakeholder Point 4].
*   **Simplified Cost Template:** Provide a standardized, simplified cost-reporting template specifically designed for agricultural operations with fewer than 500 acres or under $1 million in gross annual revenue [Stakeholder Point 1].
*   **Extend Comment Period:** Extend the comment period by at least 60 days to allow land-grant university extension services and AFBF state affiliates to assist member farmers in preparing substantive cost data submissions [Stakeholder Point 1].
*   *Applicable Provisions:* Cost Assessment Requirement, Operational Impact Analysis.

[CONCLUSION]
AFBF opposes the proposed regulatory framework as currently written because its uniform requirements and timelines will impose disproportionate and unsustainable burdens on agricultural operations, threatening the economic viability of family farms and, by extension, the nation's food security. EPA should not treat the absence of small-farm cost data as evidence that costs are low—it is evidence that the data-collection mechanism itself is inaccessible [Stakeholder Point 1].

We strongly urge EPA to adopt tailored, flexible approaches for agricultural entities, including exemptions for small farms, interoperable documentation standards, revised implementation timelines, and robust technical assistance programs. AFBF stands ready to engage with EPA to develop a compliance framework that achieves the agency's modernization goals without undermining the critical role of American agriculture in feeding the world.

### References
[3] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2023-03/fy-2024-epa-bib.pdf
[5] https://www.agriland.ie/farming-news/epa-agriculture-ghg-emissions-down-1-7-in-2024/
[6] https://www.regulations.gov/document/EPA-HQ-OAR-2023-0614-0001
[7] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2023-07/ria_aerr_proposal_2023-07.pdf

comment_text:
{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
Dr. Beverly Wright
[Contact Information Redacted for Privacy]
November 11, 2025

Office of Air and Radiation (OAR)
Environmental Protection Agency
[Address Redacted]

Re: Comments on Proposed Rule, EPA-HQ-OAR-2025-0194, Modernization of Reporting Obligations and Standardization of Documentation

Dear Administrator Regan,

As an environmental justice scholar and founder of the Deep South Center for Environmental Justice (DSCEJ) at Dillard University, I strongly support the Environmental Protection Agency's (EPA) proposed rule, EPA-HQ-OAR-2025-0194, aimed at modernizing reporting obligations and standardizing documentation. The DSCEJ has spent over three decades conducting community health impact assessments (HIAs) and advocating for equitable environmental protection in overburdened communities, particularly across the Gulf South. We understand firsthand the critical need for transparent, consistent, and accessible data to accurately document cumulative environmental burdens and inform effective regulatory decision-making.

While we commend the EPA's commitment to improving compliance and oversight mechanisms, we urge the agency to recognize that the rule as currently framed, without specific accommodations for Historically Black Colleges and Universities (HBCUs) and communities in active disaster recovery, risks exacerbating existing environmental inequities. Without targeted support, the proposed requirements, particularly the Cost Assessment Requirement and the Operational Impact Analysis, will place disproportionate and uncompensated burdens on under-resourced institutions and communities, potentially undermining the rule's very goals in the places that need it most. Our comments are grounded in environmental justice scholarship and informed by direct experience documenting the disproportionate impacts of pollution and climate change on Gulf South communities.

[BACKGROUND]
The proposed rule (EPA-HQ-OAR-2025-0194) seeks to overhaul how regulated entities interact with the EPA, focusing on modernizing reporting obligations, standardizing documentation, and establishing new implementation timelines. Key provisions include Revised Reporting Obligations, New Implementation Timelines, Updated Documentation Standards, a Cost Assessment Requirement, and an Operational Impact Analysis. The EPA's rationale is to address gaps in current practices, improve compliance monitoring, and assess regulatory effectiveness, while also mitigating economic burdens on regulated entities.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
The DSCEJ's research and advocacy demonstrate that these provisions, if implemented without equity-focused modifications, will have significant and disproportionate impacts on HBCUs and the environmental justice communities they serve, particularly in the Gulf South.

**Impact of Cost Assessment Requirement on HBCU Resource Limitations:**
As an environmental justice scholar whose research depends on HBCU institutional partnerships, I strongly support the EPA's proposed rule but urge the agency to recognize that the Cost Assessment Requirement—as currently framed—places a disproportionate and uncompensated data-collection burden on HBCUs, which are already operating under severe resource constraints that will prevent meaningful participation in the cost-reporting process without targeted agency support. My community health impact assessments are conducted in direct partnership with HBCU faculty, staff, and students who serve as co-investigators and community liaisons. These institutions lack the administrative infrastructure to absorb new compliance data-collection tasks. The Thurgood Marshall College Fund's 2024 survey found that HBCUs carry an average of $96 million in deferred maintenance costs per campus—19% higher than 2021 figures [1]—meaning that baseline institutional capacity is already stretched to a breaking point. When the EPA 'encourages' entities to provide cost data, it implicitly assumes that entities have the staff, time, and systems to conduct that analysis. HBCUs do not. My HBCU partners routinely redirect faculty time from research to administrative survival tasks, and adding an unfunded cost-assessment obligation risks either (a) excluding HBCU voices from the rulemaking record entirely, or (b) further straining the very institutions that anchor environmental justice research in frontline communities. The UNCF has reported that at least 20 HBCUs each had over $5 million in deferred maintenance costs in 2022 [2], meaning that any new compliance cost is additive to an already unsustainable baseline. My own research budget, constrained by HBCU resource limitations, cannot absorb the cost of conducting a formal cost-assessment analysis on behalf of my partner institutions—yet that data is precisely what the EPA needs to design an equitable implementation timeline. Without HBCU-specific accommodations, cost data will skew toward well-resourced entities, away from EJ communities (including HBCU campuses). HBCU campuses often located in overburdened communities. Disaster recovery competing priorities further limit capacity.

**Impact of Revised Reporting Obligations on Community Health Impact Assessments:**
As an environmental justice scholar whose research center conducts community health impact assessments in frontline communities, I strongly support the revised reporting obligations because standardized, timely data submission will directly improve the quality and reliability of the health impact assessments my team uses to document cumulative environmental burdens on communities of color and low-income populations. However, I urge the agency to ensure that the newly standardized data is made publicly accessible in formats that community-based researchers and HBCU institutions—not just well-resourced corporate compliance teams—can readily use. My center's community health impact assessments depend critically on the quality and completeness of data that regulated entities submit to EPA. Under current fragmented reporting practices, we routinely encounter gaps, inconsistencies, and delays in chemical exposure data that undermine the scientific defensibility of our HIAs. For example, the EPA's own use of Health Impact Assessments as a decision-support tool requires robust underlying data—data that the revised reporting obligations are designed to improve. As an HBCU-affiliated researcher with limited staff capacity and no dedicated data-cleaning infrastructure, I spend disproportionate time reconciling inconsistent submissions before I can even begin analysis. Standardized protocols would reduce this burden and allow my team to redirect resources toward community engagement and disaster recovery work, which competes heavily for our attention. Inconsistent and fragmented reporting by regulated entities creates significant gaps in our ability to conduct credible cumulative impact analyses in overburdened communities like those surrounding Cancer Alley in Louisiana. When regulated entities submit data under disparate, non-standardized formats, our team—operating under HBCU resource constraints without the large research budgets of R1 institutions—must expend disproportionate staff time cleaning, reconciling, and validating data before any substantive analysis can begin. Standardized reporting protocols under this rule would directly reduce that burden and allow our limited resources to be directed toward community-facing research and disaster recovery support obligations we carry in post-Katrina and post-Ida communities. The EPA's own use of Health Impact Assessments as a decision-support tool requires robust underlying data—data that the revised reporting obligations are designed to improve [3]. NEJAC recommended cumulative impact assessment frameworks in 2024 [4].

**Impact of Operational Impact Analysis on Disaster Recovery Competing Priorities:**
As an environmental justice scholar conducting community health impact assessments in disaster-affected regions, I strongly support the EPA's proposed Operational Impact Analysis requirement, but urge the agency to explicitly recognize that regulated facilities in disaster-impacted communities face compounding operational burdens that must be accounted for in implementation timelines — and that failure to do so will disproportionately harm the most vulnerable populations these facilities serve. My research institution, an HBCU with limited administrative resources, partners with communities still in active disaster recovery — including Gulf Coast communities that have faced repeated hurricane impacts. When regulated facilities in these areas are simultaneously managing disaster recovery operations and new EPA compliance transitions, their operational bandwidth is severely constrained. Our community health impact assessments depend on these facilities' cooperation and data-sharing capacity. If the new reporting and documentation modernization requirements are imposed on facilities already stretched thin by disaster recovery competing priorities, we risk losing critical environmental health data from the communities most at risk. The 2024 National Preparedness Report confirms that 'resilient infrastructure' and disaster response capacity are deeply intertwined, and that low-income communities bear disproportionate costs when systems fail [6, 7]. Facilities in disaster-impacted communities often lack the IT infrastructure, staffing continuity, and administrative capacity to absorb new documentation and reporting mandates simultaneously with disaster recovery operations. Our HBCU research center faces analogous resource constraints: we operate with limited grant funding, faculty who carry dual obligations to community partners, and institutional infrastructure that cannot easily absorb new compliance-driven data demands. The UNDRR 2024 Annual Report notes that 'flexible core funding remains essential' for organizations to 'adapt to rapidly changing disaster risk landscapes' — a principle the EPA should apply to its own regulated community [6, 7].

**Impact of Updated Documentation Standards on Community Partnership Obligations:**
As an environmental justice scholar at an HBCU conducting community health impact assessments in frontline communities, I strongly support the EPA's updated documentation standards because standardized formats will, for the first time, create a level playing field that allows community-based research like ours to be directly comparable to and integrated with data submitted by well-resourced regulated entities — but I urge the agency to pair these standards with dedicated technical assistance and phased implementation timelines specifically for under-resourced academic and community partners, or risk inadvertently excluding the very voices most critical to environmental justice outcomes. Our center conducts community health impact assessments in partnership with frontline communities in the Gulf South, many of which are still recovering from compounding disaster events. We operate under significant HBCU resource constraints — limited administrative staff, restricted IT infrastructure, and grant funding cycles that do not align with compliance transition timelines. When our community partners are required to adopt new standardized documentation formats simultaneously with large industrial entities, the operational burden falls disproportionately on us. Our staff hours spent reformatting existing community health data into new EPA-required templates are hours diverted from active fieldwork and community engagement obligations. This is not a theoretical concern: EPA's own FY 2023-2024 OECA National Program Guidance acknowledges the importance of 'community engagement in the development of cleanup and reuse agreements to ensure community concerns are addressed in a meaningful manner' [8] — yet compliance burdens that overwhelm community-based researchers undermine exactly that goal.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To ensure the proposed rule achieves its environmental justice goals and does not inadvertently create new barriers for under-resourced institutions and communities, we recommend the following specific changes:

**For the Cost Assessment Requirement:**
1.  **HBCU/MSI Distinct Reporting Category:** The EPA should explicitly designate HBCUs and Minority Serving Institutions as a distinct reporting category with a streamlined, abbreviated cost-assessment template that requires fewer staff hours to complete.
2.  **Targeted Technical Assistance Grants:** The agency should provide direct technical assistance grants or assign EPA regional staff to assist HBCU-affiliated entities in compiling cost data.
3.  **Contextual Weighting of HBCU Data:** The EPA should weight HBCU-submitted cost data with appropriate context in the final regulatory record, acknowledging that resource-constrained institutions may underreport costs due to capacity limitations rather than low actual impact. The agency should also clarify in the final rule preamble that 'encouraged' cost submissions from HBCUs will be treated as substantively equivalent to submissions from better-resourced entities.
4.  **Dedicated HBCU Cost Assessment Assistance Program:** The EPA should establish a dedicated HBCU Cost Assessment Assistance Program as part of this rulemaking, providing small grants (suggested range: $15,000–$50,000 per institution) to enable HBCUs to hire temporary staff or consultants to compile the cost data the agency is requesting. This is consistent with the agency's existing HBCU and MSI Advisory Council framework and with federal HBCU support programs. The cost of this assistance program would be modest relative to the value of having accurate, representative cost data from environmental justice-adjacent institutions in the final regulatory record.
5.  **Tiered Cost-Reporting Framework:** In response to the agency's specific question about alternatives to the proposed compliance requirements, we recommend the EPA adopt a tiered cost-reporting framework: (1) a full cost-assessment submission for large regulated entities with dedicated compliance staff; (2) a simplified qualitative impact statement for HBCUs and MSIs, allowing narrative description of expected costs without requiring formal cost-accounting analysis; and (3) an extended submission window of at least 180 days for HBCU-affiliated entities to account for competing institutional priorities including disaster recovery. The agency should also commit to publishing a disaggregated analysis of cost data by institution type in the final rule, so that HBCU cost experiences are visible in the regulatory record rather than averaged into aggregate figures.

**For the Revised Reporting Obligations:**
1.  **Public Accessibility of Standardized Data:** Mandate that all standardized data submissions be made publicly available through a searchable, machine-readable portal—similar to the 2024 Chemical Data Reporting release—within 90 days of submission deadlines.
2.  **Technical Assistance for Data Access:** Create a dedicated technical assistance program for HBCU and community-based research institutions to access and interpret the new data formats.
3.  **Census-Tract Level Geographic Identifiers:** Explicitly require that submitted data include geographic identifiers at the census-tract level so that community health impact assessments can link exposure data to Environmental Justice Index metrics, such as those updated in the 2024 EJI, which now integrates climate burden, environmental hazards, and population health indicators.
4.  **Alignment with Cumulative Impact Assessment:** The agency should explicitly align the new reporting protocols with cumulative impact assessment frameworks, as recommended by NEJAC in its November 2024 recommendations to EPA [4]. Specifically, the revised data submission standards should require regulated entities to report co-pollutant data, facility proximity to sensitive receptors, and community vulnerability indicators in a machine-readable, publicly accessible format.

**For the Operational Impact Analysis:**
1.  **Disaster Recovery Status Exemption/Extension:** Build a formal 'disaster recovery status' exemption or timeline extension mechanism into the Operational Impact Analysis framework, allowing regulated facilities in federally declared disaster zones to receive phased compliance timelines of no less than 24 additional months.
2.  **Assessment of Disproportionate Burden:** Require that the Operational Impact Analysis explicitly assess whether proposed compliance burdens fall disproportionately on facilities serving environmental justice communities in active recovery.
3.  **Technical Assistance for Disaster-Impacted Facilities:** Create a dedicated technical assistance program — accessible to community-based researchers and HBCU partners — to help facilities in disaster-impacted areas meet new documentation standards without diverting resources from recovery operations.
4.  **Disaggregated Cost Analysis for Disaster Zones:** In response to the agency's question on expected compliance costs, we recommend the EPA: (1) conduct a disaggregated cost analysis that separately estimates compliance costs for facilities in active or recent (within 5 years) federally declared disaster zones, rather than relying on aggregate industry-wide cost estimates that mask this variation; (2) require that the Operational Impact Analysis include a specific sub-analysis of facilities serving environmental justice communities, consistent with Executive Order 14096 on environmental justice; and (3) consider subsidized compliance assistance grants for small and under-resourced regulated facilities in disaster-impacted areas, modeled on the flexible funding mechanisms recommended by UNDRR for disaster-resilient organizations.

**For the Updated Documentation Standards:**
1.  **Tiered Implementation Timeline:** Establish a tiered implementation timeline that grants academic institutions and community-based organizations at least 24 additional months beyond the standard compliance deadline to transition to new documentation formats.
2.  **Dedicated EPA Technical Assistance Program:** Create a dedicated EPA technical assistance program — modeled on existing P2 documentation support described in the FY 2023-2024 OCSPP National Program Guidance — that provides free template tools, training webinars, and one-on-one support specifically for HBCU researchers and community partners.
3.  **Exemption/Simplification for Non-Regulated Entities:** Explicitly exempt or simplify documentation requirements for non-regulated entities participating voluntarily in EPA partnership programs, so that the burden of standardization falls on regulated polluters rather than on the community health researchers documenting their impacts.

[CONCLUSION]
The modernization of reporting obligations and standardization of documentation proposed by the EPA is a critical step towards improving environmental oversight and ensuring that communities have access to the data needed to understand and address environmental health disparities. However, the rule's effectiveness in achieving environmental justice outcomes hinges on its equitable implementation. Without explicit accommodations, targeted technical assistance, and phased timelines for under-resourced institutions like HBCUs and facilities in disaster-affected environmental justice communities, the proposed requirements risk deepening existing inequities and undermining the rule's very purpose.

We urge the EPA to adopt these recommendations to ensure that the final rule promotes transparency, accountability, and, most importantly, equitable protection for all communities.

Sincerely,

Dr. Beverly Wright
Environmental Justice Scholar
Founding Director, Deep South Center for Environmental Justice at Dillard University
[Signature Redacted]

### References
[1] https://www.ache.edu/wp-content/Finance/HBCU/HBCUDeferredMain__FinalReport_Oct2024.pdf
[2] https://www.laregents.edu/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/2024-HBCU-Report.pdf
[3] https://www.pfasinsights.com/2025/11/practical-changes-to-epas-pfas-reporting-requirements/
[4] https://www.kslaw.com/attachments/000/011/447/original/Environmental_Justice_A_2023_Recap_And_2024_Forecast_%28Law360%29.pdf
[6] https://cldigital.com/blog/best-practices-for-disaster-recovery-planning-in-2024/
[7] https://trilio.io/resources/disaster-recovery-plan-checklist/
[8] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2022-08/fy-2023-2024-oeca-npg.pdf

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The National Mining Association (NMA) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA) proposed new regulatory framework, EPA-HQ-OAR-2025-0194, which seeks to modernize reporting obligations, standardize documentation, establish new implementation timelines, and require cost assessments and operational impact analyses from regulated entities. The NMA is the national trade organization representing the interests of the U.S. mining industry, whose members produce the critical minerals, metals, and energy resources essential to American manufacturing, infrastructure, energy independence, and national security. Our membership spans the full spectrum of mining operations, including producers of coal, copper, gold, iron ore, lithium, cobalt, nickel, rare earth elements, and other vital commodities.

While the proposed framework does not directly impose new emission limits, the associated administrative and compliance burdens will have a similar chilling effect on domestic mining investment and operations, thereby indirectly functioning as de facto regulatory barriers that threaten mineral supply and energy independence. As detailed below, the NMA strongly opposes the proposed requirements as structured, arguing that their implementation will impose disproportionate and unworkable costs on the mining sector, undermine U.S. critical mineral supply chain security, and conflict with broader federal policy goals of accelerating domestic mineral production.

[BACKGROUND]
The NMA's comments specifically address the following key provisions of EPA-HQ-OAR-2025-0194:
*   **Revised Reporting Obligations:** New protocols for submitting data to the agency.
*   **New Implementation Timelines:** Specific windows for when entities must come into compliance with the new rules.
*   **Updated Documentation Standards:** Entities are required to maintain records according to new, standardized formats.
*   **Cost Assessment Requirement:** Entities are encouraged to provide data on "expected costs" associated with the transition to these new standards.
*   **Operational Impact Analysis:** A review of how these changes will affect the day-to-day "operational impacts" of regulated facilities.

Our analysis focuses on how these administrative and compliance requirements, despite not being direct emission limits, will function as significant regulatory hurdles that deter domestic mine development, increase operational costs, and ultimately threaten the reliability of U.S. mineral supply chains and energy independence.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
**1. Operational Impact Analysis (OIA) as an Unintended Threat to U.S. Critical Mineral Supply Chain Security**

The NMA fundamentally opposes the proposed Operational Impact Analysis framework as an unintended threat to U.S. critical mineral supply chain security. We argue that the compliance burden associated with this new reporting requirement will deter domestic mine development and processing investment at precisely the moment when federal policy—including EPA's own interagency commitments—calls for accelerating domestic critical mineral production.

NMA coordinates policy on behalf of producers of lithium, cobalt, nickel, copper, rare earth elements, and other minerals identified as critical to the energy transition and national security. The International Energy Agency's Global Critical Minerals Outlook 2024 documents that global supply of these minerals is already dangerously concentrated among a small number of foreign producers, with China dominating processing capacity for multiple key minerals. Analysis by the Roosevelt Project further confirms that the top three producers of nickel and lithium control over 60% of global supply. Against this backdrop, any new domestic regulatory burden that increases the cost or complexity of U.S. mine permitting, operation, or supply chain coordination directly undermines the federal government's stated goal of diversifying critical mineral supply chains.

EPA's own interagency work through the American Battery Materials Initiative and the Critical Minerals Permitting Interagency Working Group reflects a whole-of-government commitment to reducing barriers to domestic mineral production [1]. These initiatives, chaired by the White House and Department of Energy, with representatives from federal agencies including EPA, are explicitly designed to streamline permitting and accelerate domestic production of critical minerals. The proposed OIA requirements, by adding new operational friction and administrative overhead, directly contradict these established interagency priorities.

Mining operations—particularly coal and hardrock mines in remote locations—do not operate on the continuous, facility-based production rhythms that EPA's proposed reporting modernization appears to assume. Mine planning cycles span decades, production rates fluctuate seasonally and with commodity markets, and supply chain coordination for critical minerals involves multi-party logistics across federal, state, and private lands. Requiring standardized day-to-day operational impact documentation imposes a continuous administrative overhead that our member operations are structurally ill-equipped to absorb. A single mid-sized hardrock mine may coordinate ore shipments, processing contracts, and reclamation bond drawdowns simultaneously—each with distinct regulatory touchpoints. Layering a new EPA operational impact reporting regime atop existing Mine Safety and Health Administration (MSHA), Bureau of Land Management (BLM), and state reclamation reporting obligations creates duplicative, conflicting documentation demands that will divert skilled compliance personnel from actual environmental stewardship activities.

Furthermore, mining operations in remote operational locations face unique challenges in deploying and maintaining the infrastructure required for continuous, real-time data collection and reporting. Many mines operate in areas with limited internet connectivity, requiring significant capital investment in satellite communications or dedicated data transmission lines. The cost of transitioning to new reporting protocols and documentation standards, including the acquisition of new software, hardware, and specialized personnel, will be substantial. These costs, while not direct emission control expenses, will nevertheless increase the overall regulatory burden on mining operations, making domestic production less competitive globally and potentially leading to a decrease in domestic supply.

**2. New Implementation Timelines as a Barrier to Innovation and Operational Stability**

The National Mining Association opposes the proposed implementation timelines as structurally incompatible with the long-term planning cycles inherent to coal and hardrock mining operations, which routinely span 10–30 years from permitting through reclamation. Imposing compressed, fixed compliance windows on an industry whose operational commitments are made decades in advance creates a fundamental mismatch that will impose severe and disproportionate costs on mining operators without commensurate environmental benefit.

Mining operations are not analogous to manufacturing facilities or point-source emitters that can retool reporting systems on an annual or biennial basis. A mine's air emissions reporting infrastructure—including monitoring equipment placement, data management systems, and staffing allocations—is integrated into mine plans that are approved by multiple federal and state agencies, bonded against reclamation obligations, and locked into capital expenditure schedules that cannot be revised on short notice. For example, a surface coal mine operating under a multi-year permit may have already committed capital to emissions monitoring systems calibrated to existing Air Emissions Reporting Requirements (AERR) standards. Requiring transition to new documentation and reporting frameworks within a compressed window forces operators to either absorb unplanned capital costs mid-cycle or risk non-compliance penalties. The EPA's own docket (EPA-HQ-OAR-2024-0333) acknowledges that even vehicle inspection and maintenance programs—far simpler operational contexts—require up to 4 years for implementation. Mining operations are substantially more complex, yet the proposed timelines appear to offer no equivalent accommodation.

The EPA's Office of Air and Radiation (OAR) Final FY 2025-2026 National Program Guidance references implementing revisions to the Air Emissions Reporting Requirements (AERR) for point source emissions submissions for the 2023 and 2024 emissions years [3]. This indicates an ongoing evolution of reporting requirements, further underscoring the need for flexible implementation timelines that align with the long-term operational planning inherent to mining. The recent extension of the 2024 reporting period for manufacturers from January 31 to March 31, 2024, to allow for the use of a new electronic reporting tool, demonstrates EPA's recognition that transitions to new reporting mechanisms require adequate time [4]. Mining operations, with their integrated and complex reporting infrastructure, require even greater accommodation.

**3. Cost Assessment Requirement and Conflict with Reclamation Bond Requirements**

The National Mining Association opposes the proposed implementation timeline embedded in the Cost Assessment Requirement because it fails to account for the long-term, legally binding nature of reclamation bond commitments, creating an unworkable compliance window that could force mining operators to submit cost transition data before they can legally or financially restructure their bonding arrangements.

Unlike most regulated industries, mining operators are legally obligated under state and federal surface mining laws to maintain active reclamation bonds for the full duration of mine operations and post-closure reclamation periods. Bond release is contingent on the successful completion of all reclamation activities and cannot occur before statutory responsibility periods expire. This means that a mining operator cannot simply 'transition' its bonding documentation to comply with a new EPA reporting framework on EPA's preferred schedule — the operator's legal obligations under existing bond instruments run on a separate, state-law-governed timeline. Imposing a federal EPA transition deadline that conflicts with these pre-existing legal commitments exposes our members to simultaneous non-compliance risk under both EPA's new framework and their existing bond agreements.

The costs associated with complying with the new reporting and documentation standards will also have a direct impact on reclamation bond requirements. As the cost of operations increases due to administrative burdens, the potential cost of reclamation could also be perceived as increasing, potentially leading to higher bonding requirements. This creates a vicious cycle where EPA's reporting requirements inadvertently inflate the financial assurances needed for reclamation, further increasing the cost of mining and deterring investment. The EPA's own FY 2024 Justification of Appropriation Estimates for the Office of Air and Radiation highlights the substantial resources dedicated to regulatory development and implementation [6], but fails to adequately account for the ripple effects of these regulations on other critical aspects of mining operations, such as reclamation bonding.

**4. Revised Reporting Obligations and Updated Documentation Standards as Operational Burdens**

The National Mining Association opposes the proposed Revised Reporting Obligations and Updated Documentation Standards as structured, because the standardized day-to-day reporting framework fails to account for the inherently episodic, long-cycle nature of mining operations and will impose disproportionate compliance burdens on energy and mineral supply chain coordination without commensurate environmental benefit.

As previously noted, mining operations do not operate on continuous production cycles. Mine planning cycles span decades, production rates fluctuate, and supply chain coordination for critical minerals involves multi-party logistics. Requiring standardized day-to-day operational impact documentation imposes a continuous administrative overhead that our member operations are structurally ill-equipped to absorb. Layering a new EPA operational impact reporting regime atop existing Mine Safety and Health Administration (MSHA), Bureau of Land Management (BLM), and state reclamation reporting obligations creates duplicative, conflicting documentation demands that will divert skilled compliance personnel from actual environmental stewardship activities.

The EPA releases preliminary Toxic Release Inventory (TRI) data annually, demonstrating the volume of reporting already required from facilities, including mines [2]. Adding new, standardized documentation requirements will increase the administrative burden without necessarily improving environmental outcomes. Instead, it will divert resources that could otherwise be used for environmental improvements or investments in new mining technologies. The increase in compliance costs will also disproportionately affect smaller mining operations, potentially leading to mine closures and a further concentration of mineral production in foreign countries.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
In direct response to EPA's request for feedback on costs and operational impacts, the NMA urges the agency to implement the following specific changes to the proposed framework:

1.  **Critical Mineral Supply Chain Impact Assessment and Coordination:**
    *   **Recommendation:** EPA should conduct a formal Critical Mineral Supply Chain Impact Assessment before finalizing any operational reporting requirements that apply to mines producing minerals on the DOE/USGS critical minerals list.
    *   **Justification:** This assessment would directly address the "Operational Impact Analysis" provision by ensuring that new reporting burdens do not inadvertently undermine U.S. critical mineral supply chain security, consistent with the identified operational_burden impact.
    *   **Specific Changes:** EPA should explicitly coordinate with the White House-chaired American Battery Materials Initiative and the Critical Minerals Permitting Interagency Working Group to ensure the proposed rule does not conflict with interagency permitting streamlining efforts [1]. Additionally, EPA should consider a categorical exclusion or streamlined compliance pathway for operations whose primary output is a federally designated critical mineral, consistent with the administration's supply chain security objectives.

2.  **Tiered Implementation Timelines:**
    *   **Recommendation:** The agency should establish a tiered implementation timeline that explicitly accounts for the long-term planning cycles of extractive industries, in response to the "New Implementation Timelines" provision.
    *   **Justification:** This addresses the identified innovation_barrier impact by providing a realistic timeframe for compliance that aligns with the multi-decade planning horizons of mining operations, contrasting with EPA's own timelines for simpler programs [3].
    *   **Specific Changes:** NMA recommends: (1) a minimum 5-year phase-in period for existing permitted operations, measured from the date of final rule publication; (2) a grandfather provision allowing mines with approved mine plans submitted prior to the rule's effective date to comply under existing AERR standards until their next scheduled permit renewal; and (3) a formal consultation mechanism requiring EPA to coordinate with the Office of Surface Mining Reclamation and Enforcement (OSMRE) and relevant state mining agencies before imposing compliance deadlines that intersect with existing permit conditions.

3.  **Extended Compliance Schedules for Reclamation Bond-Bound Entities:**
    *   **Recommendation:** EPA should explicitly exempt or provide extended compliance schedules for regulated entities whose transition timelines are constrained by pre-existing, legally binding financial instruments such as reclamation bonds, specifically addressing the "Cost Assessment Requirement" provision.
    *   **Justification:** This directly responds to the identified compliance_cost impact by preventing conflicts between federal reporting deadlines and state-mandated reclamation bond review cycles, which are driven by distinct legal and financial obligations.
    *   **Specific Changes:** The agency should: (1) allow mining operators to align Cost Assessment Requirement compliance with their next scheduled bond review or renewal date rather than a fixed federal deadline; (2) establish a formal coordination mechanism with OSMRE and relevant state regulatory authorities to harmonize federal reporting timelines with existing bond release schedules; and (3) provide a safe harbor from enforcement for operators who demonstrate good-faith compliance efforts constrained by pre-existing bond instrument terms.

4.  **Tailored Reporting Framework for Mining Operations:**
    *   **Recommendation:** EPA should exempt or create a tailored compliance pathway for extractive industries with long-term mine planning cycles, recognizing that 'day-to-day operational impacts' are not a meaningful unit of analysis for operations planned in 5-to-20-year increments, thereby addressing the "Operational Impact Analysis," "Revised Reporting Obligations," and "Updated Documentation Standards" provisions.
    *   **Justification:** This addresses the identified operational_burden and compliance_cost impacts by avoiding duplicative reporting and administrative overhead that is not aligned with the episodic nature of mining operations and existing MSHA, BLM, and state reporting requirements [2].
    *   **Specific Changes:** EPA should (1) conduct a formal inter-agency coordination review with MSHA, BLM, and DOE before finalizing any new reporting standardization to eliminate duplicative requirements; and (2) extend the proposed implementation timeline by no fewer than 36 months for mining sector entities to allow integration with existing reclamation bond reporting cycles and mine plan amendment processes.

[CONCLUSION]
The National Mining Association strongly urges EPA to reconsider the proposed modernization of reporting obligations, standardization of documentation, new implementation timelines, and cost/operational impact analysis requirements as currently structured. While these provisions do not directly impose new emission limits, their cumulative administrative and compliance burdens will function as de facto regulatory barriers, increasing costs, deterring investment, and slowing down domestic mineral production. This will directly undermine U.S. critical mineral supply chain security and energy independence, conflicting with broader federal policy objectives.

By incorporating the NMA's recommendations for tiered implementation timelines, tailored reporting frameworks, and explicit coordination with other federal and state agencies, EPA can develop a final rule that achieves its goals of improved oversight without imposing unworkable burdens on the mining industry. A balanced approach is essential to ensure that America's mining sector can continue to provide the vital minerals and energy resources needed for economic prosperity, national security, and a sustainable future.

[4] https://www.lawbc.com/recent-federal-developments-for-march-2024/
[6] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2023-04/fy24-cj-00-overview.pdf
