{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
Chevron Corporation ("Chevron") appreciates the opportunity to provide comments to the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) on the proposed regulatory framework to update compliance and oversight mechanisms. As a global, integrated energy company with significant operations in upstream oil and gas production, petroleum refining, and emerging renewable fuels and carbon capture technologies, Chevron is a highly regulated entity. We are committed to complying with all applicable environmental regulations and collaborating with EPA to achieve shared environmental goals. However, we have serious concerns that the proposed rule, particularly its implementation timelines, revised reporting obligations, and documentation standards, will impose disproportionate compliance costs, hinder energy reliability and affordability, and create barriers to innovation in decarbonization technologies, without commensurate environmental benefit.

[BACKGROUND]
EPA's proposal introduces a comprehensive overhaul of how regulated entities interact with the agency, focusing on the modernization of reporting obligations and the standardization of documentation. Key provisions relevant to Chevron include revised reporting obligations, new implementation timelines, updated documentation standards, a cost assessment requirement, and an operational impact analysis. While we support the agency's objective of improving data quality and oversight, we strongly urge EPA to revise the proposed rule to ensure it is practical, cost-effective, and aligned with industry operational realities.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]

**Challenge to Cost-Benefit Analysis and Disproportionate Compliance Costs for Large Integrated Operators**
Chevron opposes the proposed revised reporting obligations without a rigorous, publicly available cost-benefit analysis that separately quantifies compliance costs for large upstream operators. EPA's current economic analysis appears to undercount the full system integration, personnel training, and third-party verification costs that Chevron and similarly situated companies will incur. As a large corporation with upstream oil and gas production operations subject to multiple concurrent EPA rulemakings—including the 2026 NSPS/EG Oil and Gas rule and the Subpart W Greenhouse Gas Reporting Rule—Chevron faces a compounding compliance cost burden that no single rulemaking's economic analysis fully captures. The proposed new data submission protocols will require: (1) enterprise IT system modifications to accommodate new data submission formats, estimated internally at significant capital expenditure given the scope of our asset base; (2) retraining of environmental compliance personnel across multiple business units and geographic regions; (3) potential engagement of third-party data verification services if the new protocols require independent certification; and (4) legal and regulatory affairs resources to interpret and implement new documentation standards. EPA's own guidelines for preparing economic analyses, as required under OMB Circular A-4 (2023), mandate that agencies analyze at least one alternative that costs less than the proposed option; Chevron has not identified such an alternative in the proposal [2], [3]. The absence of a transparent, disaggregated cost analysis for large upstream operators specifically—as distinct from small businesses—means the agency cannot accurately assess whether the compliance cost burden is proportionate to the environmental monitoring benefits claimed.

Furthermore, Chevron opposes the proposed Cost Assessment Requirement as currently structured because it fails to account for the multi-decade capital planning horizons inherent to upstream oil and gas production and petroleum refining, rendering any 'expected cost' submission based on near-term compliance windows fundamentally misleading and analytically incomplete. Chevron's capital investment decisions for refinery infrastructure, upstream production facilities, and carbon capture projects are planned on 10-to-30-year cycles, with refinery turnaround schedules locked in years in advance. The proposed framework's implicit assumption that compliance costs can be meaningfully estimated within a standard regulatory analysis window (e.g., 2024–2038 as used in comparable EPA RIAs) does not capture the full lifecycle cost burden Chevron faces. When EPA proposes new documentation and reporting standards mid-cycle, Chevron must either absorb unplanned capital expenditures to retrofit existing systems, accelerate turnaround schedules at significant operational cost, or delay compliance—none of which are adequately reflected in a simple 'expected cost' data submission [4], [5]. The NBER research on regulatory compliance costs confirms that chemical manufacturing sectors already bear regulatory compliance expenses averaging 2.3% of labor spending, and for a company of Chevron's scale, even marginal increases in compliance overhead compound into hundreds of millions of dollars in unbudgeted expenditure.

**Impacts on Energy Reliability and Affordability from Rigid Implementation Timelines**
Chevron Corporation opposes the proposed implementation timelines as written because they fail to account for the fixed, multi-year scheduling cycles that govern refinery turnarounds, making compliance within the proposed windows operationally impossible without forcing unplanned shutdowns or unsafe modifications to live process units. Chevron operates large-scale petroleum refining facilities where major compliance-related equipment modifications—such as emissions control system upgrades, monitoring instrumentation installation, and documentation infrastructure changes—can only be safely executed during planned turnaround events. These turnarounds are scheduled 4–6 years in advance based on equipment inspection intervals, contractor availability, and regulatory permit windows. The proposed compliance windows do not align with Chevron's existing turnaround schedules at key facilities, meaning compliance would require either (a) forcing modifications during live operations, creating significant safety and process integrity risks, or (b) conducting unplanned partial shutdowns at an estimated cost of tens of millions of dollars per facility in lost throughput and emergency contractor mobilization. Such unplanned disruptions directly undermine energy reliability by reducing fuel supply and increase affordability concerns by driving up operational costs that are ultimately borne by consumers. EPA's own recent precedent—extending oil and gas compliance deadlines in both July and December 2025 specifically because 'more realistic timelines for owners and operators' were needed—demonstrates that the agency has recognized this exact problem in analogous rulemakings [1].

Furthermore, Chevron opposes the one-size-fits-all compliance window structure and urges EPA to consider the unintended consequence that rigid timelines will incentivize refiners to defer or compress turnaround maintenance activities in ways that increase safety and environmental risk—the opposite of the agency's stated regulatory objectives. Forcing compliance modifications into compressed, agency-dictated windows creates perverse incentives that undermine both safety and environmental performance. Refinery turnarounds are carefully sequenced events where inspection, maintenance, repair, and upgrade activities are integrated into a single planned outage to minimize the number of times process units are taken offline. When an external compliance deadline forces a facility to either (a) add compliance-related scope to an already-planned turnaround on an accelerated basis, or (b) conduct a separate compliance-only outage, the result is either a compressed turnaround with reduced time for critical safety inspections or an additional unplanned outage that itself carries process safety risks during startup and shutdown transitions. These operational disruptions affect energy reliability and can increase costs.

**Barriers to Innovation and the Need for Technology-Neutral Standards**
Chevron opposes the proposed Operational Impact Analysis framework because, as applied to carbon capture investments, it creates a structural disincentive to voluntary emissions reduction projects by subjecting carbon capture facility operations to the same compliance review burden as conventional emission-generating activities — an unintended consequence that EPA should correct by establishing a differentiated review pathway for carbon management technologies. Chevron has made capital commitments to carbon capture and sequestration projects, including facilities that qualify for the 45Q tax credit. These projects involve novel operational configurations — CO2 compression, pipeline injection, geological storage monitoring — that will inevitably generate 'operational impact' documentation requirements under the proposed framework. However, unlike conventional refinery operations, carbon capture facility operations are inherently emissions-reducing. Subjecting them to the same operational review burden as emission-generating processes imposes compliance costs on activities that advance EPA's own environmental objectives [6], [7]. If compliance costs for carbon capture operations are treated identically to those for conventional operations, the net economics of 45Q-eligible projects deteriorate, potentially causing projects to fall below investment thresholds, thereby hindering innovation and decarbonization efforts.

Finally, Chevron Corporation opposes the proposed Updated Documentation Standards as written because the mandated standardized record formats are incompatible with Chevron's existing global data management architecture, imposing substantial transition costs without commensurate environmental benefit and threatening operational consistency across our international upstream, refining, and renewable fuels operations. Chevron operates across dozens of jurisdictions with integrated data systems that have been built over decades to satisfy multiple overlapping regulatory regimes—including international standards, SEC disclosure requirements, and existing EPA reporting frameworks. The proposed uniform documentation formats would require Chevron to either (1) maintain entirely parallel record-keeping systems for U.S. EPA compliance versus all other regulatory and operational purposes, or (2) undertake a comprehensive, enterprise-wide IT overhaul estimated to affect thousands of data fields across upstream production, refinery operations, and renewable fuels tracking [8], [9]. Industry benchmarks indicate that large organizations spend approximately $12,800 per employee annually on compliance infrastructure; for a company of Chevron's scale, a forced format migration of this scope could represent hundreds of millions of dollars in one-time transition costs alone, on top of ongoing maintenance burdens. This directly undermines our global operational consistency requirements, which depend on unified data standards to enable real-time monitoring, cross-facility benchmarking, and consolidated environmental performance reporting. We urge EPA to adopt technology-neutral standards that allow for functional equivalence rather than prescriptive formats.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]

Based on the analysis above, Chevron respectfully submits the following recommendations to EPA:

1.  **Revise Cost Assessment and Conduct Disaggregated Cost-Benefit Analysis:**
    *   **Provision Addressed:** Revised Reporting Obligations, Cost Assessment Requirement.
    *   **Justification:** The current approach undercounts compliance costs for large integrated operators and fails to account for multi-decade capital planning.
    *   **Specific Change:** Publish a disaggregated compliance cost analysis that separately estimates costs for large integrated upstream operators, mid-size independents, and small producers. Explicitly analyze at least one lower-cost alternative, such as incremental modification of existing Subpart W submission formats rather than wholesale protocol replacement, consistent with OMB Circular A-4 (2023) requirements [2], [3]. Revise the Cost Assessment Requirement to explicitly request that regulated entities submit cost data across multiple time horizons—specifically short-term (0–5 years), medium-term (5–15 years), and long-term (15–30 years)—aligned with actual industry capital planning cycles rather than a single compliance window [4], [5].

2.  **Establish Turnaround-Aligned and Tiered Implementation Timelines to Protect Energy Reliability:**
    *   **Provision Addressed:** New Implementation Timelines.
    *   **Justification:** Rigid timelines are incompatible with fixed refinery turnaround schedules, leading to operational impossibility, safety risks, and lost throughput which impacts energy reliability and affordability.
    *   **Specific Change:** Replace the fixed compliance windows with a facility-specific turnaround-aligned compliance pathway. Specifically, allow regulated entities to submit a Turnaround Compliance Integration Plan (TCIP) within 180 days of the rule's effective date, documenting their next scheduled turnaround date and demonstrating that all required modifications will be completed at that event. Compliance deadlines should then be set at the later of the proposed window or the entity's next scheduled turnaround, not to exceed 6 years from the rule's effective date. As an alternative, adopt a tiered compliance framework that establishes a 'standard pathway' allowing facilities to comply at their next scheduled turnaround within a defined outer bound (e.g., 6 years), and an 'accelerated pathway' with interim documentation for facilities whose next turnaround falls beyond the outer bound [1].

3.  **Create Differentiated Review Pathway for Carbon Capture and Renewable Fuels Facilities to Foster Innovation:**
    *   **Provision Addressed:** Operational Impact Analysis.
    *   **Justification:** The current framework disincentivizes voluntary emissions reduction projects by subjecting them to the same compliance burden as conventional emission-generating activities, creating barriers to innovation and decarbonization [6], [7].
    *   **Specific Change:** Create a separate, streamlined Operational Impact Analysis pathway for facilities whose primary operational purpose is carbon capture, utilization, or storage, or renewable fuels production. This pathway should: (1) presumptively classify operational changes at certified facilities as 'beneficial operational impacts' not subject to standard review timelines; (2) require only an annual attestation rather than a project-by-project review for operational changes within an approved carbon management plan; and (3) coordinate with Treasury's 45Q credit administration to ensure that EPA's operational review findings do not create retroactive eligibility uncertainty for tax credit claims already in process.

4.  **Adopt Technology-Neutral Documentation Standards Allowing for Functional Equivalence:**
    *   **Provision Addressed:** Updated Documentation Standards.
    *   **Justification:** Mandated standardized formats are incompatible with Chevron's existing global data management architecture, imposing substantial transition costs without commensurate environmental benefit and threatening operational consistency [8], [9].
    *   **Specific Change:** Conduct a formal cost-benefit analysis specifically quantifying transition costs for large, globally integrated operators before finalizing any standardized format mandate. Allow regulated entities to demonstrate 'functional equivalence'—i.e., that their existing documentation systems produce data of equal or superior integrity to the proposed standard formats—as an alternative compliance pathway. Convene a technical working group inclusive of large industrial operators to co-develop format standards that are interoperable with existing international reporting frameworks (e.g., ISO 14001, GHG Protocol) rather than creating a U.S.-only silo that fragments global data governance.

[CONCLUSION]
Chevron is committed to environmental stewardship and robust compliance. However, we believe that the proposed rule, as currently drafted, presents significant practical challenges, imposes disproportionate costs, and creates unintended consequences for energy reliability, affordability, and innovation. We urge EPA to reconsider key provisions, particularly the implementation timelines and reporting requirements, to ensure they are realistic, flexible, and aligned with industry operational realities. By adopting the recommendations outlined above, EPA can achieve its compliance modernization objectives without unduly burdening industry, hindering energy security, or stifling the development of critical decarbonization technologies. We appreciate EPA's consideration of our comments and look forward to continued engagement on this important rulemaking.

### References
[1] https://downloads.regulations.gov/EPA-HQ-OAR-2024-0411-0031/attachment_1.pdf
[2] https://downloads.regulations.gov/EPA-HQ-OAR-2023-0434-0297/attachment_1.pdf
[3] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2022-06/fy23-24-oar-draft-npg.pdf
[4] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2023-05/utilities_ria_proposal_2023-05.pdf
[5] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2024-04/final-hon-ria-march-2024_0.pdf
[6] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2022-12/420d22003.pdf
[7] https://www.ren21.net/wp-content/uploads/2019/05/GSR2024_Demand_Full_Report.pdf
[8] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2024-07/ejscreen-tech-doc-version-2-3.pdf
[9] https://atsdr.cdc.gov/place-health/media/pdfs/2024/10/EJI_2024_Technical_Documentation.pdf

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
Environmental Defense Fund (EDF) is a leading national environmental advocacy organization working to create powerful solutions for our most serious environmental problems. EDF combines science, economics, law, and innovative private-sector partnerships to make lasting change. Our work on climate, energy, and methane emissions reduction relies fundamentally on accurate, standardized, and publicly accessible data. We welcome the opportunity to submit comments on the Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA) proposed new regulatory framework, which aims to modernize reporting obligations, standardize documentation, and establish new implementation timelines.

[BACKGROUND]
The EPA's proposed rule, as outlined in the Executive Summary, introduces a comprehensive overhaul of how regulated entities interact with the agency, focusing on the modernization of reporting obligations and the standardization of documentation. Key provisions include revised reporting obligations, new implementation timelines, updated documentation standards, a cost assessment requirement, and an operational impact analysis. EDF strongly supports EPA’s efforts to address perceived gaps in current reporting and documentation practices. These gaps hinder the agency’s ability to monitor compliance, assess the effectiveness of environmental regulations, and ultimately undermine the credibility and efficiency of market-based environmental policies.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
EDF strongly supports the EPA's proposed modernization of data submission protocols for methane reporting. Standardized, high-quality data is the essential foundation for effective methane emission reduction campaigns and market-based environmental policy. Without rigorous, consistent reporting infrastructure, neither regulators nor the private-sector partners EDF works with can accurately measure progress, price emissions appropriately, or verify reductions. The 2024 updates to EPA's Greenhouse Gas Reporting Program Subpart W, which expanded allowances for advanced measurement technologies, demonstrated that modernized protocols can accommodate innovation while improving data quality [1]. However, without standardized submission requirements, the data generated by these advanced technologies remains difficult to aggregate, compare, or use as the basis for market-based incentive structures.

EDF strongly supports the EPA's proposed standardized recordkeeping requirements. Uniform data formats are essential to validating the environmental performance claims made by corporate partners in our sustainability programs and ensuring those claims withstand independent scrutiny. Currently, inconsistent recordkeeping formats across regulated entities make it extremely difficult for EDF to independently verify whether partner companies are meeting their commitments or whether reported reductions are real, additional, and permanent. Standardized formats would allow EDF's technical staff to conduct apples-to-apples comparisons across facilities and sectors, strengthening the scientific rigor and bipartisan credibility that are foundational to our market-based advocacy model. Without standardization, our corporate partnership programs risk being undermined by data-quality problems similar to those the EPA's own Office of Inspector General identified in RFS/RIN reporting, where data-quality problems raised concerns about accuracy and completeness of reported data.

EDF's core mission relies on designing and advocating for market-based environmental policies, including cap-and-trade programs, methane emission reduction frameworks, and corporate sustainability partnerships. These policies are only as credible and effective as the underlying data infrastructure. When reporting standards are inconsistent or documentation is fragmented, it undermines the price signals and accountability mechanisms that make market-based approaches work. Standardized reporting directly reduces the transaction costs EDF and its corporate partners face when verifying emissions claims, benchmarking performance, and designing incentive structures. Poor data quality forces EDF to expend significant organizational resources reconciling inconsistent datasets rather than advancing policy solutions.

In response to the agency's question on expected costs of the proposed compliance requirements, EDF asserts that the long-term compliance cost savings from standardized, high-quality emissions data will substantially outweigh near-term transition costs. For example, CNX Resources reported spending $5.8 million on methane mitigation in 2024 and achieved over 100,000 tons CO2e in annualized benefits, demonstrating that compliance investments generate measurable returns that offset reporting costs. Furthermore, the long-run cost of continued data fragmentation—including EPA's own reduced capacity to assess regulatory effectiveness—constitutes a significant unquantified cost of the status quo. Upfront system design investment by EPA in robust, user-tested digital reporting infrastructure is the single most effective lever for reducing the net compliance cost for regulated facilities.

In direct response to the agency's question regarding the operational impacts of the proposed compliance requirements, EDF asserts that while transition costs are real, the long-term operational benefits of standardized data submission—including reduced duplicative reporting burdens, improved regulatory certainty, and enhanced ability to access market-based compliance pathways—outweigh short-term implementation costs, particularly for operators already investing in advanced monitoring under the 2024 OOOO rules [1, 7]. EDF's experience shows that operators with modern monitoring infrastructure find standardized digital reporting less burdensome than legacy paper-based or facility-specific formats, because automation reduces per-submission labor costs over time.

The methane mitigation sector is expanding nationwide, creating high-quality jobs and enabling operators to capture lost revenue. Strong, predictable federal policy creates the market conditions necessary for this sector to scale. When compliance deadlines are repeatedly delayed—as occurred with the July 2025 Interim Final Rule extending OOOOb/c deadlines—it erodes the regulatory certainty that drives this economic growth [6, 7]. Each delay signals to investors and technology providers that compliance obligations are negotiable, chilling capital deployment into monitoring, reporting, and mitigation infrastructure.

EDF supports the EPA's goal of improving data integrity through standardized recordkeeping, but urges the agency to design implementation timelines that allow corporate partners to align new compliance formats with existing voluntary sustainability reporting frameworks, thereby reducing duplicative burden without sacrificing data integrity. If the EPA's new standardized formats are implemented through unreliable or poorly designed digital tools, the compliance cost burden will spike, undermining our ability to make the economic case for partnership. The EPA's own record shows this risk is real: in 2024, EPA extended the reporting deadline for Chemical Data Reporting specifically because "technical issues with the ability of the reporting tool to correctly capture" required data fields caused widespread compliance failures [4]. A similar failure in this rulemaking would impose unnecessary costs on facilities, damage agency credibility, and create a chilling effect on voluntary sustainability commitments. The EPA has advanced its goal to provide better access to compliance data by improving its online tools [8]. EDF supports this continued commitment to public access and robust digital infrastructure.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
**Regarding Revised Reporting Obligations and Data Submission Protocols:**
*   EPA should finalize the new data submission protocols as proposed, but explicitly incorporate provisions that (1) accept data generated by advanced and emerging measurement technologies as recognized under the 2024 Subpart W updates [1], (2) require machine-readable, standardized data formats to enable third-party verification and public transparency, and (3) establish a clear, publicly accessible data portal so that advocacy organizations, researchers, and market participants can use the submitted data to assess industry-wide progress.
*   EPA should also offer a streamlined "single submission pathway" for entities that can demonstrate their advanced monitoring data meets or exceeds the new protocol standards, reducing duplicative reporting while maintaining data quality.

**Regarding Updated Documentation Standards and Standardized Recordkeeping:**
*   EPA should finalize the standardized recordkeeping format requirement as proposed and resist any industry requests to allow "equivalent" but non-uniform formats.
*   The agency should additionally specify that standardized records must be machine-readable and publicly accessible via a searchable database, enabling third-party verification by organizations like EDF.
*   EPA should explicitly map the proposed standardized recordkeeping formats to existing voluntary frameworks such as GHG Protocol, CDP, and TCFD disclosure templates, identifying areas of overlap to allow facilities to satisfy both requirements through a single data collection effort.
*   EPA should publish a crosswalk document at the time of final rule publication that corporate sustainability officers can use to integrate EPA-required data fields into their existing reporting systems.

**Regarding New Implementation Timelines:**
*   The agency should finalize the proposed data submission timelines without further extension, and explicitly state in the preamble that these deadlines reflect the minimum time necessary for compliance given the lead times already built into prior rulemakings [6, 7].
*   EPA should clarify that the new reporting protocols are designed to be technologically accessible, reducing the operational burden argument that regulated entities have used to justify prior deadline extensions.
*   EPA should establish a phased implementation timeline of no less than 18 months for initial system upgrades, and for entities already operating under the 2024 OOOO framework [1, 7], allowing them to integrate new submission requirements into existing compliance systems rather than building parallel infrastructure.
*   This phased timeline should include an initial 6-month "parallel reporting" period during which facilities may submit in both legacy and new formats to identify technical gaps.

**Regarding Cost Assessment and Operational Impacts:**
*   In response to the agency's question on expected costs of the proposed compliance requirements, EPA should conduct and publish a full cost-benefit analysis that explicitly accounts for the downstream efficiency gains to market-based compliance systems and disaggregates compliance costs by entity size and operational type.
*   EPA should model how standardized documentation reduces duplicative reporting burdens across overlapping regulatory frameworks (e.g., GHGRP, state-level cap-and-trade, and emerging SEC climate disclosure requirements).
*   EPA should provide technical assistance resources, particularly for smaller operators, to prevent a two-tier compliance landscape that disadvantages smaller market participants.
*   The agency should conduct and publicly release results of beta testing of any new digital recordkeeping portal with a representative sample of regulated facilities—including small and mid-sized entities—at least 6 months before the compliance deadline.
*   EPA should establish a dedicated technical assistance helpdesk for the first 12 months of implementation, modeled on successful EPA compliance assistance programs.
*   EPA should commit to a no-penalty grace period of 90 days for good-faith compliance attempts that encounter documented system errors.
*   EPA should publish quantitative cost estimates disaggregated by facility size and sector in the final rule's economic analysis, so that EDF and other stakeholders can independently verify that compliance costs are proportionate to environmental benefits.
*   EPA should conduct and publish a formal harmonization analysis mapping the new submission protocols against existing Subpart W, OOOO rule, and any remaining Waste Emissions Charge reporting obligations to eliminate redundancy.

[CONCLUSION]
EDF strongly supports EPA’s proposed modernization of reporting obligations, standardization of documentation, and establishment of new implementation timelines. This framework is crucial for improving data integrity, enhancing the effectiveness of market-based environmental policies, and ultimately achieving significant economic and climate benefits. By adopting the recommendations outlined above, EPA can maximize the public benefit of this important rulemaking, ensure robust monitoring and verification, and minimize unnecessary compliance burdens, particularly through the development of user-friendly and reliable digital tools.

### References
[1] https://www.arnoldporter.com/en/perspectives/blogs/environmental-edge/2024/04/implementation-of-epas-new-methane-rule
[4] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2023-03/fy-2024-epa-bib.pdf
[6] https://encinoenviron.com/epa-extends-methane-compliance-deadlines/
[7] https://www.taxpayer.net/energy-natural-resources/epa-finalizes-delays-of-2024-methane-waste-rule/
[8] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2025-03/eoy2024.pdf

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment (CDPHE) appreciates the opportunity to submit comments on the Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA) proposed regulatory framework to update compliance and oversight mechanisms. As the primary state agency responsible for protecting and improving Colorado's public health and environment, CDPHE strongly supports EPA's efforts to modernize reporting obligations, standardize documentation, and establish clear implementation timelines for regulated entities. These updates are crucial for ensuring the effectiveness of environmental regulations, particularly in states like Colorado that face significant air quality challenges and have already adopted stringent state-level emissions reduction standards.

CDPHE's expertise spans air quality regulation, state implementation plan (SIP) development and enforcement, methane emissions control, and environmental justice program implementation. Our interest in this rulemaking is twofold: first, to ensure that federal standards are at least as stringent as Colorado's existing regulations to prevent a regulatory patchwork that could undermine public health and environmental protection; and second, to ensure alignment between federal and state compliance frameworks to reduce operational burdens on both regulators and regulated entities. We believe a robust federal approach that complements and strengthens Colorado's existing efforts is essential for achieving cleaner air, reducing greenhouse gas emissions, and protecting the health of our communities, especially those disproportionately affected by pollution.

[BACKGROUND]
Colorado's Front Range, encompassing the Denver Metro/Northern Front Range (DMNFR) area, faces an acute and worsening ozone crisis. In July 2024, the DMNFR area was voluntarily reclassified from Moderate to Serious nonattainment under the 2015 ozone National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS). This reclassification followed a summer of unprecedented ozone pollution, with Colorado recording 40 ozone exceedance days in 2024 alone [1]. As a result, Colorado must submit a Serious nonattainment attainment plan by January 1, 2026 [2]. Volatile organic compound (VOC) and methane co-emissions from oil and gas operations are a primary driver of ozone formation and a significant contributor to greenhouse gas emissions in Colorado.

In response to these challenges, CDPHE has developed and implemented among the most stringent state methane and VOC regulations for the oil and gas sector in the nation, codified primarily in Colorado Air Quality Control Commission Regulation 7, Part B. These regulations include requirements for leak detection and repair (LDAR), emissions controls, and reporting. Additionally, CDPHE is currently developing a state plan for existing sources under 40 CFR Part 60, Subpart OOOOc, with a submission deadline of March 9, 2026 [7].

The proposed rule directly impacts Colorado's ability to meet its SIP obligations and effectively enforce its state regulations. Specifically, the rule's provisions on revised reporting obligations, new implementation timelines, updated documentation standards, cost assessment requirements, and operational impact analysis are critical to ensuring timely emissions reductions, improving data quality, and aligning federal and state regulatory efforts.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
**Implementation Timelines and Ozone Nonattainment Obligations:**
CDPHE strongly supports the proposed compliance windows as structured and urges EPA to maintain or accelerate the implementation timelines for Serious nonattainment areas. Given Colorado's acute ozone crisis and the January 1, 2026 deadline for our Serious nonattainment attainment plan, clear, enforceable federal compliance windows are essential. These timelines provide CDPHE with the necessary regulatory scaffolding to compel timely emissions reductions from regulated entities, particularly oil and gas operators in Weld County, on a schedule that aligns with our SIP obligations. Without firm federal compliance timelines, CDPHE faces the operational burden of defending SIP commitments to EPA while lacking the downstream enforcement leverage over regulated entities that this rule would provide.

Furthermore, overly long or ambiguous compliance timelines for Serious nonattainment areas could undermine Colorado's ability to demonstrate reasonable further progress (RFP) in its SIP. Failure to demonstrate credible progress toward attainment exposes the state to EPA disapproval, which can trigger mandatory sanctions, including the 2-to-1 emission offset requirement for new major sources and potential loss of federal highway funding. These sanctions would impose far greater costs on Colorado's economy and regulated community than the proposed rule's compliance requirements. CDPHE's SIP projections, built around the assumption of timely compliance, could become legally indefensible if the final rule weakens or extends the proposed compliance windows in response to industry cost arguments.

**Standardized Reporting and Documentation:**
CDPHE strongly supports the proposed standardized recordkeeping format requirement and standardized data submission protocols. Currently, CDPHE receives emissions data in inconsistent, operator-defined formats that require significant staff time to normalize before analysis. This creates data quality gaps that undermine our ozone nonattainment obligations and hinder our ability to effectively enforce Colorado's state methane and VOC regulations. Standardized federal recordkeeping formats and submission protocols would allow CDPHE to more rapidly identify discrepancies between state-reported and federally-reported emissions data, cross-reference federal compliance data against state-level emissions inventories more efficiently, and accelerate enforcement actions that directly protect Front Range communities. Without standardization, our environmental justice program staff spend disproportionate time on data reconciliation rather than community-facing compliance work, increasing CDPHE's operational burden.

**Cost Assessment and Operational Impacts:**
CDPHE strongly supports EPA's request for industry cost data on compliance transitions, but urges the agency to require granular, facility-level cost reporting from oil and gas operators in Colorado, rather than accepting aggregated national estimates that obscure regional cost structures and compliance capacity disparities. Colorado's experience with industry cost estimates during state rulemaking proceedings frequently shows that actual compliance costs are overstated once operators implement controls. If repeated at the federal level, this pattern could lead EPA to set unnecessarily lenient timelines or thresholds. Colorado's oil and gas sector spans both large integrated operators and smaller independent producers in the DJ Basin and Piceance Basin, whose cost profiles differ dramatically. Without disaggregated cost data, EPA cannot accurately assess whether proposed implementation timelines are appropriately calibrated for Colorado's specific operator mix.

CDPHE also urges EPA to reject industry cost arguments that fail to account for the demonstrated co-benefits of compliance, including avoided enforcement penalties, reduced health costs in environmental justice communities, and the economic value of methane captured rather than vented. CDPHE's experience in Colorado shows that these co-benefits can substantially offset or exceed compliance costs for well-managed operators. For example, EPA's own enforcement data shows that Apache Corporation paid a $4 million civil penalty for Clean Air Act violations at 23 oil and gas facilities and committed to bringing 400 facilities into compliance [5], demonstrating the substantial financial risk of non-compliance. When EPA evaluates industry-submitted cost data, failure to require operators to account for these offsets will systematically bias the cost record against strong standards, potentially leading to weaker implementation timelines that perpetuate health disparities in Colorado's overburdened communities.

CDPHE supports the proposed compliance timelines as a mechanism to address the agency's question regarding operational impacts, and specifically argues that the timelines reduce—rather than increase—CDPHE's operational burden. Colorado has already invested significant regulatory infrastructure in oil and gas emissions control through Regulation 7, and CDPHE's air quality program operates under the dual constraint of Front Range ozone nonattainment obligations and a politically active oil and gas industry. The proposed compliance windows create a federally enforceable schedule that CDPHE can reference when industry stakeholders challenge state-level timelines as overly aggressive. Without this federal alignment, CDPHE staff must independently defend every compliance deadline in state proceedings, increasing litigation risk and administrative costs. The proposed rule's standardized timelines reduce this duplicative burden and provide CDPHE with a defensible federal baseline.

Finally, CDPHE acknowledges that upfront transition costs for smaller operators in Colorado's DJ Basin and Piceance Basin are real. These operators are already absorbing compliance costs from Colorado's state methane rules (Regulation 7) and, for high-emitting facilities, the federal Waste Emissions Charge beginning at $900/metric ton in 2024 and rising to $1,500/metric ton by 2026 [3], [4]. Adding a new, immediate recordkeeping format transition requirement on top of these layered obligations could strain smaller operators' compliance capacity, potentially leading to underreporting or data quality degradation—the exact opposite of the rule's intent. CDPHE's enforcement experience shows that compliance quality degrades when operators are simultaneously managing multiple overlapping regulatory transitions.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To ensure that the final rule is at least as stringent as Colorado's existing regulations, prevents regulatory patchwork, and effectively addresses air quality challenges and operational impacts, CDPHE offers the following specific recommendations:

**On Implementation Timelines:**
1.  **Maintain or Accelerate Timelines for Serious Nonattainment Areas:** EPA should retain the proposed compliance windows for Serious nonattainment areas and explicitly clarify in the final rule preamble that state agencies administering SIPs in Serious or higher nonattainment classifications may use these federal compliance deadlines as a floor—not a ceiling—when establishing more stringent state-level schedules.
2.  **Confirm Binding August 3, 2027 Deadline:** EPA should confirm that the August 3, 2027 Serious-area implementation deadline is binding and not subject to discretionary extension absent extraordinary circumstances, to prevent regulated entities from using ambiguity to delay compliance.
3.  **Address SIP Attainment Demonstration Relationship:** EPA should explicitly address in the final rule's preamble the relationship between the proposed compliance windows and SIP attainment demonstration requirements, confirming that the timelines were calibrated to support Serious nonattainment area attainment plans.
4.  **Provide Technical Assistance for SIP Integration:** EPA should commit to providing technical assistance to states like Colorado that must integrate these federal timelines into pending SIP submissions before the January 2026 deadline, and should clarify that any entity-specific compliance schedule variances granted under the rule will not be treated as SIP revisions requiring separate EPA approval, to avoid creating an administrative bottleneck that delays Colorado's attainment planning.
5.  **Reduce State Operational Burden through Incorporation by Reference:** EPA should include in the final rule a provision explicitly recognizing that state agencies in nonattainment areas may incorporate the federal compliance windows by reference into state SIP submissions and enforcement orders. This would reduce duplicative rulemaking at the state level and allow CDPHE to direct resources toward monitoring, enforcement, and environmental justice program implementation rather than re-litigating timeline justifications already established at the federal level. EPA should also provide model SIP language that states can adapt.
6.  **Implement Tiered Timelines for Smaller Operators:** In direct response to EPA's question on expected compliance costs and operational impacts, EPA should provide a tiered implementation timeline, with larger operators (those already subject to the Waste Emissions Charge threshold of 25,000 metric tons CO2e) required to comply within 12 months, and smaller operators given 24 months.

**On Standardized Reporting and Documentation:**
7.  **Ensure Interoperability with State Systems:** EPA should design the standardized recordkeeping format to be explicitly interoperable with state-level reporting systems, including Colorado's Air Pollution Control Division (APCD) electronic reporting infrastructure.
8.  **Convene State Working Group on Data Schema:** EPA should convene a working group with leading state air agencies—including CDPHE—prior to finalizing the data schema to ensure federal format fields map cleanly onto existing state data fields, avoiding a scenario where operators must maintain parallel, non-harmonized records for state and federal compliance.
9.  **Accommodate Colorado's Existing Reporting Fields:** EPA should ensure the standardized submission protocols accommodate Colorado's existing Regulation 7 and Air Quality Control Commission reporting fields so that operators are not burdened with maintaining two parallel, incompatible reporting streams.
10. **Design with API/Data-Sharing Interfaces:** EPA should design the new data submission protocols with explicit API or data-sharing interfaces that allow state air quality agencies to directly access and ingest federal compliance submissions without manual re-entry.
11. **Publish Machine-Readable Data Schema:** EPA should publish a machine-readable data schema compatible with state ICIS-Air and emissions inventory systems.
12. **Include State Agency Liaison Provision:** EPA should include a state agency liaison provision in the final rule that requires EPA regional offices to provide technical onboarding support to state agencies within 90 days of the rule's effective date.
13. **Provide Free Data Templates and Tools:** EPA should develop and publish free, open-source data templates and submission tools compatible with the standardized format.
14. **Establish Dedicated Technical Assistance Program for Small Operators:** EPA should establish a dedicated technical assistance program for small operators, potentially coordinated through state agencies like CDPHE that already have established relationships with these operators.

**On Cost Assessment Requirements:**
15. **Require Granular Cost Data:** EPA should require that cost data submissions be disaggregated by: (1) operator size (major vs. independent), (2) basin/geographic region, (3) facility type and vintage, and (4) existing state regulatory baseline.
16. **Require Incremental Federal Costs:** Operators in states like Colorado that already comply with stringent state methane and VOC rules should be required to report incremental federal compliance costs above their existing state obligations—not gross compliance costs—to avoid double-counting and to give EPA an accurate picture of the true marginal burden of the new federal standards.
17. **Cross-Reference with Capital Expenditure Data:** EPA should cross-reference submitted cost estimates against actual capital expenditure data; upstream oil and gas capital expenditures surpassed $600 billion globally in 2024 [6], suggesting the sector has substantial investment capacity that industry cost submissions may not adequately reflect.
18. **Require 'Co-benefit Offset' Section:** In direct response to EPA's question about alternatives to the proposed compliance requirements, CDPHE recommends that EPA require all cost submissions to include a standardized 'co-benefit offset' section that documents: (1) estimated value of methane recovered through improved monitoring, (2) avoided penalty exposure based on current enforcement trends, and (3) projected reduction in health-related costs in nearby communities.
19. **Reject Incomplete Cost Arguments:** EPA should reject industry cost arguments that fail to account for the demonstrated co-benefits of compliance, as these systematically bias the cost record against strong standards.

[CONCLUSION]
The Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment strongly supports EPA's proposed regulatory framework to modernize compliance and oversight mechanisms. These updates are vital for addressing Colorado's acute ozone crisis, effectively enforcing our stringent state methane and VOC regulations, and protecting public health and the environment, particularly in nonattainment and environmental justice communities.

We urge EPA to finalize a rule that maintains or strengthens the proposed implementation timelines for Serious nonattainment areas, designs standardized reporting and documentation requirements with explicit state agency integration pathways, and requires granular, co-benefit-inclusive cost data to avoid underestimating the benefits and overestimating the burdens of compliance. A robust federal approach that complements and strengthens Colorado's existing efforts is essential for achieving cleaner air, reducing greenhouse gas emissions, and preventing a regulatory patchwork that could undermine our collective progress.

We appreciate EPA's consideration of these comments and look forward to continued collaboration to ensure the final rule achieves its intended objectives while supporting state efforts to protect public health and the environment.

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The American Water Works Association (AWWA) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the Environmental Protection Agency's (EPA) proposed regulatory framework to update compliance and oversight mechanisms (Docket EPA-HQ-OAR-2025-0194). AWWA represents over 50,000 members, including more than 4,600 utilities, dedicated to the safe and sustainable management of water resources. We strongly support the development of health-protective water quality standards that safeguard public health and the environment. However, we are deeply concerned that the implementation timelines and unfunded mandates associated with the proposed Revised Reporting Obligations, New Implementation Timelines, and Updated Documentation Standards will create untenable financial and operational burdens for water utilities, disproportionately impacting small systems and ultimately jeopardizing ratepayers’ ability to afford essential water services. Our comments focus on the practical implications for the drinking water sector, providing feedback on the expected costs, operational impacts, and implementation timelines.

[BACKGROUND]
EPA's proposal introduces a comprehensive overhaul of how regulated entities interact with the agency, focusing specifically on the modernization of reporting obligations and the standardization of documentation, along with the establishment of new implementation timelines. AWWA's comments address the following key provisions of the proposed rule:
- **Revised Reporting Obligations:** Concerns regarding the administrative burden and operational impacts of new data submission protocols.
- **New Implementation Timelines:** Concerns that the proposed windows are insufficient to accommodate utility capital planning cycles, infrastructure procurement, and the concurrent demands of existing regulatory mandates.
- **Updated Documentation Standards:** Concerns about the costs and operational challenges associated with migrating to new, standardized record-keeping formats from existing state-mandated systems.
- **Cost Assessment Requirement:** Our response provides detailed feedback on the expected costs, disaggregated by system size, and highlights significant underestimations in the agency's initial assessments.
- **Operational Impact Analysis:** Our comments detail the real-world operational challenges, including staff diversion, IT system upgrades, and the opportunity costs of reallocating technical resources.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
AWWA's member utilities are simultaneously navigating a complex landscape of overlapping compliance obligations, aging infrastructure needs, and ratepayer affordability constraints. The proposed new compliance framework, as currently structured, fails to account for these realities and will impose significant, unquantified costs that will ultimately be borne by ratepayers.

**1. Financial Burden of Concurrent Regulatory Mandates and Underestimated Costs:**
AWWA member utilities are currently grappling with significant financial demands from simultaneous regulatory requirements, including EPA's 2024 PFAS National Primary Drinking Water Regulation (NPDWR), lead service line replacement requirements, and ongoing aging infrastructure rehabilitation. These mandates compete for the same capital and operational budgets. The addition of new reporting and documentation standardization requirements, without adequate transition time or cost offsets, will force utilities to divert resources from direct public health investments. Between 2023 and 2030, drinking water utility spending is projected to climb to nearly $13.5 billion driven by PFAS MCL compliance alone [1]. Layering new administrative compliance costs onto this trajectory without a rigorous, utility-specific economic impact analysis risks breaking the financial capacity of small and medium-sized systems that lack economies of scale. EPA's current cost estimates significantly understate the true burden on ratepayers and water systems [2].

**2. Insufficient Compliance Windows for Capital Planning Cycles:**
Water utilities operate on long-term capital improvement plans (CIPs) that are typically developed on 5-20 year cycles, subject to rate case proceedings, bond financing approvals, and state regulatory review. The proposed compliance windows, as structured, would force utilities to compress or bypass these established planning processes, creating financial instability for ratepayers. AWWA's member utilities are simultaneously managing an estimated $648.8 billion in drinking water infrastructure investment needs over the next 20 years—a figure that has grown by over 7.6% since EPA's prior estimate [3, 4]. Any new compliance obligation layered onto this existing capital burden must be sequenced carefully to avoid crowding out higher-priority safety investments such as lead service line replacement and PFAS treatment upgrades.

**3. Disproportionate Operational Burdens from New Data Submission Protocols:**
The proposed new data submission protocols impose disproportionate operational burdens on water utilities without adequate transition timelines, threatening the integrity of water quality standards development processes that depend on consistent, high-quality data flows. Overhauling data submission protocols on an accelerated timeline forces utilities to divert limited technical staff and IT resources away from core water quality monitoring and standards development activities. Smaller utilities in particular lack dedicated compliance IT departments, meaning the same personnel responsible for water quality standards work must absorb the administrative burden of learning and implementing new reporting systems. This directly degrades the quality and timeliness of the underlying water quality data that EPA relies upon for standards development—the very outcome the rule seeks to improve [5].

**4. Ratepayer Affordability Crisis:**
Ratepayer affordability is a binding constraint for AWWA member utilities, particularly in communities already experiencing economic stress. Black & Veatch's 2024 Water and Wastewater Rate Report identifies affordability as the top challenge facing water utilities, and uniform compliance mandates that do not account for system size or financial capacity will accelerate rate increases that fall hardest on low-income households. When compliance costs rise—whether from new monitoring equipment, IT system upgrades, staff training, or third-party data management vendors—those costs flow through to ratepayer bills. EPA's 2024 Water Affordability Needs Assessment documents that water affordability burdens are already significant across many household and utility categories [3, 6]. Imposing new data submission protocol costs without a rigorous cost-benefit analysis risks pushing low-income ratepayers past affordability thresholds, potentially triggering service disconnections and undermining the public health goals that water quality standards are designed to protect.

**5. Treatment Technology Readiness Gap for Emerging Contaminants:**
The proposed compliance windows fail to account for the treatment technology readiness gap facing water utilities, particularly for emerging contaminants such as PFAS, and imposing fixed compliance deadlines before proven, scalable treatment solutions are commercially available will impose disproportionate and potentially stranded costs on ratepayers. AWWA's water quality standards development work depends on the availability of validated treatment technologies that can be reliably deployed at scale across systems of varying size and source water chemistry. USGS estimates that at least 45% of U.S. tap water may contain PFAS, and documented cases—such as Hudson, MA and Wellesley, MA—show that PFAS compliance has driven utility rate increases of 18% or more [3, 4]. If the proposed compliance windows require utilities to commit capital to treatment infrastructure before best available technologies (BAT) are fully proven or before EPA finalizes associated technical guidance, utilities risk investing in systems that become obsolete or non-compliant upon subsequent regulatory revision, resulting in stranded asset costs that are ultimately borne by ratepayers.

**6. Costs from Mandatory Standardized Record-Keeping Formats:**
The proposed mandatory standardized record-keeping formats impose disproportionate compliance costs on water utilities without adequate consideration of the highly variable data systems, legacy infrastructure, and existing state-level reporting frameworks already in use across the drinking water sector. Water utilities—particularly small and medium-sized systems—have invested significantly in existing data management platforms, Quality Assurance Project Plans (QAPPs), and state-integrated reporting systems such as STORET and SDWIS that already meet data integrity objectives [6, 7]. Mandating migration to a new, standardized federal format would require utilities to simultaneously maintain dual reporting systems during any transition period, retrain staff, and potentially procure new software or hardware. These costs fall directly on ratepayers, exacerbating the affordability crisis already documented across the sector.

**7. Inadequate Cost Solicitation Framework:**
The agency's solicitation for 'expected cost' data, while welcome, is structured in a way that will systematically undercount operational impacts on water quality standards development processes, particularly for utilities with limited technical staff and legacy data management infrastructure. The proposed standardization of documentation and reporting formats will require utilities to overhaul existing data collection workflows, retrain personnel, and in many cases procure new software or third-party compliance support. For the hundreds of small and rural water systems that rely on AWWA guidance and technical assistance, these are not trivial administrative adjustments—they represent fundamental operational changes. The agency's cost solicitation, as structured, does not appear to account for indirect costs such as staff retraining, legacy system migration, or the opportunity cost of diverting technical staff from water quality monitoring and treatment optimization to administrative compliance tasks [1, 2].

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
In light of these significant impacts, AWWA urges EPA to revise the proposed compliance framework to incorporate the following recommendations:

**1. Extend Compliance Timelines:**
*   **Revised Reporting Obligations & Updated Documentation Standards:** Extend the implementation timeline for new data submission protocols and standardized documentation to a minimum of 24-36 months, with a phased rollout that prioritizes large utilities first, allowing smaller systems to learn from early adopters [5].
*   **New Implementation Timelines:** Establish a tiered, risk-based schedule that grants a minimum baseline compliance window of five years for all regulated entities, with extensions of up to seven years available for small and medium-sized systems that can demonstrate financial hardship or capital planning constraints. The agency should also require that compliance milestones be synchronized with existing state-approved CIP and rate-setting cycles [3, 4]. Specifically, AWWA recommends a tiered compliance framework modeled on existing Safe Drinking Water Act precedents, under which: (1) large systems (serving 100,000+) implement full documentation standardization within 18 months; (2) medium systems (10,000–99,999) receive a 36-month implementation window with simplified reporting templates; and (3) small systems (under 10,000) are offered a compliance assistance pathway with agency-provided template tools and extended timelines of 48 months [1, 2].
*   **Concurrent Regulatory Mandates:** Extend the overall compliance transition timeline by a minimum of 24 months beyond what is currently proposed, and establish a phased implementation schedule that sequences new reporting obligations after—not concurrent with—existing PFAS and lead compliance deadlines [1, 2].

**2. Provide Federal Funding for Treatment Upgrades and Technical Assistance:**
*   **Treatment Upgrades:** Condition the activation of compliance deadlines on the concurrent finalization and publication of EPA-approved BAT guidance and associated cost-effectiveness data for each regulated contaminant or reporting category [3, 4]. EPA should establish a formal technology readiness review checkpoint at the midpoint of any compliance window, allowing utilities to petition for timeline adjustments if commercially available treatment solutions have not achieved sufficient market maturity or cost stability.
*   **Technical Assistance for Small Systems:** AWWA recommends that small systems (under 10,000 customers) be offered eligibility for Drinking Water State Revolving Fund (DWSRF) administrative assistance grants to offset transition costs for documentation and reporting standardization. EPA should also explore whether federal technical assistance funding (e.g., through existing Water Infrastructure Finance and Innovation Act (WIFIA) programs) can offset transition costs for all systems [1, 2, 6, 7].
*   **Hardship Waivers:** EPA should establish a hardship waiver or extended compliance pathway for utilities serving communities where new compliance costs would push average household water bills above EPA's affordability threshold [5, 6].

**3. Improve Cost Analysis and Solicitation:**
*   **Disaggregated Cost Analysis:** Conduct and publish a full, disaggregated cost analysis that separately estimates burdens on small, medium, and large utilities before finalizing the rule. This analysis should incorporate EPA's own Water Affordability Needs Assessment methodology to model ratepayer impact [5, 6]. The analysis must disaggregate costs by system size (large, medium, small, and very small systems), account for the cost of transitioning from existing state-mandated formats (e.g., California's Integrated Report QAPP framework and Ohio's SDWIS-based reporting), and include ratepayer impact assessments [6, 7].
*   **Expanded Cost Data Solicitation:** Expand its cost data solicitation to explicitly request information on: (1) one-time transition costs including IT system upgrades and staff retraining; (2) ongoing annual administrative burden in staff-hours; (3) third-party contractor costs for systems without in-house compliance capacity; and (4) opportunity costs associated with diverting technical resources from water quality operations [1, 2]. AWWA further recommends that EPA convene a technical working group—inclusive of AWWA, state drinking water programs, and small system representatives—to develop a standardized cost reporting template that captures these categories consistently across respondents, improving the quality of data the agency receives.
*   **Affordability Analysis:** Conduct a formal affordability analysis consistent with its March 2024 Financial Capability Assessment Guidance before finalizing any uniform compliance timeline [1, 2].

**4. Enhance Guidance and Technical Support:**
*   **Clear Guidelines:** Provide clear, written guidelines and quality assurance/quality control protocols—as recommended in EPA's own 2023 CWA 303(d) guidance—before any compliance deadline takes effect, not after [5]. A pre-compliance technical assistance period with agency support should be mandatory.
*   **Update FCA Guidance:** Commit to updating its Financial Capability Assessment Guidance (March 2024) to explicitly incorporate the cumulative burden of simultaneous regulatory requirements [1, 2].

[CONCLUSION]
AWWA reiterates its strong commitment to public health and the development of effective, evidence-based water quality standards. However, the proposed compliance and oversight framework, as currently defined, imposes unrealistic timelines and significant, unquantified costs that will be disproportionately borne by small water systems and ratepayers. We urge EPA to extend compliance timelines, provide federal funding for treatment upgrades and technical assistance, and conduct a more comprehensive and disaggregated cost analysis that accurately reflects the real-world operational and financial impacts on the drinking water sector. We stand ready to collaborate with EPA to develop a compliance framework that is both protective of public health and sustainable for the nation's water systems.

### References
[1] https://www.jdsupra.com/post/fileServer.aspx?fName=104c122c-c758-405e-90f8-70f37029e98b.pdf
[2] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2023-04/fy24-cj-00-overview.pdf
[3] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2023-03/fy-2024-epa-bib.pdf
[4] https://ag.ok.gov/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/WQSIP-2023-24-Review.pdf
[5] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2023-03/2024IRmemo_032923.pdf
[6] https://edr.state.fl.us/content/natural-resources/2024_AnnualAssessmentWaterResources_Chapter4.pdf
[7] https://www.calwater.com/docs/ccr/2024/liv-liv-2024.pdf
