{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
Kwik Print & Graphics appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA) proposed new regulatory framework for updating compliance and oversight mechanisms (Docket EPA-HQ-OAR-2025-0194). As a 10-employee commercial offset and digital printing operation, we are deeply concerned that the proposal, particularly its revised reporting obligations, new implementation timelines, and updated documentation standards, will impose disproportionate compliance costs and operational burdens on small businesses like ours. We urge the EPA to adopt a more flexible, tiered approach that recognizes the unique challenges faced by small commercial printers, including extended timelines for ink reformulation, sector-specific exemptions, and standards aligned with the availability of compliant technologies.

[BACKGROUND]
The EPA’s proposal introduces a comprehensive overhaul of how regulated entities interact with the agency, focusing on the modernization of reporting obligations and the standardization of documentation. Specifically, it highlights the establishment of new implementation timelines, revised reporting protocols, updated documentation standards, and a requirement for entities to provide data on expected costs and operational impacts. Our comments will address these key provisions, emphasizing their impact on small commercial printing operations that rely on solvent-based inks and cleaning agents, and advocating for a regulatory framework that is both environmentally effective and economically feasible for small businesses.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]

**1. Disproportionate Compliance Costs and Operational Burden for Small Commercial Printers:**
The proposed revised reporting obligations and updated documentation standards impose a significant administrative burden on small businesses like Kwik Print & Graphics. As a 10-employee shop, we lack dedicated environmental health and safety (EHS) staff. Every hour spent learning, implementing, and executing new data submission protocols or maintaining standardized digital records is an hour diverted from production and revenue-generating activities. According to a 2023 National Association of Manufacturers report, compliance costs for small businesses can reach $50,100 per employee per year, more than double the $24,800 per employee cost borne by larger manufacturers [1]. For a 10-person shop operating on thin commercial printing margins, even a fraction of that burden could threaten our viability.

Transitioning to a new standardized digital recordkeeping format, as required by the updated documentation standards, would force us to either hire outside EHS consultants—an unbudgeted expense we estimate at $3,000–$8,000 per year based on local vendor quotes—or divert our press operators from production duties to learn and maintain a new documentation system. This is a direct operational impact the agency appears not to have adequately quantified for small printers. Unlike large printing corporations with dedicated compliance departments, we have no internal capacity to absorb this administrative burden without cutting production hours or staff. The EPA's own regulatory impact analyses for similar TSCA reporting rules (e.g., the 2023 TSCA reporting rulemaking for PFAS) acknowledged that small manufacturers face substantially higher per-unit compliance costs than large entities [4], yet the proposed standardized format appears to apply uniformly regardless of entity size. Our VOC emission profile is not static—it changes with every ink reformulation our suppliers push to us and with every custom color-matching job we run for clients. A rigid standardized format with fixed data fields assumes a stable, predictable chemical inventory that simply does not reflect how a small commercial printer operates. If the format requires us to pre-register specific solvent products or ink formulations and then log deviations, we will be generating paperwork exceptions constantly, not because we are out of compliance, but because the format does not fit our operational reality.

The EPA's own Compliance Monitoring Program received a $50.9 million budget increase for FY 2024 [6], signaling heightened enforcement activity precisely as small businesses are being asked to absorb new compliance infrastructure costs with no corresponding agency support specifically for small printers.

**2. Unworkable Implementation Timelines Due to Supplier-Side Ink Reformulation Delays and Customer Color Matching Requirements:**
The proposed new implementation timelines fail to account for the reality that small commercial printers like Kwik Print & Graphics have no direct control over when compliant ink formulations become available from their suppliers. We cannot manufacture our own inks, and we cannot force our suppliers to accelerate reformulation on our behalf. Industry evidence shows that even large, well-resourced ink manufacturers like Flint Group—which confirmed FCM compliance only after completing full raw material evaluations and supplier data collection across their entire supply chain—required substantial lead time to achieve reformulation ahead of a December 2025 deadline [2]. If a major global ink supplier needs that runway, a 10-person print shop with no procurement leverage and no EHS staff has no realistic path to compliance within a compressed window. We face a situation where we could be found non-compliant not because of our own inaction, but because our supplier's reformulated product simply does not yet exist on the market.

Furthermore, as a 10-employee commercial printer, we rely on solvent-based inks to meet precise Pantone spot-color specifications demanded by our customers. Per industry practice documented in EPA's own communications guidance, color files must be defined by specific Pantone spot-color numbers for accurate output [8]. If the proposed documentation and reformulation standards require us to transition to compliant ink systems on a fixed schedule, we cannot guarantee color fidelity to existing customer contracts. We would face breach-of-contract exposure, customer attrition, and potential loss of accounts that represent the majority of our revenue—all without a single EHS professional on staff to manage the transition. Customer color matching represents a critical operational constraint that the proposed timelines do not adequately address.

Our VOC emission profile is not static—it changes with every ink reformulation our suppliers push to us and with every custom color-matching job we run for clients. When a supplier substitutes a solvent component in a cleaning agent or ink base, our VOC emissions data changes, often without advance notice to us. Under the proposed new submission protocols, we would be required to report updated emissions data on a standardized schedule that does not align with the unpredictable cadence of supplier-driven reformulations. This creates a scenario where our submitted data is either perpetually out of date or we are forced to halt production while we recalculate and resubmit—neither of which serves the agency's stated goal of accurate compliance monitoring. This is a direct operational impact the agency appears not to have considered.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
In direct response to the EPA's questions about expected costs and operational impacts of the proposed compliance requirements, Kwik Print & Graphics recommends the following modifications to the rule:

1.  **Small Business Exemptions and Tiered Compliance Pathways for Reporting and Documentation:**
    *   **Revised Reporting Obligations:** The EPA should conduct and publish a formal Small Business Regulatory Enforcement Fairness Act (SBREFA) analysis specific to commercial printing operations before finalizing any new data submission protocols. The agency should also establish a tiered compliance pathway that scales reporting complexity to facility size and emission volume, exempting or simplifying requirements for facilities below a defined VOC emission threshold. As an alternative, EPA should develop and formally offer a 'Small Commercial Printer Simplified VOC Reporting Option' modeled on existing sector-specific compliance tools such as the Small Entity Compliance Guide framework used in other EPA programs [7]. This alternative pathway should allow qualifying facilities—defined by employee count (under 50) and annual VOC usage below a defined threshold—to submit an annual product-category-based certification in lieu of the full proposed data submission protocol.
    *   **Updated Documentation Standards:** EPA should establish a tiered recordkeeping framework where facilities below a defined employee or revenue threshold may use simplified, abbreviated formats that still capture core VOC and solvent data. The agency should provide free, pre-formatted recordkeeping templates and a dedicated small business compliance helpline so that shops like ours are not forced to pay consultants simply to understand what records to keep and how. Furthermore, EPA should convene a small commercial printing sector working group—including representatives from shops like ours—before finalizing the standardized format, to ensure data fields reflect actual operational variability rather than assumptions derived from large, continuous-process manufacturers. Flexible 'variable formulation' fields should be built into the standardized format that allow facilities to log solvent and ink changes dynamically without triggering exception flags. The rule preamble should clarify that format non-conformance due to legitimate operational variability will not, by itself, constitute a compliance violation.

2.  **Extended Implementation Timelines for Ink Reformulation and Color Matching:**
    *   **New Implementation Timelines:** The EPA should extend the compliance window by a minimum of 24 months beyond the current proposed deadline to allow the ink supply chain to fully develop and distribute compliant formulations. For small printing operations with fewer than 25 employees, the implementation timeline should be extended to a minimum of 36 months, allowing time for ink suppliers to develop compliant formulations that still meet Pantone/spot-color matching standards. The agency should establish an affirmative defense or safe harbor provision for small entities that can demonstrate good-faith efforts to source compliant inks but are blocked by supplier availability. The agency should explicitly address the 'operational impacts of the proposed compliance requirements' by acknowledging that for downstream users like commercial printers, the operative constraint is supplier readiness, not operator readiness—and the timeline should be calibrated accordingly. The agency should also require ink and solvent suppliers above a certain production volume to provide standardized VOC composition data sheets in a format directly compatible with the new EPA submission portal, shifting part of the data burden upstream to larger, better-resourced entities. EPA must consult with printing industry suppliers before finalizing timelines to confirm that compliant ink alternatives capable of meeting commercial color matching standards are actually available in the market.

[CONCLUSION]
Kwik Print & Graphics urges the EPA to reconsider the uniform application of its proposed revised reporting obligations, new implementation timelines, and updated documentation standards to small commercial printing operations. Without significant adjustments, the proposal risks imposing unmanageable compliance costs and operational disruptions on businesses like ours, potentially threatening the viability of small printers across the country. We strongly recommend the adoption of tiered compliance pathways, sector-specific exemptions, extended timelines that align with supplier readiness and customer color matching requirements, and the provision of free compliance assistance resources. A regulatory framework that is flexible, practical, and responsive to the realities of small business operations will ultimately be more effective in achieving environmental goals while preserving economic vitality.

### References
[1] https://trdsf.com/blogs/news/epa-regulations-impact-on-small-firms?srsltid=AfmBOoqk9vcDHTxXRSubWewf5IuB5WWqnjgj1IbBAX_HcRLZdk8N5iSR
[2] https://www.flintgrp.com/news-and-events/news/how-your-ink-supplier-can-support-fcm-compliance/

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The American Public Health Association (APHA) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA) proposed framework to update compliance and oversight mechanisms, including revised reporting obligations, new implementation timelines, updated documentation standards, and requests for cost and operational impact analyses. As the nation's leading public health professional association, APHA represents a multidisciplinary membership spanning environmental health officers, epidemiologists, community health practitioners, and public health policy analysts. Our mission is to protect and improve the health of all people and all communities.

APHA strongly supports the EPA's efforts to modernize reporting and documentation standards. Consistent, high-quality environmental data is a foundational prerequisite for the evidence-based policy recommendations that APHA's multidisciplinary membership produces and relies upon. The proposed changes have the potential to significantly strengthen environmental public health surveillance, improve our ability to correlate environmental exposures with health outcomes, and advance health equity. However, to fully realize these benefits, APHA urges the EPA to ensure the new protocols are explicitly designed to capture health equity indicators alongside traditional compliance metrics and to provide robust technical assistance to support smaller regulated entities and state/tribal programs in meeting the new requirements.

[BACKGROUND]
The EPA's proposal introduces a comprehensive overhaul of how regulated entities interact with the agency, focusing on the modernization of reporting obligations and the standardization of documentation. Key provisions include:
*   Revised Reporting Obligations: New protocols for submitting data to the agency.
*   New Implementation Timelines: Specific windows for when entities must come into compliance.
*   Updated Documentation Standards: New, standardized formats for maintaining records.
*   Cost Assessment Requirement: Encouraging entities to provide data on "expected costs."
*   Operational Impact Analysis: Review of "operational impacts" on facilities.

APHA views these provisions as critical steps toward strengthening environmental public health surveillance and improving the data infrastructure that public health professionals depend on for evidence-based assessments and decision-making.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
1.  **Impact of Revised Reporting Obligations:** APHA's members rely directly on the quality and granularity of EPA-reported environmental data to conduct evidence-based assessments, develop professional standards, and advise policymakers. Current fragmented and inconsistent reporting formats create significant gaps in our members' ability to correlate environmental exposures with health outcomes—particularly in overburdened communities. For instance, EPA's own FY 2023-2024 National Water Guidance noted health-based violations linked to fragmented reporting [1]. Standardized, modernized submission protocols would directly improve the data infrastructure that environmental health professionals depend on for surveillance and decision-making. Additionally, APHA's multidisciplinary membership, including environmental health scientists and epidemiologists, relies on timely access to EPA compliance data to conduct peer-reviewed research and develop professional standards. The current fragmented reporting landscape, as reflected in EPA's own Office of Air and Radiation (OAR) strategic planning documents [6], [7], creates gaps in data accessibility that undermine our members' ability to link emissions data to health outcomes at the community level.

2.  **Impact of Updated Documentation Standards:** APHA's environmental health professionals routinely synthesize compliance and emissions data from multiple regulated entities to develop policy recommendations on air quality, environmental justice, and community health outcomes. Currently, inconsistent recordkeeping formats across regulated entities force our researchers and policy analysts to spend significant resources on data cleaning and harmonization before any substantive analysis can begin. This inefficiency delays the publication of evidence-based recommendations and introduces methodological uncertainty that weakens the credibility of our policy positions. Standardized formats, as proposed, would directly reduce this burden and improve the reliability of the data inputs underlying our recommendations [2], [3]. APHA's ability to fulfill its health equity mandate is materially constrained when underlying compliance data is inconsistent or incomparable across regulated entities [2], [3].

3.  **Impact of New Implementation Timelines and Operational Costs:** APHA's membership includes environmental health professionals working within state and local health departments, tribal programs, and academic institutions that frequently serve as technical advisors to regulated entities or directly submit data on behalf of smaller jurisdictions. If the new protocols impose high operational costs without corresponding support infrastructure, our members report—consistent with needs assessments by groups like NEHA—that under-resourced programs will struggle to comply, resulting in incomplete or low-quality data submissions that undermine the very surveillance improvements the rule seeks to achieve. This directly addresses the agency's question regarding operational impacts of the proposed compliance requirements [1]. The operational costs of compliance—while real—are substantially outweighed by the public health benefits of improved data integrity, particularly for communities disproportionately burdened by environmental pollution. The framing of compliance costs must account for the far greater societal costs of inadequate environmental monitoring data in underserved communities [2], [3].

4.  **Impact of Cost Assessment Requirement:** As a multidisciplinary professional membership organization representing epidemiologists, environmental health scientists, health educators, and community health workers, APHA's members routinely engage in cost-benefit analyses of environmental regulations and advise public health agencies on implementation feasibility. Without disaggregated cost data, APHA's members cannot meaningfully evaluate whether compliance timelines are equitable across regulated entities operating in environmental justice communities versus those in higher-resourced areas. The agency's own FY 2024 budget explicitly prioritized incorporating environmental justice considerations into compliance monitoring, allocating $165 million to the Compliance Monitoring Program—a $50.9 million increase over FY 2023—yet this investment is undermined if the cost data collected does not allow the agency to assess distributional impacts [4], [5].

5.  **Addressing Alternatives to Compliance Requirements:** In direct response to the agency's question on alternatives, APHA asserts that voluntary or self-selected reporting formats are not a viable alternative, as they have demonstrably failed to produce the data consistency needed for evidence-based public health policy. When compliance records exist in incompatible formats, linking environmental compliance data with health outcome data to establish exposure-response relationships is technically infeasible or methodologically compromised. Tennessee's experience with standardized data sharing frameworks demonstrates that templatized, governance-backed data systems enable rapid expansion of data integration across agencies—precisely the kind of cross-system linkage that APHA's evidence-based policy work requires between EPA compliance data and public health datasets [2], [3].

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To maximize the public health utility and ensure equitable implementation of the proposed compliance and oversight mechanisms, APHA offers the following recommendations:

1.  **For Revised Reporting Obligations:**
    *   The agency should explicitly require that new data submission protocols include standardized fields for demographic and geographic disaggregation—such as census tract-level reporting and proximity-to-sensitive-population flags—so that submitted data can be cross-referenced with environmental justice indices like EPA's own Environmental Quality Index (EQI). This aligns with the White House's Final EJ Science, Data, and Research Plan and EPA's stated commitment to integrating justice considerations into programmatic work [1].
    *   EPA should expand the proposed data submission standards to require regulated entities to report not only emissions quantities but also co-pollutant profiles and proximity-to-population metrics, enabling environmental health professionals to more readily conduct health impact assessments [6], [7].
    *   APHA recommends the agency publish a data schema that is interoperable with existing public health surveillance systems to maximize utility for the professional community [1]. EPA should also establish a publicly accessible, machine-readable data portal—building on frameworks like the Combined Air Emissions Reporting System (CAERS)—so that APHA members and other public health researchers can access standardized data without additional FOIA requests or data cleaning burdens [6], [7].

2.  **For Updated Documentation Standards:**
    *   The EPA should adopt standardized formats that align with existing federal data governance frameworks, specifically the Evidence-Based Policymaking Act of 2018 and the Information Quality Act of 2000, to ensure interoperability with other federal environmental and public health datasets [2], [3].
    *   APHA recommends that the agency explicitly require that standardized records be made publicly accessible in open digital formats (e.g., JSON, CSV with standardized data dictionaries, or HL7 FHIR-compatible schemas where health data is involved), consistent with NIH and NSF scientific integrity policies that promote free public access to government-generated scientific information [2], [3]. This approach reduces long-term operational burden on both regulated entities and data users while maximizing public health value [1].

3.  **For New Implementation Timelines and Operational Costs:**
    *   Responding directly to the agency's question on operational impacts and costs, EPA should commit to a phased implementation timeline of no less than 18 months, paired with a publicly accessible resource portal modeled on successful federal data submission portals—which included improved tools, templates, and guidance materials. The agency should also establish a dedicated technical assistance program for small and under-resourced entities, including tribal governments, consistent with EPA's FY2024 commitment to better support tribes and states through data system modernization [1]. APHA further recommends the agency conduct a post-implementation review at 12 months to assess whether compliance rates differ systematically by entity size or resource level [1].
    *   EPA should conduct a distributional analysis of compliance costs that distinguishes between large industrial entities and smaller regulated parties, and consider tiered implementation timelines that reduce burden on smaller entities without compromising data standardization goals. Additionally, EPA should establish a technical assistance program—modeled on CDC's Environmental Health Data Modernization initiative—to help regulated entities, particularly those in or near environmental justice communities, transition to standardized formats efficiently [2], [3].

4.  **For Cost Assessment Requirement:**
    *   The agency should revise its cost data solicitation to require that submissions include: (1) cost estimates broken down by entity size and type; (2) identification of whether the regulated facility is located in or adjacent to an environmental justice community as defined by EPA's EJScreen tool; (3) projected health outcome costs avoided as a result of compliance, not merely administrative transition costs. This approach aligns with the scientific integrity standards articulated in EPA's 2024 Scientific Integrity Policy, which calls for analyses that meet standards for objectivity and accuracy consistent with the Information Quality Act [4], [5].
    *   APHA recommends the agency issue a structured cost-reporting template rather than accepting unstructured narrative submissions, which will improve comparability and reduce the risk that well-resourced entities dominate the evidentiary record [4], [5].

5.  **Addressing Alternatives to Compliance Requirements:**
    *   In direct response to the agency's question on alternatives, APHA urges EPA to reject voluntary or flexible-format alternatives as insufficient substitutes for mandatory standardization [2], [3].
    *   EPA should adopt standardized formats that are explicitly designed for interoperability with public health data systems, including CDC's National Environmental Public Health Tracking Network [2], [3].
    *   APHA further recommends that EPA convene a technical working group that includes public health professionals, epidemiologists, and environmental justice advocates—in addition to regulated industry representatives—to finalize the specific data fields and formats, ensuring that the standardized records capture variables of direct relevance to health impact assessment, not merely regulatory compliance verification [2], [3].

[CONCLUSION]
The modernization of EPA's compliance and oversight mechanisms, including revised reporting obligations and standardized documentation, represents a critical opportunity to strengthen environmental public health surveillance and advance health equity. APHA strongly supports the proposed framework and urges the EPA to adopt our recommendations, particularly those focused on incorporating health equity indicators, ensuring interoperability with public health data systems, and providing robust technical assistance. By implementing these changes, EPA can transform compliance records from a regulatory compliance tool into a proactive environmental public health monitoring asset, ultimately leading to more effective, evidence-based policies that protect the health of all communities.

### References
[1] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2022-08/fy-2023-2024-ow-npg.pdf
[2] https://peer.org/wp-content/uploads/2024/10/1_24_NSF_SIP.pdf
[3] https://bidenwhitehouse.archives.gov/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/Biennial_Report-on-the_-mplementation-Status-of-Scientific-Integrity-Policy-and-Practice.pdf
[4] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2023-03/fy-2024-epa-bib.pdf
[5] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2025-01/castnet_modernization_plan_dec2024_final.pdf
[6] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2022-06/fy23-24-oar-draft-npg.pdf
[7] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2024-07/fy-2025-2026-oar-npg.pdf

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
Marathon Petroleum Corporation (Marathon) appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA) proposed regulatory framework to update compliance and oversight mechanisms (Docket ID: EPA-HQ-OAR-2025-0194). As one of the largest petroleum refiners in the United States, with 13 operating refineries across the country, as well as extensive midstream pipeline and terminal operations, Marathon is deeply committed to environmental stewardship, compliance, and ensuring the reliable supply of fuels that power the American economy.

While we support the underlying goals of improving regulatory efficiency and data integrity, Marathon strongly opposes the proposed rule as currently structured. Our analysis reveals that several key provisions, particularly the revised reporting obligations, new implementation timelines, and updated documentation standards, are fundamentally misaligned with the operational realities of petroleum refining. These provisions create irreconcilable conflicts with existing consent decree compliance schedules, fail to account for the mandatory turnaround cycles that govern refinery maintenance and upgrades, and pose significant risks to fuel supply reliability, ultimately undermining rather than enhancing environmental outcomes. Marathon urges EPA to adopt a more flexible and practical approach that recognizes the complex operational and legal landscapes in which refineries operate.

[BACKGROUND]
The proposed rule introduces a comprehensive overhaul of how regulated entities interact with the agency, focusing on the modernization of reporting obligations and the standardization of documentation. Key provisions include:
*   **Revised Reporting Obligations:** Regulated entities must adhere to new protocols for submitting data to the agency.
*   **New Implementation Timelines:** The proposal establishes specific windows for when entities must come into compliance with the new rules.
*   **Updated Documentation Standards:** Entities are required to maintain records according to new, standardized formats to ensure data integrity.
*   **Cost Assessment Requirement:** Entities are encouraged to provide data on "expected costs" associated with the transition.
*   **Operational Impact Analysis:** The proposal requires a review of how these changes will affect "operational impacts."

Marathon operates under active federal consent decrees at several of our refineries, which specify precise data submission formats, timelines, and protocols negotiated with EPA and the Department of Justice over many years. Our refineries also operate on staggered, multi-year turnaround schedules, which are the only practical windows for major equipment modifications, systems upgrades, and infrastructure-level changes. These two fundamental aspects of refinery operations — legally binding consent decrees and mandatory turnaround cycles — are in direct conflict with the inflexible provisions of the proposed rule, leading to significant operational burdens, compliance costs, and potential threats to fuel supply reliability.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
**1. Irreconcilable Conflicts with Existing Consent Decree Compliance Schedules**
Marathon Petroleum Corporation opposes the proposed revised reporting obligations as structured because they create irreconcilable conflicts with our existing consent decree compliance schedules, effectively forcing us to simultaneously satisfy two incompatible federal reporting regimes without adequate transition time or legal clarity on which obligation takes precedence.

Marathon operates under active consent decrees that specify precise data submission formats, timelines, and protocols negotiated with EPA and the Department of Justice. Our 13 refineries each have individualized consent decree reporting obligations — covering NOx, SO2, PM, and other criteria pollutants — that were negotiated over years of enforcement proceedings. The proposed new reporting protocols would require us to reformat, resubmit, or duplicate data streams that are already being submitted under legally binding consent decree terms. If we modify our reporting systems to comply with the new rule, we risk being found in technical violation of our consent decrees. If we maintain consent decree compliance, we may be found non-compliant with the new rule. This is not a hypothetical conflict: EPA's own Air Emissions Reporting Requirements (AERR) framework, as reflected in Docket EPA-HQ-OAR-2023-0614, imposes standardized data submission protocols that were not designed with consent decree-specific carve-outs in mind [1, 2]. The agency has not addressed this conflict in the proposed rule. This operational burden and compliance cost are significant, as violating consent decrees can lead to substantial penalties and further enforcement actions, while attempting to comply with both simultaneously would require redundant systems and data processing.

**2. Misalignment with Refinery Turnaround Schedules for New Implementation Timelines**
Marathon Petroleum Corporation opposes the proposed implementation timelines as structured because they fail to account for the immovable, multi-year scheduling constraints inherent to refinery turnaround cycles, making compliance within the proposed windows operationally impossible for a significant portion of our 13-refinery system at any given time.

Marathon's refineries operate on staggered turnaround schedules occurring every 4-5 years, during which major equipment modifications, inspections, and system upgrades are physically executed. These windows—typically lasting 30-60 days and planned 18-36 months in advance—represent the only feasible opportunity to implement infrastructure-level changes required by new reporting and documentation standards [3]. At any given compliance deadline, a substantial subset of our refineries will be mid-cycle, meaning they cannot safely or economically implement required physical or systems modifications until their next scheduled turnaround. Forcing out-of-cycle compliance would require either unplanned shutdowns (costing tens of millions of dollars in lost throughput and contractor mobilization) or operating in technical non-compliance, neither of which is acceptable. As publicly reported, Marathon expected above-average turnaround activity in Q1 2024 alone, illustrating how turnaround scheduling is a continuous, system-wide constraint—not an isolated event that can be rescheduled around regulatory deadlines. This operational burden directly impacts our ability to meet the proposed compliance requirements without significant disruption to fuel production.

**3. Operational Impacts of Implementation Timeline for Revised Reporting Obligations (IT Systems)**
Marathon Petroleum opposes the proposed implementation timeline for the revised reporting obligations because it fails to account for the operational realities of refinery turnaround scheduling, which governs when IT system upgrades and data infrastructure changes can be safely and practically implemented at our facilities — directly responding to the agency's question about operational impacts of the proposed compliance requirements.

Marathon's 13 refineries operate on 4-to-5-year turnaround cycles during which major equipment, instrumentation, and monitoring system upgrades are performed. Outside of these scheduled turnarounds, modifications to emissions monitoring hardware, data acquisition systems (DAHS), and reporting infrastructure carry significant safety, operational continuity, and cost risks. The proposed rule's implementation timeline does not align with these turnaround windows. For example, a refinery that completed its turnaround in 2023 will not have another scheduled opportunity for major systems integration until 2027 or 2028. Forcing that facility to implement new data submission protocols and potentially new monitoring instrumentation on an arbitrary regulatory deadline — rather than during a planned turnaround — would require either unsafe mid-cycle system modifications or the construction of parallel, redundant reporting systems at substantial cost. EPA's own AERR guidance framework, as reflected in evidence [1], acknowledges multi-year implementation cycles for data system transitions (e.g., five-year cycle year submissions), yet the proposed rule does not extend comparable flexibility to refinery operators. This operational burden and compliance cost are directly linked to the inflexible implementation timeline.

**4. Operational Impacts of Implementation Timeline for Updated Documentation Standards**
Marathon Petroleum opposes the proposed implementation timeline for the Updated Documentation Standards because it fails to account for the mandatory refinery turnaround scheduling cycle that governs when major operational and systems changes can safely and practically be implemented at petroleum refineries, creating an unintended consequence of forcing simultaneous compliance investments during periods of reduced operational capacity.

Each of Marathon's 13 refineries undergoes a full turnaround—a planned shutdown for inspection, maintenance, and capital upgrades—on a 4-to-5-year cycle. These turnarounds are the only practical windows during which major changes to process control systems, data historians, and integrated compliance recordkeeping platforms can be implemented without disrupting fuel production or creating safety risks. The proposed documentation standards would require system-wide transitions on a timeline that does not align with these turnaround schedules, meaning Marathon would be compelled to either: (a) implement documentation system changes during active refinery operations, introducing data integrity risks and operational disruptions that are antithetical to the rule's stated goal of improving data integrity; or (b) incur the cost of accelerated turnarounds outside of our planned maintenance cycle, which industry data and our operational experience confirm can cost $50–$150 million per facility depending on scope [4]. This is not a hypothetical concern—it is a structural feature of petroleum refining operations that EPA has recognized in prior rulemakings [5]. Furthermore, Marathon's existing consent decree compliance obligations impose additional scheduling constraints that cannot be unilaterally modified to accommodate a new documentation standard timeline. This operational burden and compliance cost are a direct consequence of the proposed timeline.

**5. Threat to Fuel Supply Reliability**
The collective operational disruptions, unplanned shutdowns, and increased turnaround costs resulting from the unaligned compliance timelines (Impacts 2, 3, 4) directly threaten fuel supply reliability. Refineries are critical infrastructure, and prolonged or unplanned outages reduce gasoline, diesel, and jet fuel production, impacting consumers, transportation, and national security. The proposal's failure to account for refinery operational realities creates unnecessary risk to the nation's energy supply. This is an indirect but critical operational impact that must be considered.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
In light of the significant operational burdens, compliance costs, and threats to fuel supply reliability identified above, Marathon Petroleum Corporation respectfully submits the following recommendations for EPA’s consideration in developing the final rule:

**1. Establish a Consent Decree Safe Harbor Provision for Revised Reporting Obligations:**
*   **Specific Provision:** Revised Reporting Obligations.
*   **Recommended Change:** EPA must explicitly include a consent decree safe harbor provision in the final rule, stating that regulated entities operating under active federal consent decrees are deemed compliant with the new reporting protocols to the extent their consent decree obligations govern the same data streams. Additionally, EPA should establish a formal coordination mechanism between the Office of Air and Radiation and the Office of Enforcement and Compliance Assurance (OECA) to reconcile new reporting mandates with existing consent decree terms before the rule takes effect. No compliance deadline under the new rule should apply to consent decree-covered facilities until EPA and DOJ have jointly confirmed that the new protocols are consistent with — or have formally modified — the applicable consent decree terms.
*   **Justification:** This recommendation directly addresses Impact 1, resolving the irreconcilable conflict between the proposed revised reporting obligations and existing consent decree compliance schedules, thereby preventing technical violations and aligning federal regulatory requirements [1, 2].

**2. Implement a Turnaround-Linked Compliance Schedule for Implementation Timelines and Documentation Standards:**
*   **Specific Provisions:** New Implementation Timelines, Updated Documentation Standards, Cost Assessment Requirement, Operational Impact Analysis.
*   **Recommended Change:** The agency should replace the single fixed compliance window with a facility-specific compliance election mechanism that allows each regulated refinery to designate its next scheduled turnaround date as its compliance deadline, provided that date falls within a reasonable outer bound (e.g., no later than 60 months from the rule's effective date). EPA should require entities to submit a Turnaround-Linked Compliance Schedule within 90 days of the final rule's publication, documenting planned turnaround dates and the specific compliance actions to be completed during each event. Interim compliance measures — such as parallel reporting under both the old and new protocols — should be accepted during this transition window. Additionally, EPA should explicitly recognize existing consent decree compliance schedules as a factor in granting compliance deadline extensions, so that facilities are not forced to choose between consent decree obligations and new documentation standard deadlines.
*   **Justification:** This recommendation directly addresses Impacts 2, 3, and 4, aligning compliance schedules with the immovable operational realities of refinery turnaround cycles. This approach avoids unplanned shutdowns, significant costs (tens of millions to $150 million per facility) [3, 4], ensures safety and operational continuity, and mirrors flexibility EPA has historically extended in other air rules (e.g., AERR guidance [1]).

**3. Conduct a Formal Fuel Supply Reliability Assessment and Provide Compliance Flexibilities:**
*   **Specific Provisions:** Operational Impact Analysis, Cost Assessment Requirement.
*   **Recommended Change:** EPA should include a formal fuel supply reliability assessment as a mandatory component of the final rule's Regulatory Impact Analysis (RIA). Furthermore, the agency should implement a mechanism for compliance deadline extensions for refineries facing imminent operational disruptions that would significantly impact regional or national fuel supply due to the proposed compliance requirements.
*   **Justification:** This recommendation directly addresses Impact 5, mitigating the risk of operational disruptions and unplanned outages that could severely impact consumers, transportation, and national security. A formal assessment would provide critical data to inform compliance flexibility provisions.

[CONCLUSION]
Marathon Petroleum Corporation reiterates its strong opposition to the proposed rule as currently structured. While we share EPA’s commitment to environmental protection and regulatory compliance, the proposed revised reporting obligations, new implementation timelines, and updated documentation standards fail to account for the fundamental operational realities and legal obligations of the petroleum refining industry. These provisions create unnecessary conflicts with existing consent decrees, impose infeasible compliance timelines that clash with essential refinery turnaround cycles, and ultimately threaten the reliable supply of fuels critical to the nation's economy and security.

Marathon urges EPA to adopt the recommended changes to ensure that the final rule is practical, effective, and achieves its environmental objectives without imposing undue operational burdens, excessive costs, or creating risks to America's energy infrastructure. We stand ready to engage further with the agency to develop a final rule that is both environmentally protective and operationally feasible.

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The Pennsylvania Department of Environmental Protection (PA DEP) submits these comments in response to the Environmental Protection Agency's (EPA) proposed regulatory framework, docket EPA-HQ-OAR-2025-0194, which seeks to modernize compliance and oversight mechanisms. PA DEP is committed to enhancing environmental protection through effective data management and reporting, but we are concerned that the current proposal does not adequately account for the operational realities and existing regulatory frameworks within states like Pennsylvania. We urge EPA to consider the significant administrative burdens and costs that these changes will impose on state co-regulators and to provide the necessary federal resources and flexibility to ensure a successful transition without compromising ongoing environmental protection efforts.

[BACKGROUND]
EPA's proposal introduces a comprehensive overhaul of how regulated entities interact with the agency, focusing on the modernization of reporting obligations and the standardization of documentation. Key provisions include revised reporting obligations, new implementation timelines, updated documentation standards, and a requirement for cost and operational impact assessments. While PA DEP supports the goal of improved data integrity and compliance monitoring, we are concerned that the proposed implementation timelines and uniform standards fail to recognize the existing, complex environmental regulatory landscape at the state level, particularly in areas like oil and gas permitting, air quality oversight, and abandoned mine drainage remediation.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
PA DEP anticipates several significant operational and compliance impacts that require EPA's direct attention and resource allocation:

**1. Operational Burden on Oil and Gas Well Site Inspections and Permitting:**
The proposed revised reporting obligations and new implementation timelines will create an untenable operational burden on PA DEP's oil and gas well permitting and inspection program, particularly given existing staff limitations across our three district offices serving one of the nation's most active shale gas regions. PA DEP's Bureau of District Oil and Gas Operations currently manages a high-volume permitting workload across the Marcellus and Utica shale formations [2]. The proposed compliance windows would require simultaneous updates to our internal permitting documentation standards, inspector training protocols, and data reporting systems—all within a compressed federal timeline that does not account for Pennsylvania's own administrative rulemaking requirements under the Commonwealth Documents Law.

If federal compliance deadlines are triggered before PA DEP can update its state-level permitting forms and inspection checklists to align with the new federal documentation standards, we face a period of regulatory misalignment in which operators may be simultaneously compliant with state permits but non-compliant with federal requirements, or vice versa. This creates enforcement ambiguity that undermines both programs. PA DEP's 2023 Oil and Gas Annual Report documents the complexity of our three-district operational structure, which requires coordinated rollout of any new procedural requirements—a process that itself takes months. In direct response to EPA's question regarding the expected costs of the proposed compliance requirements, PA DEP will face real, quantifiable costs to comply, including: (1) reprogramming or replacing legacy data management systems used for well permit tracking and inspection records; (2) retraining inspection staff across three district offices on new reporting formats and documentation protocols; and (3) potential need for additional legal and technical staff to manage the transition period during which dual state-federal documentation standards may be in effect [3].

**2. Duplicative Reporting and Administrative Costs for Air Quality Methane Regulation:**
The proposed new data submission protocols impose implementation timelines that are incompatible with our existing air quality permitting and inspection infrastructure in the shale gas region, and the agency has underestimated the operational burden these changes will place on state co-regulators who must simultaneously administer their own parallel reporting systems. PA DEP's air quality program in the shale gas region currently manages hundreds of active permits for unconventional well operations, compressor stations, and processing facilities [1]. Our inspectors and permit writers rely on established data workflows that interface with Pennsylvania's own eDMR and e-permitting systems. The proposed new federal data submission protocols would require PA DEP to either (1) build costly middleware to translate between state and federal data formats, or (2) require regulated entities to submit duplicative reports in two separate formats—one to EPA and one to PA DEP—dramatically increasing the compliance burden on operators and the administrative burden on our already inspection-staff-limited agency [1, 8].

EPA's own Regulatory Impact Analysis for related New Source Performance Standards (NSPS) rules acknowledges annualized compliance costs in the tens of millions of dollars beginning in 2027–2028 [8], yet the proposed rule does not appear to account for the cascading administrative costs imposed on state environmental agencies that serve as the primary day-to-day compliance interface with regulated facilities. Furthermore, PA DEP requires major point sources—including natural gas production, processing, and transmission facilities across the shale region—to report air emissions information annually, and prepares a triennial emissions inventory under 40 CFR Part 51, Subpart A [1]. If the new federal protocols are not designed with this heterogeneous operator landscape in mind, DEP anticipates a significant increase in deficient or non-conforming submissions that our inspection and data review staff will be required to remediate. This creates a perverse outcome: a modernization effort intended to improve data quality could temporarily degrade it during the transition period.

**3. Disproportionate Compliance Costs for Abandoned Mine Drainage (AMD) Remediation Programs:**
The proposed standardized recordkeeping format requirements impose disproportionate compliance costs on state environmental agencies administering abandoned mine drainage remediation programs, where existing data systems are deeply integrated with multi-partner grant structures, watershed-level monitoring networks, and legacy federal funding frameworks that do not align with the proposed uniform format. PA DEP administers AMD remediation across hundreds of legacy coal mining sites using data collected through Growing Greener Watershed Restoration grants, SMCRA Bond Forfeiture programs, and OSMRE Abandoned Mine Land Reclamation (AMLR) funding streams—each with distinct reporting architectures [4, 5].

Transitioning these interconnected systems to a new standardized EPA format would require DEP to simultaneously remap data fields across multiple active grant programs, retrain field inspection staff already stretched thin across oil and gas well sites and AMD treatment facilities, and potentially re-enter years of historical baseline water quality data [4]. The complexity of data provenance that a single standardized format cannot easily accommodate without significant resource investment would divert limited DEP resources away from actual remediation work—including the treatment of acid mine drainage affecting Pennsylvania's impaired waterways—toward administrative reformatting exercises that serve no additional environmental protection purpose [5]. PA DEP already submits AMD remediation data to OSMRE under the Abandoned Mine Land Reclamation program and maintains records consistent with SMCRA requirements [5]. Requiring DEP to additionally reformat this same data to meet a new EPA-specific standard creates duplicative compliance burdens without producing meaningful improvements in data quality or agency oversight capacity.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
In light of these concerns, PA DEP offers the following specific recommendations to EPA:

**1. Provide Federal Resources for Well Site Inspections:**
*   **Recommendation:** Extend the proposed implementation timeline for state co-regulators—specifically regarding revised reporting obligations and new implementation timelines for oil and gas—by a minimum of 24 months to allow state agencies to align their own data systems with the new federal protocols.
*   **Justification:** This extension is necessary to account for the significant operational burden on PA DEP's oil and gas permitting and inspection program, including reprogramming legacy data management systems, retraining inspection staff across three district offices, and managing a high-volume permitting workload across the Marcellus and Utica shale formations [2, 3].
*   **Recommendation:** Provide dedicated technical assistance funding and IT integration support to state agencies, not just regulated industry, to facilitate the transition. EPA should establish a dedicated technical assistance and funding mechanism—potentially through existing EPA state program grants—to help state agencies defray the costs of systems and procedural updates required by the new documentation standards.
*   **Justification:** EPA's current cost analysis appears to focus primarily on costs to regulated entities rather than to co-regulatory state agencies. PA DEP will incur real, quantifiable costs for systems modernization, which EPA should explicitly quantify in its regulatory impact analysis [3].

**2. Recognize Pennsylvania's Existing Methane Rules:**
*   **Recommendation:** Establish a formal interoperability standard that allows a single operator submission to satisfy both state and federal air quality reporting obligations related to methane emissions, eliminating duplicative reporting. This standard should explicitly recognize compliance with Pennsylvania's existing methane rules and reporting protocols as satisfying federal requirements.
*   **Justification:** PA DEP's air quality program currently manages hundreds of active permits for unconventional well operations and requires annual air emissions reporting from major point sources in the shale gas region [1]. New federal protocols would require PA DEP to either build costly middleware or force regulated entities to submit duplicative reports, dramatically increasing administrative burden on our inspection-staff-limited agency [1, 8].
*   **Recommendation:** EPA should conduct a sector-specific feasibility assessment for the oil and natural gas source category before finalizing protocol requirements, with particular attention to small and mid-size operators in shale-producing states. As an alternative to uniform compliance, PA DEP recommends a phased, tiered approach in which major point sources transition first under a pilot program, with lessons learned informing protocol adjustments before smaller operators are required to comply [1].
*   **Justification:** The shale gas sector involves a large number of smaller, geographically dispersed operators alongside major point sources, each with varying technical capacity to implement new submission protocols. A phased approach would help prevent an increase in deficient or non-conforming submissions during the transition period [1].

**3. Provide Legacy Pollution Remediation Funding:**
*   **Recommendation:** Conduct a formal cost assessment specifically for state environmental agencies administering multi-source AMD remediation programs before finalizing the standardized format requirements related to updated documentation standards.
*   **Justification:** Transitioning PA DEP's interconnected AMD data systems (Growing Greener, SMCRA, OSMRE AMLR) to a new standardized EPA format would require significant resource investment, including remapping data fields, retraining field staff, and potentially re-entering historical data [4, 5]. EPA's cost assessment should explicitly address these state-level costs.
*   **Recommendation:** Explicitly exempt or provide extended transition timelines for AMD data systems already compliant with OSMRE and SMCRA federal reporting standards. As an alternative to mandating a single uniform format, EPA should establish a formal interoperability standard that recognizes OSMRE AMLR reporting, SMCRA bond forfeiture documentation, and state-administered Growing Greener grant records as equivalent to the proposed standardized format [4, 5].
*   **Justification:** PA DEP already submits AMD remediation data to OSMRE and maintains records consistent with SMCRA requirements. Requiring DEP to additionally reformat this same data creates duplicative compliance burdens without improving data quality or oversight capacity, diverting limited resources from actual remediation work [5].
*   **Recommendation:** Provide dedicated federal funding or technical assistance to offset format conversion costs for state agencies administering AMD remediation programs.
*   **Justification:** This funding is essential to prevent disproportionate compliance costs and ensure that valuable state resources are not diverted from critical remediation activities toward administrative reformatting exercises [5].

[CONCLUSION]
PA DEP supports the modernization of compliance and oversight mechanisms to enhance environmental protection. However, we urge EPA to adopt a more realistic and collaborative approach that recognizes the complex operational realities and existing regulatory frameworks at the state level. Providing dedicated federal resources for well site inspections, recognizing Pennsylvania's existing methane rules and reporting protocols, and allocating specific funding for legacy pollution remediation programs like AMD will be critical to ensuring a successful and equitable transition. These measures will prevent undue administrative burdens on state co-regulators, eliminate duplicative reporting, and ultimately strengthen our collective ability to protect public health and the environment.

**Subject: Comments on EPA Proposed Regulatory Framework: Updating Compliance and Oversight Mechanisms (Docket ID: EPA-HQ-OAR-2025-0194)**

[INTRODUCTION]
My name is James Hartford, and I am a cattle rancher operating a 500-head beef cattle operation in rural Montana. I am writing to express my strong opposition to the Environmental Protection Agency's (EPA) proposed regulatory framework, specifically the new reporting obligations, documentation standards, and implementation timelines, which I understand include methane reporting requirements. These provisions, as currently drafted, pose an unacceptable and disproportionate burden on small, family-run agricultural operations like mine.

[BACKGROUND]
The EPA, through the Office of Air and Radiation, is proposing to update compliance and oversight mechanisms. The proposal introduces a comprehensive overhaul of how regulated entities interact with the agency, focusing specifically on the modernization of reporting obligations and the standardization of documentation. Key provisions include revised reporting obligations, new implementation timelines, updated documentation standards, and requirements for regulated entities to provide data on "expected costs" and "operational impacts." While I understand the agency's stated goal to improve monitoring and ensure data integrity, the proposed approach fails to account for the unique operational realities and financial vulnerabilities of the livestock sector.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
As a cattle rancher, I am deeply concerned about the practical implications of these proposed requirements on my daily operations and the financial viability of my ranch:

*   **Disproportionate Compliance Costs for Small Operations:** Running 500 head of cattle means I am already tracking daily feeding, health treatments, movement, and grazing rotation records, but in formats that work for my operation and my accountant. Transitioning to a new, standardized EPA format would require me to either purchase new record-keeping software, hire outside administrative help, or spend hours each day reformatting existing data. Conservative estimates for small agricultural operations suggest compliance software and training alone can run $3,000–$8,000 in upfront costs, with ongoing annual costs for subscriptions and updates [1]. On a 500-head operation with narrow profit margins — margins that have been further compressed by drought conditions that have driven the national cattle inventory down from 94.7 million head in 2019 to just 86.7 million head in January 2025 [2] — these costs are not trivial. They represent a meaningful percentage of net operating income and could push operations like mine into the red. The EPA's own Updated Air Emissions Reporting Rule (AERR), proposed in August 2023, was acknowledged to impose significant transition costs on regulated entities, yet the agency's cost modeling has historically been calibrated to industrial-scale facilities, not family-scale agricultural operations [4].

*   **Operational Burden Due to Seasonal Agricultural Cycles:** On a 500-head Montana cattle operation, there are periods — particularly calving season in late winter and early spring (February–April), spring turnout and grazing (May–June), and fall shipping (September–October) — when administrative record-keeping in any standardized format is simply not feasible on a daily basis [8]. My current system allows me to batch-record during these periods and reconcile within a reasonable window. A rigid daily standardized format requirement with no grace period or seasonal adjustment would mean I am technically out of compliance during the most operationally demanding and financially critical weeks of the year. Every available hand during calving season is working 24-hour rotations monitoring heifers, pulling calves, and managing newborn health. During fall shipping, my entire labor force is dedicated to sorting, weighing, loading, and transporting cattle to market — the single largest revenue event of my year. If the proposed compliance windows overlap with either of these periods, I would be forced to divert labor from critical animal husbandry tasks to documentation and reporting activities. A single lost calf during calving represents $800–$1,200 in lost revenue. Diverting even one worker for a full day of compliance paperwork during peak calving could cost me multiple animals. The national cattle herd's documented decline to 86.7 million head (from 94.7 million in 2019) reflects exactly the kind of climate and drought stress that creates these emergency operational periods [2].

*   **Lack of Rural Broadband and Digital Divide:** My ranch operates in an area with limited or no reliable broadband connectivity. If the EPA's standardized format is designed around electronic submission or digital record-keeping systems — as the agency's stated goal of 'modernization' implies — I have no practical way to comply without driving to town to upload records, which is not a realistic daily operational burden [1, 7]. The proposed electronic submission mandate would require me to either invest in expensive satellite internet service — a cost I cannot absorb given the narrow profit margins inherent in cattle ranching — or drive hours to a location with reliable connectivity to meet filing deadlines. Even if paper-based compliance is technically permitted, standardized formats designed for digital systems often translate poorly to handwritten or typewritten records, creating ambiguity about whether my records will be deemed compliant during an inspection. A missed deadline due to a connectivity outage could expose me to enforcement action through no fault of my own [3].

*   **Unrealistic Expectations for Small Operators:** As a cattle rancher operating a 500-head operation in rural Montana, I strongly oppose the proposed compliance framework as written because the new emissions monitoring and documentation requirements impose disproportionate operational burdens on small and mid-sized agricultural producers who lack the administrative infrastructure, technical staff, and reliable connectivity to meet standardized reporting timelines designed for large industrial facilities. I do not employ a compliance officer or environmental consultant. The proposed requirements would require me to either hire outside expertise I cannot afford or divert significant time away from daily animal husbandry, feeding, and herd health management — tasks that cannot be deferred [6].

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
To address these critical concerns and ensure that any final rule is both effective and fair to the agricultural community, I urge the EPA to consider the following specific recommendations:

*   **Tiered Compliance and Small Agricultural Exemptions:** The EPA should conduct a formal Small Business Regulatory Enforcement Fairness Act (SBREFA) analysis specifically for cattle operations under 1,000 head and publish the results for public comment. The agency should also establish a tiered compliance cost threshold, exempting or providing simplified alternative formats for operations below a defined revenue or herd-size threshold. At minimum, the agency should provide free, government-supplied record-keeping templates that require no software purchase and are compatible with paper-based systems. Specifically, the agency should disaggregate its compliance cost estimates by operation size and sector, specifically isolating costs for agricultural operations with fewer than 1,000 animal units, and consider a simplified, annual aggregate reporting option rather than frequent submission intervals. The agency should explicitly exempt or provide a tiered compliance pathway for operations under a defined animal unit threshold (e.g., fewer than 1,000 head), consistent with how EPA has historically distinguished large concentrated animal feeding operations (CAFOs) from smaller operations.

*   **Seasonal Adjustment and Emergency Waivers for Implementation Timelines:** The EPA should build an explicit seasonal adjustment provision into the final rule, allowing livestock operations to designate up to 60 days per calendar year as 'operational peak periods' during which a simplified or batch-reconciliation record-keeping standard applies, with full standardized records to be completed within 14 days of the peak period's end. The agency should also create a formal drought/emergency waiver process, coordinated with USDA's existing Emergency Livestock Relief Program (ELRP) designations, so that ranchers already certified as experiencing drought emergencies are automatically granted compliance flexibility without having to file separate EPA waiver requests [2].

*   **Paper-Based and Mail-In Reporting Alternatives:** The EPA must explicitly design and publish a paper-based compliance pathway that is fully equivalent to any digital pathway, with no penalty or additional scrutiny applied to paper filers. The agency should also survey the broadband availability of its regulated agricultural community before finalizing any digitally-oriented standardized format, and should coordinate with USDA's existing rural broadband mapping data. Implementation timelines should not begin running for operations in USDA-designated rural areas until broadband access is confirmed available in those areas. Specifically, the agency should provide a permanent paper-based or mail-in alternative for operations located in counties designated as underserved for broadband by the FCC or USDA, establish a 'connectivity hardship' exemption, and extend submission deadlines by at least 30 additional days for rural agricultural operations [1, 3, 7].

*   **Extended Implementation Timelines and Technical Assistance:** Implementation timelines should be extended by no fewer than 36 months for small agricultural operations to allow time for affordable compliance tools and technical assistance programs to be developed, and EPA should partner with USDA extension services to provide on-farm technical assistance [6].

[CONCLUSION]
In closing, I urge the EPA to recognize the severe and unique operational and financial impacts these proposed compliance requirements, including methane reporting, will have on small, independent cattle operations. Without significant modifications, including agricultural exemptions and tiered compliance pathways that account for the realities of working ranches, this rule will impose an unmanageable burden, threatening the livelihoods of countless family ranchers and the stability of the nation's food supply. I implore the agency to engage directly with agricultural producers and incorporate our feedback to develop a framework that is both effective and fair.

Sincerely,

James Hartford
Cattle Rancher
