comment_text:
{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
May 26, 2026

Environmental Protection Agency
Office of Air and Radiation
Docket ID: EPA-HQ-OAR-2025-0194
Regulations.gov

Re: Comments on Proposed Regulatory Framework to Update Compliance and Oversight Mechanisms (Docket ID: EPA-HQ-OAR-2025-0194)

Dear Environmental Protection Agency,

My name is Tom Jackson, and I am writing to you today as a corn and soybean farmer, a volunteer firefighter, and a dedicated member of my township board in rural Iowa. My family has farmed this land for generations, and I have served our community for decades, responding to emergencies and helping to govern our local affairs. I am deeply invested in the health and prosperity of rural America, and it is from this perspective that I must strongly oppose the proposed regulatory framework outlined in Docket ID: EPA-HQ-OAR-2025-0194.

While I understand and support the EPA’s mission to protect our environment, the specific provisions of this proposal – particularly the **Revised Reporting Obligations**, **New Implementation Timelines**, **Updated Documentation Standards**, **Cost Assessment Requirement**, and **Operational Impact Analysis** – fundamentally misunderstand and disproportionately burden the realities of life and work in rural communities. For small family farms like mine, and for volunteer emergency services that run on the dedication of local citizens, these requirements are not merely an inconvenience; they represent a significant financial and operational threat that could lead to the collapse of vital community services and multi-generational livelihoods.

[BACKGROUND]
The Environmental Protection Agency, through the Office of Air and Radiation, is proposing a new regulatory framework to update compliance and oversight mechanisms. This proposal introduces a comprehensive overhaul of how regulated entities interact with the agency, focusing specifically on the modernization of reporting obligations and the standardization of documentation. At its core, the proposal establishes new implementation timelines that will dictate how quickly entities must transition to these updated standards. It also includes specific requirements for entities to provide data on "expected costs" associated with the transition and to conduct an "operational impact analysis" review of how these changes will affect day-to-day operations.

While the agency states that the proposal aims to address perceived gaps in current practices and improve compliance monitoring, it has failed to adequately consider the unique challenges faced by small entities in rural areas, particularly agricultural operations and volunteer emergency services. The very nature of this modernized, standardized, and data-intensive framework presupposes a level of administrative infrastructure, digital connectivity, and available personnel that simply does not exist in our communities. As detailed below, these provisions will create insurmountable barriers for the very people who are the backbone of rural America.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
The proposed regulatory framework, while perhaps well-intentioned, fails to account for the practical realities and limitations inherent in volunteer-based emergency services and small-scale agricultural operations. The impacts of these provisions will be devastating and far-reaching.

**Operational Impact Analysis on Volunteer Fire Departments: Diverting Critical Resources from Emergency Response**
As a volunteer firefighter, I can tell you firsthand that our department runs on donated time. Every hour I spend completing an **Operational Impact Analysis** review is an hour I am not training, maintaining equipment, or responding to calls. The proposed requirement to systematically document day-to-day operational impacts assumes a level of administrative infrastructure — dedicated staff, internet connectivity, digital recordkeeping systems — that simply does not exist in a rural Iowa volunteer department. Our department relies on local volunteers, many of whom are farmers like myself, who already juggle full-time livelihoods with their service. During peak seasons like planting and harvest, my availability is already constrained to narrow windows.

The National Volunteer Fire Council has explicitly warned that compliance requirements that 'stretch department resources even thinner' place firefighters and communities at direct risk, and that some departments would be unable to continue operating under such burdens [1]. For us, this is not a hypothetical — it is a foreseeable outcome. The time and resources required to fulfill the **Operational Impact Analysis** would directly divert our limited capacity from emergency response readiness. Our department serves a vast, sparsely populated area, and rapid response is critical. Any diversion of time towards administrative burdens directly compromises our ability to protect lives and property. Furthermore, our volunteer department shares equipment with two neighboring farms and coordinates closely with adjacent volunteer departments for mutual aid. Any compliance framework that requires individual operational impact documentation for each entity involved in shared arrangements multiplies the burden exponentially and could cause neighboring departments to withdraw from mutual aid agreements to avoid triggering additional reporting obligations. This fragmentation of services would be catastrophic for rural emergency response.

**Cost Assessment Requirement on Corn and Soybean Farmers: Unquantified Financial Burdens on Negative Margins**
The EPA’s **Cost Assessment Requirement** to provide data on 'expected costs' of transition imposes an unreasonable and unquantified financial burden on small, independent corn and soybean farmers like myself. We are already operating on razor-thin or, more accurately, negative total margins. As a multi-generational corn and soybean farmer in rural Iowa, I am already absorbing the financial shock of 2024's collapsed commodity prices and rising production costs. According to the Congressional Research Service, total margins for corn and soybeans in 2024 were negative — meaning I am already losing money on every acre before any new compliance obligation is added. The EPA's **Cost Assessment Requirement** would force me to gather, document, and submit detailed financial transition data — a task that requires accounting expertise, digital infrastructure, and staff time that a family farm operation simply does not have.

I share equipment with neighboring farms, which further complicates any attempt to isolate and report 'expected transition costs' on a per-operation basis. For example, if we jointly use a sprayer, how do we accurately attribute the "expected cost" of updating its emissions reporting or documentation standards to individual farms? The USDA’s Emergency Commodity Assistance Program distributed over $9.3 billion to more than 560,000 farmers in 2024 precisely because farm sector profitability had collapsed [3] — yet the EPA is now proposing to layer additional compliance cost burdens on top of this crisis without a credible accounting of what those burdens will cost farmers like me. The increased cost of compliance due to EPA regulations has been estimated to be as high as $50,100 per small business, a figure that is simply unsustainable for a farm already operating at a loss [1, 2].

**New Implementation Timelines on Multi-Generational Family Farms: An Existential Threat to Rural Livelihoods**
As a corn and soybean farmer operating a multi-generational family farm in rural Iowa, I strongly oppose the proposed implementation timelines as written. They impose compliance costs and transition burdens that are financially unsurvivable for small family farming operations already operating on razor-thin margins. My family farm, like the majority of small family farms, operates with an operating profit margin in the high-risk zone — often below 10 percent. Forcing rapid transition to new reporting and documentation standards within the proposed timeline means I must divert capital — capital I do not have in reserve — toward compliance infrastructure rather than seed, equipment maintenance, or debt service. For a multi-generational operation where my children's inheritance and livelihood are at stake, a compliance cost spike during a narrow transition window could be the financial shock that ends 80-plus years of family farming.

The EPA's own draft FY 2023-2024 National Program Guidance for the Office of Air and Radiation discusses the need for engagement with state and local air agencies and territories, but it fails to specifically address the unique timelines required for agricultural entities [5]. Other EPA proposals have considered implementation deadlines of up to 4 years to allow for numerous challenges and milestones [6]. A rapid timeline for agriculture is simply unrealistic. I share equipment with three neighboring farms under informal cost-sharing arrangements; if any one of us cannot absorb compliance costs on the proposed schedule, it disrupts all of our operations simultaneously, creating a domino effect that could jeopardize the viability of multiple family farms. This proposal, if implemented as written, poses an existential threat to the viability of small, independent farms, which are already struggling to survive.

**Updated Documentation Standards and Revised Reporting Obligations: The Digital Divide and Rural Infrastructure Limitations**
The **Updated Documentation Standards** requiring records in new, standardized formats and the **Revised Reporting Obligations** focusing on the modernization of reporting obligations both fail to address the fundamental challenge of digital connectivity in rural Iowa. My farm, like many in our area, does not have reliable high-speed internet access. We rely on satellite internet, which is expensive, slow, and frequently interrupted by weather. Trying to comply with digital reporting requirements or maintain electronic records in standardized formats under these conditions is not merely difficult; it is often impossible. The EPA’s proposal presumes a level of digital infrastructure that simply does not exist for many rural Americans.

Furthermore, these new standards and obligations do not align with the practical, often informal, record-keeping practices common on small family farms and in volunteer fire departments. Farmers often keep records on paper, in logbooks, or through simple spreadsheets, not in complex, standardized digital formats. Volunteer fire departments prioritize paper records for incident reports and equipment maintenance, especially in areas with unreliable power or internet. Requiring a rapid shift to new, standardized digital formats would necessitate significant investments in computer equipment, software, and training, all while battling inadequate internet infrastructure. This would create an undue burden and a significant barrier to compliance, not because entities are unwilling, but because they are structurally unable.

**Impact on Volunteer Firefighter Recruitment and Retention**
Finally, the proposed **Operational Impact Analysis** requirement, as applied to volunteer fire departments and township boards, will accelerate the already-documented national crisis of volunteer firefighter recruitment and retention — an unintended consequence the EPA must address before finalizing this rule. Volunteer firefighting is already in decline nationally, and adding regulatory compliance obligations is a well-documented deterrent to recruitment. I have seen firsthand in our department that potential volunteers — many of them farmers like me with full-time livelihoods — weigh the total time commitment before joining. When that commitment includes not just training and emergency response but also federal paperwork requirements, the calculus shifts against volunteering.

Our department shares equipment with two neighboring farms and coordinates with adjacent volunteer departments; any compliance framework that requires individual operational impact documentation for each entity involved in shared arrangements multiplies the burden exponentially and could cause neighboring departments to withdraw from mutual aid agreements to avoid triggering additional reporting obligations. This further strains an already fragile system, putting communities at greater risk. The EPA must recognize that volunteer time is a finite and precious resource, and diverting it to administrative tasks directly undermines the capacity of these essential community services.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
In light of the significant and disproportionate impacts on rural Iowa farmers and volunteer emergency services, I urge the Environmental Protection Agency to make the following specific changes to the proposed regulatory framework:

1.  **Exempt Volunteer Fire Departments from Operational Impact Analysis:** The EPA should entirely exempt volunteer fire departments and township boards from the **Operational Impact Analysis** requirement. Alternatively, if some level of reporting is deemed absolutely necessary, the agency must create a streamlined, paper-based alternative pathway that does not require internet access or dedicated administrative personnel. Before finalizing any compliance mechanism that touches volunteer emergency services, the EPA should consult directly with the National Volunteer Fire Council and FEMA's U.S. Fire Administration, which have extensive experience with the operational realities of these departments. The agency should also publish a specific small-entity compliance guide tailored to volunteer departments, similar to EPA guidance in other regulatory contexts.

2.  **Conduct Farm-Level Cost Analysis and Establish Small Farm Exemption for Cost Assessment:** In direct response to the agency's question — 'What are the expected costs of the proposed compliance requirements?' — I urge the EPA to conduct and publish a full, farm-level cost analysis before finalizing this rule, specifically disaggregated by operation size and commodity type. The agency should commission an independent economic assessment that accounts for: (1) the cost of hiring outside accounting or consulting help to prepare transition cost data; (2) the opportunity cost of farmer time diverted from planting, harvesting, and equipment maintenance; and (3) the cumulative burden on operations already receiving emergency federal assistance due to negative margins. The agency should also establish a small farm exemption threshold — for example, operations under 1,000 acres or under $500,000 in gross receipts — that either waives the **Cost Assessment Requirement** entirely or substitutes a simplified, one-page attestation form in lieu of detailed financial documentation.

3.  **Extend Implementation Timelines for Small Agricultural Operations:** In direct response to the agency's question about expected costs of the proposed compliance requirements, I urge EPA to conduct a formal Small Farm Economic Impact Analysis before finalizing any implementation timeline. The agency should extend the transition period to a minimum of 5-7 years for agricultural operations with gross revenues under $500,000 annually, with phased milestone checkpoints rather than a single hard deadline. The agency should also explicitly exempt or defer requirements for operations that can demonstrate shared-resource arrangements with neighboring farms, recognizing that compliance costs are not isolated to a single entity in cooperative farming communities.

4.  **Provide Paper-Based Compliance Alternatives for All Rural Entities:** For all aspects of the proposed rule — including **Revised Reporting Obligations** and **Updated Documentation Standards** — the EPA must provide clear, accessible, and equivalent paper-based compliance alternatives for rural entities that lack reliable high-speed internet access or the necessary digital infrastructure. This is not a matter of preference; it is a necessity for achieving equitable compliance in areas where digital solutions are simply not feasible.

5.  **Analyze and Mitigate Impact on Volunteer Firefighter Recruitment:** The EPA should conduct a specific analysis of how this rule interacts with volunteer firefighter recruitment and retention trends before finalizing the **Operational Impact Analysis** requirement. The agency should coordinate with the U.S. Fire Administration, which has published research on volunteer decline, to ensure this rule does not function as an additional structural barrier to volunteer service. As an alternative to the proposed requirement, the EPA should consider allowing township boards to submit a single consolidated operational impact statement on behalf of all volunteer emergency services within their jurisdiction, reducing duplication while still providing the agency with the information it seeks. The agency should also explicitly clarify that mutual aid and equipment-sharing arrangements do not independently trigger additional compliance obligations for each participating entity.

[CONCLUSION]
In conclusion, the proposed regulatory framework, as currently written, poses an unacceptable and unsustainable burden on small family farms and volunteer emergency services in rural Iowa and across the nation. The EPA’s vision of modernized, standardized, and digital compliance does not align with the realities of our limited infrastructure, financial constraints, and reliance on volunteer time. Implementing these provisions without significant modification will not improve environmental oversight; instead, it will jeopardize livelihoods, dismantle community services, and accelerate the decline of rural America.

I urge the EPA to reconsider this proposal through the lens of those who will be most directly affected. We are not large corporations with dedicated compliance departments; we are farmers, firefighters, and neighbors, striving to provide for our families and serve our communities. We ask for a regulatory approach that is practical, flexible, and respectful of the unique challenges we face. We stand ready to work with the EPA to find solutions that achieve environmental goals without sacrificing the essential fabric of rural life.

Sincerely,

Tom Jackson
Volunteer Firefighter
Corn and Soybean Farmer
Rural Iowa

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
Physicians for Social Responsibility (PSR) strongly supports the Environmental Protection Agency’s (EPA) proposed rule to update compliance and oversight mechanisms. As a national organization of medical and public health professionals dedicated to protecting public health from environmental threats, PSR’s work depends critically on the quality, consistency, and timely availability of environmental emissions and exposure data. Our physician members treat patients whose health is directly impacted by pollution, from children with asthma exacerbated by particulate matter to communities living near toxic release sites and nuclear facilities, where exposure data is essential for clinical diagnosis, patient counseling, and epidemiological research. The proposed modernization of reporting obligations and standardization of documentation is a crucial step towards building the rigorous, evidence-based foundation required for effective environmental health advocacy and patient care.

[BACKGROUND]
PSR specifically addresses and offers strong support for the following key provisions of the proposed rule:
*   **Revised Reporting Obligations:** We welcome the establishment of new protocols for submitting data, which promises to improve the completeness and reliability of environmental health data.
*   **New Implementation Timelines:** We endorse the establishment of specific windows for compliance, recognizing the importance of timely data for public health surveillance and research.
*   **Updated Documentation Standards:** We strongly support the requirement for entities to maintain records according to new, standardized formats, which will dramatically enhance data utility for medical and public health analyses.
*   **Cost Assessment Requirement and Operational Impact Analysis:** While we acknowledge the need for these assessments, we urge the EPA to explicitly incorporate public health burden metrics as mandatory components, recognizing that the current absence of standardized data imposes substantial operational costs on public health advocates and researchers.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
1.  **Impact of Updated Documentation Standards on Epidemiological Evidence and Physician Advocacy:**
    Our organization's physician advocates routinely rely on EPA-collected emissions and chemical exposure data to build the evidentiary case for stronger environmental health protections. Currently, inconsistent reporting formats across regulated entities create significant barriers to aggregating and analyzing exposure data across geographic regions and time periods [1]. When our physicians testify before regulatory bodies or publish peer-reviewed research on toxic exposures — including ethylene oxide emissions from commercial sterilizers and chemical manufacturing facilities — fragmented, non-standardized data forces us to spend substantial staff resources on data cleaning and reconciliation rather than analysis [2]. This operational burden is borne by public health organizations, not by the regulated entities, and it directly delays and weakens the medical evidence base we bring to advocacy campaigns. Standardized, machine-readable data formats will dramatically improve the quality of epidemiological evidence available to physician advocates working to link environmental exposures to patient health outcomes — a foundational need for our work in environmental health advocacy.

2.  **Impact of Revised Reporting Obligations on Toxic and Radiation Exposure Research:**
    PSR's physician-researchers and affiliated scientists rely on EPA emissions and exposure data as foundational inputs for epidemiological studies linking air radiation, chemical releases, and toxic exposures to disease outcomes, such as cancer, respiratory disease, and neurological harm in exposed communities. Under current fragmented reporting protocols, our researchers routinely encounter incomplete, inconsistently formatted, or delayed datasets. For instance, the EPA's own Air Quality System (AQS) requires quarterly data reviews and annual certification processes precisely because submitter inconsistencies are endemic [3]. Standardized, modernized reporting protocols would reduce the data-cleaning burden our research teams face and increase the reliability of exposure estimates used in peer-reviewed studies. Furthermore, PSR physicians who advise patients with occupational radiation exposures—including nuclear workers, veterans, and communities near nuclear facilities—rely on coherent, cross-agency exposure data to inform clinical decision-making. The DOE's Occupational Radiation Exposure Reports for CY 2023 and CY 2024 [7] represent a mature federal data infrastructure. If EPA's new reporting protocols establish different definitions, measurement units, dose calculation methodologies, or submission timelines than those used by DOE, physicians and researchers will face irreconcilable datasets—a problem with direct patient safety implications. This is not a hypothetical concern: the PACT Act's toxic exposure screening program [7] has already demonstrated how fragmented federal data systems create gaps in veteran health surveillance, requiring costly remediation.

3.  **Importance of Robust Operational Impact Analysis Incorporating Public Health Burden:**
    As a physician advocacy organization conducting climate and health policy campaigns, PSR's ability to engage meaningfully in regulatory proceedings depends on the quality and completeness of operational impact data disclosed by regulated facilities. When operational impact analyses focus exclusively on industry compliance costs while omitting downstream public health costs—such as increased emergency department visits, respiratory hospitalizations, and heat-related illness burdens documented in communities near regulated facilities—our campaigns are forced to fill evidentiary gaps that the agency itself should be generating [5, 9]. This asymmetry in data availability undermines the credibility and reach of our physician-led advocacy and distorts the cost-benefit calculus that policymakers rely upon. Our physician members treat patients—including children with asthma, elderly patients with cardiovascular disease, and low-income communities with disproportionate toxic exposures—whose health outcomes are measurably worsened by each year of delayed emissions reductions. When operational impact analyses are used procedurally to slow rulemakings rather than to generate genuine compliance intelligence, the human health cost is borne by our patients, not by the regulated entities claiming burden [8].

4.  **Need for Firm Implementation Timelines to Prevent Data Gaps and Health Harms:**
    PSR has direct experience with the downstream consequences of reporting delays. When regulated entities receive extended deadlines — as occurred with both the 2024 Renewable Fuel Standard compliance reporting deadline (extended to March 2025) and the 2024 TSCA Chemical Data Reporting deadline (extended to November 2024) [1, 4, 6] — the resulting data gaps can span months or years in longitudinal health studies. For communities near nuclear facilities or industrial toxic release sites, these gaps mean that exposure reconstructions used in clinical and epidemiological research are incomplete, potentially causing researchers to underestimate cumulative dose and misattribute health outcomes. This directly undermines PSR's mission and the credibility of our physician-led advocacy. We oppose any pressure to extend or phase in compliance timelines in ways that would delay the availability of improved toxic and radiological emissions data, noting that past deadline extensions, while sometimes operationally justified, have created measurable gaps in the health research record that disadvantage communities with the highest exposure burdens.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
In light of the critical public health implications of this rule, PSR offers the following specific recommendations to strengthen its effectiveness:

*   **For Updated Documentation Standards:** The EPA should adopt documentation standards that are not only internally consistent but explicitly interoperable with public health surveillance systems and electronic health record (EHR) data infrastructure. Specifically, the agency should align its standardized formats with the data modernization frameworks described by the National Environmental Health Association (NEHA) in its 2023 informatics needs assessment, which identified cross-disciplinary data integration as a critical gap in environmental public health decision-making. We further recommend that the EPA publish a public-facing API or data portal that allows medical researchers and physician advocacy organizations to access standardized compliance records in real time, without requiring individual FOIA requests [1, 2]. To mitigate transition costs for regulated entities while preserving public health benefits, the EPA should provide phased implementation timelines with technical assistance resources, particularly for smaller facilities. However, the agency should resist any proposal to make standardized formats optional or to grandfather legacy reporting systems indefinitely, as this would perpetuate the data fragmentation that currently hampers independent medical research.

*   **For Revised Reporting Obligations:** The EPA should (1) require that standardized data submissions under the revised protocols be made publicly accessible in machine-readable formats within 90 days of submission, enabling independent health researchers to conduct timely analyses; (2) align the new reporting schema with existing federal toxic exposure research frameworks, such as the Congressionally-funded Toxic Exposures Research Program (TERP), which explicitly calls for 'sharing trusted protocols and biospecimens to the broader research community' to advance evidence-based identification of exposure-disease pathways [8]; and (3) include a data-quality feedback mechanism allowing credentialed public health researchers to flag anomalies in submitted data, strengthening the agency's own compliance monitoring [3]. PSR specifically endorses provisions that would standardize occupational and environmental radiation exposure reporting—but urges the agency to align its new reporting protocols with existing federal frameworks, including DOE occupational radiation exposure reporting standards, to avoid creating duplicative or conflicting data streams that would confuse clinical interpretation and undermine the scientific credibility of exposure assessments used in patient care and policy advocacy [7]. EPA should (1) formally consult with DOE's Office of Environment, Health, Safety and Security during the finalization of new reporting protocols to ensure definitional and methodological alignment; (2) adopt or explicitly cross-reference DOE's established occupational radiation exposure reporting taxonomy where applicable; and (3) include a provision in the final rule requiring that any future revisions to reporting definitions be coordinated with DOE and other relevant federal agencies through an interagency working group, with public notice provided to health research stakeholders. This interoperability requirement would reduce compliance costs for entities that report to multiple agencies and dramatically improve the scientific utility of the resulting data for clinical and epidemiological purposes.

*   **For Operational Impact Analysis and Cost Assessment:** The EPA should revise the Operational Impact Analysis framework to require regulated facilities to report not only internal compliance costs but also estimated externalized health costs using EPA's own established co-benefit valuation methodologies, as demonstrated in the 2024 Final Regulatory Impact Analysis for the Reconsideration of the PM2.5 NAAQS [9], which concluded that the existing 12.0 µg/m³ annual standard was insufficient to protect public health. The agency should mandate that operational impact submissions include facility-level air quality modeling outputs and associated health endpoint estimates—consistent with the analytical approach used in the 2023 EPA RIA for GHG mitigation scenarios [5]—so that public health advocates have a symmetrical evidentiary basis for participation. In response to the agency's specific question regarding 'alternatives to the proposed compliance requirements,' PSR recommends that EPA establish a rebuttable presumption standard within the Operational Impact Analysis: facilities claiming significant operational burden must provide third-party-verified cost data, and the agency should cross-reference claimed compliance costs against sector-wide benchmarks from prior rulemakings. EPA should also set firm, non-extendable deadlines for operational impact submissions to prevent the review process itself from functioning as a de facto compliance delay mechanism. Implementation timelines should be anchored to health-protective milestones, not solely to industry readiness claims.

*   **For New Implementation Timelines:** In response to the agency's specific question regarding operational impacts and alternatives to the proposed compliance requirements: the EPA should establish firm, non-extendable compliance deadlines for the revised reporting protocols, with narrow, criteria-based waiver provisions that require public notice and a demonstrated showing of technical impossibility — not mere administrative inconvenience [1, 4, 6]. The agency should also publish a retrospective analysis of how prior deadline extensions (RFS, CDR) affected data continuity, so that the costs of delay are transparently weighed against the operational burden arguments that regulated industries will inevitably raise. PSR recommends a maximum 18-month implementation timeline with no blanket extensions, paired with EPA technical assistance to help smaller regulated entities meet the new standards.

[CONCLUSION]
PSR strongly supports this proposed rule as a necessary modernization that will significantly strengthen the evidentiary foundation for environmental health advocacy and medical research linking pollution exposure to patient health outcomes. The current fragmented and inconsistent reporting landscape imposes substantial operational costs on public health organizations and creates critical data gaps that directly harm patients and communities. By standardizing documentation, modernizing reporting obligations, and establishing firm implementation timelines, the EPA will not only improve its own oversight capabilities but also empower medical professionals to more effectively diagnose, treat, and prevent environmentally-attributable diseases. We urge the EPA to proceed with full implementation, explicitly account for the public health benefits in its regulatory impact assessment, and establish mechanisms for public access to standardized compliance data to maximize the return on this vital investment in public health protection.

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
Freeport-McMoRan Inc. (Freeport-McMoRan) submits these comments on the proposed rule (Docket ID: EPA-HQ-OAR-2025-0194) published by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). As the largest domestic producer of copper and a significant producer of molybdenum, Freeport-McMoRan plays a critical role in supplying the essential minerals required for the U.S. clean energy transition, including electric vehicles, renewable energy infrastructure, and grid modernization. We strongly oppose the proposed rule's standardized reporting obligations, updated documentation standards, and compressed implementation timelines as applied uniformly to critical mineral producers. These provisions threaten to impose disproportionate operational burdens and compliance costs that could directly impact the continuity of domestic mineral supply, thereby creating a conflict with federal critical minerals policy and undermining the very environmental goals EPA seeks to advance. We urge EPA to adopt flexible, sector-specific, and critical-mineral-conscious approaches that support, rather than hinder, the secure and sustainable supply of these vital resources.

[BACKGROUND]
The proposed rule introduces a comprehensive overhaul of compliance and oversight mechanisms, specifically focusing on the modernization of reporting obligations, the standardization of documentation, and the establishment of new implementation timelines. It further requests feedback on expected costs and operational impacts associated with these changes. Freeport-McMoRan's Arizona operations encompass complex, integrated facilities, including open-pit mines, concentrators, a primary copper smelter, a refinery, tailings storage facilities, and extensive water management and dust suppression systems. These operations are subject to numerous existing reporting and documentation requirements under the Clean Air Act (CAA), Clean Water Act (CWA), Resource Conservation and Recovery Act (RCRA), and Emergency Planning and Community Right-to-Know Act (EPCRA), as well as state-level permits. The proposed rule's uniform application of new standards and timelines fails to account for the unique operational realities, capital planning cycles, and critical mineral supply implications inherent to these facilities.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
1.  **Incompatibility of Uniform Compliance Windows with Multi-Decade Capital Planning:** Freeport-McMoRan's Arizona copper mining, smelting, and refining operations involve capital expenditure commitments made 10–30 years in advance, tied to mine life projections, ore body depletion curves, and infrastructure depreciation schedules. The proposed compliance windows, designed around a generalized regulatory modernization timeline, do not account for the fact that retrofitting reporting systems, documentation infrastructure, or operational controls at active smelting complexes like our Morenci or Bagdad operations requires integration with existing capital project queues that are already committed years into the future. Imposing a fixed compliance deadline that falls mid-cycle in a major capital program forces a choice between regulatory compliance and operational continuity, potentially stranding recently invested capital or requiring duplicative expenditure. Industry data confirms that 83% of major mining and metals projects already exceed planned CAPEX by more than 40% [2]; adding a regulatory compliance cost spike within a compressed, non-negotiable window compounds this overrun risk significantly and can render marginal operations economically unviable. This also runs counter to EPA's FY 2024 goals, which emphasize efficient use of agency resources [1].

2.  **Operational Complexity of Integrated Smelting and Refining Data Submission:** Our Arizona smelting and refining operations, including the Miami Smelter and associated refining infrastructure, operate continuous emissions monitoring systems (CEMS), process control systems, and environmental management platforms that were built and validated under existing EPA reporting frameworks. Migrating these systems to new data submission protocols requires not only software reconfiguration but also re-validation of data integrity pipelines, retraining of environmental compliance staff, and potential interruption of real-time monitoring continuity. Freeport-McMoRan's 10-K filing with the SEC (2023) explicitly identifies new or revised environmental regulatory requirements as a source of 'substantially increased costs,' and this proposed protocol overhaul represents exactly that category of burden. For a smelter processing hundreds of thousands of tons of copper concentrate annually, even a brief compliance gap or data submission error during transition could trigger enforcement exposure disproportionate to any actual environmental harm.

3.  **Disproportionate and Unquantified Costs on Large Integrated Operations:** In direct response to EPA's question regarding the expected costs of the proposed compliance requirements, Freeport-McMoRan notes that our Arizona operations encompass open-pit mines, concentrators, a smelter, a refinery, tailings storage facilities, and water reclamation systems — each subject to distinct existing reporting obligations under multiple EPA programs (CAA, CWA, RCRA, EPCRA). The proposed rule's standardization and documentation overhaul requires not a single system upgrade but a coordinated transformation across all of these reporting streams simultaneously, within the same compliance window. EPA's cost estimates, to the extent they have been published, appear to rely on per-facility averages that do not reflect the multiplicative complexity of integrated, multi-media operations. The KPMG 2024 Global Metals and Mining Outlook documents that 66% of mining executives report increased business risk incidence and severity, and that technology investment demands have surged [2] — meaning our compliance IT and administrative resources are already under significant competing pressure. A compliance window that does not account for this resource contention will either result in inadequate compliance quality or require emergency expenditures that were not budgeted in our long-term capital plans.

4.  **Conflict with Critical Mineral Supply Chain Policy and Clean Energy Goals:** Freeport-McMoRan opposes the proposed standardized documentation framework as applied uniformly to critical mineral producers. Copper and molybdenum are indispensable inputs for electric vehicle motors, wind turbine generators, grid infrastructure, and solar installations. Freeport-McMoRan is among the largest domestic producers of both metals, and any regulatory-driven production disruption at our Arizona operations — whether through forced operational pauses to implement new documentation systems, diversion of capital from production maintenance to compliance infrastructure, or workforce reallocation from operational to administrative functions — directly constrains domestic supply of materials the federal government has separately identified as critical. The proposed rule's implementation timeline, if applied without modification, would require us to undertake major system overhauls during a period when copper demand is projected to surge significantly. Global copper demand is forecast to outpace supply through at least 2025, meaning even marginal reductions in Freeport-McMoRan's output have outsized market consequences for U.S. clean energy manufacturing supply chains. EPA's own economic analyses for other rules, like the Lead and Copper Rule Improvements (LCRI), demonstrate the agency's capacity to estimate costs and benefits [4, 5], but this has not been done adequately for copper.

5.  **Complexity of Water Management and Dust Suppression Operations in Arid Climates:** Freeport-McMoRan opposes the proposed rule's compressed implementation timelines for updated reporting and documentation standards as applied to water management and dust suppression operations. These timelines fail to account for the irreducible physical and logistical complexity of managing integrated water and dust suppression systems across large-scale, arid-climate mining operations spanning hundreds of square miles. Freeport-McMoRan's Arizona copper and molybdenum operations — including Morenci, Bagdad, Safford, and Sierrita — rely on highly interdependent water recycling and dust suppression infrastructure where a single operational change triggers cascading adjustments across tailings impoundments, haul road suppression circuits, concentrator water circuits, and stormwater management systems. Transitioning to new standardized documentation and reporting formats is not a clerical exercise: it requires re-engineering data collection points, recalibrating sensor networks, retraining operational and environmental compliance staff, and validating new data streams against existing permit obligations under the Arizona Department of Environmental Quality and existing EPA permits. The U.S. dust suppression system market is already under significant regulatory pressure from OSHA and EPA, as noted in Grand View Research's industry analysis [6], meaning our compliance infrastructure is already stretched. Imposing a new, parallel documentation regime on an accelerated timeline creates a real risk of compliance gaps — not because of bad faith, but because the physical systems cannot be re-instrumented overnight. EPA's national water guidance also emphasizes resilience in water resources [7], which is directly impacted by these operational complexities.

6.  **Critical Mineral Supply Chain Implications of Compliance Costs:** Freeport-McMoRan opposes the proposed compliance timeline as failing to account for the critical mineral supply implications of forcing compliance costs onto domestic copper producers during a period when U.S. copper supply is essential to the clean energy transition. Freeport-McMoRan is the largest U.S. copper producer, supplying copper essential for electric vehicles, grid infrastructure, wind turbines, and solar installations. The proposed compliance windows impose near-term operational and financial burdens — including system overhauls, documentation standardization, and potential production interruptions during transition — at precisely the moment when domestic copper demand is projected to surge. PwC's Mine 2024 report identifies that fulfilling clean energy goals requires sustained high annual compound growth rates in critical mineral supply, and that recycling alone cannot close the gap [2]. Compliance-driven cost increases or production disruptions at Freeport-McMoRan's Arizona operations would directly constrain domestic copper availability, increase U.S. dependence on foreign copper sources, and undermine the very environmental goals EPA's broader regulatory agenda is designed to advance. EPA has not, to our knowledge, analyzed this supply chain feedback loop in the proposed rule's cost-benefit framework — a significant analytical gap the agency is required to address under Executive Order obligations regarding critical minerals.

7.  **Duplicative and Cost-Prohibitive Documentation Systems:** Freeport-McMoRan opposes the proposed rule's one-size-fits-all approach to documentation standardization. Our Arizona operations already maintain extensive environmental documentation and third-party audited reporting under multiple frameworks, including GRI 14 Mining Sector standards, the Copper Mark Risk Readiness Assessment (RRA), and state-level air and water permit reporting requirements. The proposed rule would require us to build and maintain an entirely separate, EPA-standardized documentation system that largely duplicates information already captured in these existing frameworks. For our water management systems alone — which involve complex mine tailings impoundment monitoring, reclaimed water tracking, and arid-climate evaporation accounting across multiple sites — the cost of reformatting and re-platforming existing data into EPA's proposed standardized schema is estimated to require 18–24 months of IT development and $15–25 million in system integration costs, without producing any new environmental information that is not already available to regulators through existing permit reporting channels.

8.  **Lack of Adequate Cost Analysis Specific to Primary Copper Smelting:** Freeport-McMoRan opposes the proposed protocols because the agency has failed to conduct an adequate cost analysis specific to primary copper smelting operations, leaving the true compliance cost burden unquantified and potentially understated—directly responsive to the agency's own request for data on expected costs of the proposed compliance requirements. EPA's economic impact analyses for air pollution regulations, as catalogued on the agency's own website, include sector-specific assessments for source categories such as taconite ore processing, but no equivalent analysis exists for primary copper smelting and refining under this proposed reporting modernization framework. Freeport-McMoRan operates one of the few remaining domestic primary copper smelters in the United States. The costs of compliance for our Arizona operations are not analogous to those of simpler stationary sources: our facilities must simultaneously track sulfur dioxide, particulate matter, hazardous air pollutants, and process-integrated parameters across smelting, converting, anode casting, and electrolytic refining stages. Implementing new data submission protocols across this operational complexity will require capital investment in IT infrastructure, third-party system integration, and ongoing staff resources that EPA has not estimated. Without a facility-type-specific cost analysis, the agency cannot make a reasoned determination that the benefits of standardized reporting outweigh the costs imposed on this sector. While EPA's FY 2023 performance report discusses general compliance efforts [3], it does not provide the specific cost analysis needed for this complex sector.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
In light of the significant operational impacts and compliance costs, and to ensure the proposed rule does not inadvertently undermine the secure domestic supply of critical minerals essential for the clean energy transition, Freeport-McMoRan offers the following recommendations:

1.  **For New Implementation Timelines (Compliance Windows):** EPA should replace the proposed uniform compliance windows with a tiered, facility-specific compliance scheduling mechanism that allows regulated entities to submit a Mine Life Cycle Compliance Integration Plan (MLCCIP) demonstrating how and when new reporting and documentation requirements will be incorporated into the next scheduled major capital project or facility upgrade cycle. Deadlines should be anchored to facility-specific milestones — such as smelter rebuild cycles, tailings facility expansion permits, or major equipment replacement schedules — rather than a single calendar date. EPA should also establish a formal process for granting compliance schedule extensions of up to 5 years where an entity can demonstrate that the proposed deadline falls within an active capital commitment period and that early compliance would require redundant or stranded investment, as evidenced by major mining project overruns [2].

2.  **For Revised Reporting Obligations (Data Submission Protocols for Smelting/Refining):** The agency should extend the implementation timeline to no fewer than 36 months from the effective date of the final rule for smelting and refining operations, and should establish a phased compliance schedule that allows facilities to pilot new submission protocols in parallel with existing systems before full cutover. EPA should also convene a technical working group that includes representatives from primary copper smelters to co-develop data field definitions and submission formats that are compatible with the continuous, high-volume nature of smelter emissions monitoring — rather than applying a one-size-fits-all framework designed for simpler source categories.

3.  **For Cost Assessment Requirement (Revised Regulatory Impact Analysis):** EPA should: (1) publish a revised Regulatory Impact Analysis that disaggregates compliance cost estimates by facility type, specifically distinguishing integrated mining/smelting/refining complexes from single-medium or single-facility operations; (2) extend the compliance window by a minimum of 24 months for facilities operating under five or more concurrent EPA reporting programs, recognizing the coordination complexity involved; and (3) establish a pre-compliance technical assistance program — consistent with EPA's own stated commitment to efficient use of agency resources [1] — that allows large integrated operators to work with EPA regional staff to develop facility-specific compliance roadmaps before the compliance clock begins running, reducing the risk of costly mid-stream corrections.

4.  **For Updated Documentation Standards and New Implementation Timelines (Critical Mineral Exemptions/Pathways):** In response to the agency's question on operational impacts, we recommend EPA: (1) conduct an inter-agency review with the Department of Energy and Department of the Interior to assess whether the proposed compliance timeline creates conflicts with critical mineral supply chain objectives established under Executive Order 14017 and the Inflation Reduction Act; (2) create a 'Critical Mineral Producer' compliance pathway that allows phased implementation tied to production cycle milestones rather than calendar deadlines; and (3) explicitly exempt or defer compliance obligations for operational systems directly tied to active mining and processing circuits during peak production periods, with make-up compliance windows during scheduled maintenance shutdowns. The agency should not finalize uniform implementation timelines without first completing this inter-agency coordination.

5.  **For Updated Documentation Standards and New Implementation Timelines (Water Management & Dust Suppression):** The agency should, in direct response to its own question regarding 'operational impacts of the proposed compliance requirements,' establish a tiered implementation schedule that grants large-scale arid-climate mining operations with integrated water and dust suppression systems a minimum 36-month transition period, with interim milestones. EPA should also convene a technical working group inclusive of mining sector operators to define operationally feasible data standardization formats before finalizing documentation requirements, rather than imposing a one-size-fits-all framework designed primarily around less complex industrial facilities, especially given the existing regulatory pressures on dust suppression systems [6].

6.  **For Operational Impact Analysis and Cost Assessment Requirement (Critical Mineral Supply Chain Impact Assessment):** In direct response to the agency's question regarding the operational impacts and costs of the proposed compliance requirements, EPA should: (1) commission or incorporate a Critical Mineral Supply Chain Impact Assessment that quantifies the effect of compliance-driven cost increases and potential production disruptions on domestic copper supply; (2) coordinate with the Department of Energy and Department of Interior — both of which have active critical mineral supply security mandates — before finalizing compliance timelines; and (3) establish a Critical Mineral Producer carve-out or extended compliance schedule for facilities producing minerals designated as critical under the Energy Act of 2020, recognizing that the national interest in uninterrupted domestic critical mineral production constitutes a compelling basis for schedule flexibility, as highlighted by the need for sustained critical mineral supply growth [2].

7.  **For Updated Documentation Standards (Alternative Documentation Framework):** In response to the agency's question on alternatives to the proposed compliance requirements, we recommend EPA: (1) formally recognize GRI 14 Mining Sector 2024 reporting and Copper Mark RRA audit documentation as presumptively equivalent to the proposed standardized reporting requirements for facilities that maintain current certification under both frameworks; (2) establish a mutual recognition or 'safe harbor' provision allowing operators to petition for alternative compliance demonstrations based on existing third-party audited environmental management systems; and (3) require EPA regional offices to conduct a gap analysis between existing permit reporting obligations and the proposed new standards before imposing additional documentation requirements on facilities already subject to comprehensive state and federal environmental oversight, similar to the approach for cost analysis in the LCRI [4, 5]. This approach would achieve the agency's stated goal of improved compliance monitoring at a fraction of the cost, while avoiding the unintended consequence of diverting capital from actual environmental controls to administrative documentation systems.

8.  **For Revised Reporting Obligations and Cost Assessment Requirement (Smelter-Specific Cost Analysis):** In direct response to the agency's question on expected costs, EPA should commission or require submission of a sector-specific compliance cost analysis for primary copper smelters before finalizing any implementation timeline. The agency should also provide a formal small-number-of-facilities analysis given that domestic primary copper smelting capacity is concentrated in a handful of facilities, meaning aggregate national cost estimates will systematically underrepresent per-facility burden. Freeport-McMoRan requests that EPA publish a supplemental economic analysis and reopen the comment period for 60 days following its release.

[CONCLUSION]
Freeport-McMoRan Inc. appreciates the opportunity to provide comments on the proposed rule. We are deeply concerned that, as currently structured, the rule's uniform application of new compliance and reporting standards threatens to impose unquantified and disproportionate costs and operational burdens on the domestic critical minerals industry. This approach risks disrupting the secure supply of copper and molybdenum, which are indispensable for the U.S. clean energy transition, thereby creating a direct conflict with federal critical minerals policy. We urge EPA to adopt flexible, sector-specific, and critical-mineral-conscious approaches, including extended implementation timelines, tailored documentation standards, inter-agency coordination, and a formal Critical Mineral Supply Chain Impact Assessment, to ensure that regulatory modernization supports, rather than hinders, the nation's environmental and economic objectives. We remain committed to working collaboratively with EPA to achieve effective and sustainable environmental outcomes.

### References
[1] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2023-03/fy-2024-congressional-justification-all-tabs.pdf
[2] https://www.pwc.com/id/en/pwc-publications/industries-publications/energy--utilities---mining-publications/mine-2024.html

[4] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2024-10/508_lcri_final-ea_appendices_10_23_24.pdf
[5] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2024-10/final_lcri_fact-sheet_cost_benefit.pdf
[6] https://www.grandviewresearch.com/industry-analysis/dust-suppression-system-market-report
[7] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2022-08/fy-2023-2024-ow-npg.pdf

{"draft": "[INTRODUCTION]
The League of Conservation Voters (LCV) writes to express our strong support for the Environmental Protection Agency's (EPA) proposed framework to update compliance and oversight mechanisms. This proposal, including its revised reporting obligations, new implementation timelines, updated documentation standards, and cost assessment requirement, represents a critical step towards modernizing environmental regulation and ensuring accountability for polluters. LCV is a national non-profit organization dedicated to electing pro-environment candidates and advocating for sound environmental policies. Our work relies heavily on the transparency, consistency, and accessibility of environmental compliance data to inform our annual National Environmental Scorecard, hold elected officials accountable, and mobilize public support for protective standards.

We commend EPA for introducing this comprehensive overhaul, which will significantly improve the agency's ability to monitor compliance and assess the effectiveness of environmental regulations. The American public broadly supports strong environmental protections, and this proposal will provide the robust data infrastructure necessary to demonstrate the effectiveness of those protections and inform future policy decisions.

[BACKGROUND]
EPA's proposed rule introduces a new regulatory framework to modernize compliance and oversight. The key provisions include:
*   **Revised Reporting Obligations:** Entities must adhere to new protocols for submitting data.
*   **New Implementation Timelines:** Specific windows for compliance transition.
*   **Updated Documentation Standards:** Entities must maintain records in new, standardized formats.
*   **Cost Assessment Requirement:** Entities are encouraged to provide data on "expected costs" associated with the transition.
*   **Operational Impact Analysis:** Review of how changes affect day-to-day operations of facilities.

LCV views these provisions as essential for strengthening EPA's ability to enforce environmental laws and for providing the public with the information needed to hold both polluters and elected officials accountable.

[ANALYSIS WITH SPECIFIC IMPACTS]
LCV's core mission depends on accurate, comparable, and timely environmental data. The proposed changes have direct, significant impacts on our operational effectiveness and our ability to advance environmental protection.

**Revised Reporting Obligations:**
LCV strongly supports EPA's revised reporting obligations as a critical tool for generating the transparent, standardized environmental compliance data that underpins our annual Legislative Scorecard [3]. When regulated entities report under inconsistent or outdated protocols, the underlying data on air quality violations, greenhouse gas emissions, and chemical releases becomes difficult to aggregate and compare across congressional districts and states. This forces our policy analysts to spend significant staff resources reconciling disparate data sets before we can assess whether an elected official's voting record aligns with real-world environmental outcomes in their district. EPA's own FY2024 enforcement results demonstrate that improved online data tools — such as the Water Quality Indicators tool — meaningfully enhanced public ability to identify pollutant hotspots, validating that standardized reporting directly enables the kind of district-level analysis LCV conducts [3]. The revised reporting obligations, by modernizing and standardizing how 800,000+ regulated facilities submit data, would substantially reduce this analytical burden and improve the accuracy of our scorecard assessments.

**New Implementation Timelines:**
LCV supports the proposed implementation timelines for the revised reporting obligations and urges EPA to resist pressure to extend transition periods, as delayed compliance timelines create data gaps that directly undermine the integrity of our electoral-cycle scorecard and disadvantage pro-environment candidates who rely on accurate environmental records [7]. Our scorecard is published on an electoral cycle — typically in advance of primary and general elections — meaning that data gaps or reporting delays caused by extended compliance transition periods can force us to rely on outdated or incomplete facility-level records when evaluating an incumbent's environmental record. This is not a hypothetical concern: EPA's finalization of new greenhouse gas reporting obligations in 2024 gave some facilities until July 2025 to comply, creating a window during which emissions data for those facilities will be absent from public records during a critical election year [7]. When data is missing, LCV must either omit relevant environmental metrics from our scorecard or rely on proxy measures, both of which reduce the precision and credibility of our bipartisan accountability tool. Compressed or inconsistent compliance windows also make it harder for LCV to coordinate with coalition partners who use the same federal data streams.

**Updated Documentation Standards:**
LCV strongly supports the EPA's proposed Updated Documentation Standards and standardized formats as a critical tool for ensuring data integrity in environmental compliance reporting [4]. Standardized, machine-readable data formats will dramatically improve LCV's ability to track and evaluate the environmental performance of regulated entities and the elected officials who oversee them — directly strengthening the evidentiary foundation of our annual National Environmental Scorecard. Currently, inconsistent documentation formats across regulated entities and reporting periods force our policy staff to spend significant resources reconciling disparate data sets before we can draw meaningful conclusions about whether environmental protections are working. Standardized formats would reduce this reconciliation burden, allow faster turnaround on policy analysis during electoral cycles, and strengthen the bipartisan credibility of our scorecard by grounding it in uniformly collected, auditable data. This directly addresses the agency's question about operational impacts of the proposed compliance requirements — for advocacy organizations like LCV that depend on public regulatory data, the operational impact is strongly positive.

**Cost Assessment Requirement:**
LCV strongly supports the Cost Assessment Requirement as a mechanism to generate rigorous, standardized cost data that will strengthen — not undermine — the evidentiary foundation for ambitious federal environmental policy [1], [2]. LCV's federal environmental policy campaigns depend on credible, publicly available cost-benefit data to counter industry-funded narratives that overstate regulatory burdens. When EPA's own cost assessments are incomplete — for example, the agency's recent decision to stop monetizing PM2.5 and ozone health benefits in air pollution rulemakings (see Evidence [5]) — it creates an asymmetric information environment that our campaigns must work against, often without equivalent institutional resources. A well-designed Cost Assessment Requirement that mandates comprehensive data submission from regulated entities gives LCV and allied organizations a richer evidentiary record to draw upon when advocating for protective standards and scoring elected officials on their environmental records. Conversely, a narrowly designed requirement that only captures industry compliance costs without corresponding benefit data would hand opponents of environmental regulation a one-sided dataset to weaponize in legislative and electoral contexts.

LCV also supports the Cost Assessment Requirement's goal of standardizing cost data submission, but urges EPA to design the data collection instrument to prevent strategic overstatement of compliance costs by regulated industries — a well-documented phenomenon that, if unchecked, will distort the agency's policy record and complicate LCV's bipartisan advocacy efforts. LCV's bipartisan credibility — a core operational constraint — depends on our ability to cite cost figures that are methodologically defensible across the political spectrum. When regulated industries submit self-reported compliance cost estimates without standardized methodology or third-party verification, those figures routinely exceed actual realized costs, as documented in the academic literature on regulatory compliance costs. This inflation of projected costs has historically been used to argue against protective environmental rules in Congress and in electoral campaigns, directly undermining LCV's scorecard and candidate advocacy work. If the Cost Assessment Requirement simply aggregates unverified industry self-reports, it risks institutionalizing inflated cost figures into EPA's official record — figures that opponents of environmental regulation will cite for years. This is particularly consequential during electoral cycles when LCV is actively communicating candidates' environmental records to voters.

Finally, LCV supports the Cost Assessment Requirement and urges EPA to use the data collected to affirmatively demonstrate that the long-term economic costs of inaction on air quality — including health system costs, lost labor productivity, and climate damages — substantially exceed the compliance costs borne by regulated entities, thereby building a durable public record that supports protective environmental policy across electoral cycles [1], [3], [10]. LCV's federal environmental policy campaigns operate on electoral cycle timelines, meaning we need durable, agency-generated cost records that remain authoritative across administrations. When EPA collects comprehensive cost data under this requirement but fails to contextualize it against the costs of regulatory inaction, the resulting record is vulnerable to being selectively cited by future administrations or Congresses to justify rollbacks. LCV has direct experience with this dynamic: the repeal of EPA's Carbon Pollution Standards generated significant hidden costs — including projected increases of 93–285 thousand tons of annual SO2 emissions [3] — that were not adequately captured in the agency's cost record at the time of repeal. A Cost Assessment Requirement that generates only forward-looking compliance cost data, without anchoring it to the costs of the environmental harms being addressed, creates an incomplete record that weakens the legal and political durability of the underlying regulation.

**EPA's Own IT Infrastructure:**
LCV supports the proposed compliance modernization framework and specifically urges EPA to address the agency's own information technology and data security infrastructure as a prerequisite to imposing new reporting obligations on regulated entities — ensuring that the data collected under the Cost Assessment Requirement and broader reporting overhaul is actually secure, usable, and actionable [6], [7]. LCV's federal environmental policy campaigns rely on EPA-published compliance and emissions data to hold polluters accountable and to score congressional votes on environmental oversight. If EPA's internal IT systems cannot securely manage the increased volume and sensitivity of data generated by the proposed modernized reporting framework — a concern directly raised by EPA's own Inspector General — then the entire premise of the rule is undermined [6]. LCV cannot credibly campaign for stronger reporting requirements if the agency lacks the infrastructure to protect and utilize the resulting data. This is particularly acute given our coalition coordination role: partner organizations rely on LCV to vet the operational soundness of rules we jointly support.

[RECOMMENDATIONS]
In response to the agency's request for feedback, LCV offers the following specific recommendations to strengthen the proposed framework:

1.  **Revised Reporting Obligations:** EPA should finalize the revised reporting obligations with an explicit requirement that all standardized data submissions be made publicly accessible through a searchable, machine-readable online portal within 90 days of submission deadlines. The agency should also ensure that the new data fields align with geographic identifiers (e.g., congressional district, county) so that advocacy organizations, journalists, and the public can readily connect facility-level compliance data to the elected officials who represent those communities. LCV recommends EPA model this approach on the improvements already demonstrated through the 2024 Chemical Data Reporting update and the Water Quality Indicators tool [3].

2.  **New Implementation Timelines:** In response to the agency's specific question about operational impacts of the proposed compliance requirements, LCV recommends that EPA establish a single, uniform compliance effective date for all covered entities under the revised reporting obligations, rather than creating tiered or facility-specific exemption windows as was done in the 2024 GHG rule. Where phased timelines are operationally necessary, EPA should publish a clear public-facing calendar indicating which facility categories are reporting under which timeline, so that data users like LCV can accurately characterize the completeness of any given reporting cycle. EPA should also commit to flagging incomplete data sets in its public-facing tools to prevent misinterpretation [7].

3.  **Updated Documentation Standards:** The EPA should finalize the standardized documentation formats as proposed and additionally require that all submitted data be made publicly available in open, machine-readable formats (e.g., JSON, CSV) through a centralized EPA data portal. This would extend the data integrity benefits beyond regulated entities to the broader public interest community. LCV further recommends that the agency establish a public-facing data dictionary and metadata standard consistent with FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) data principles, as outlined in peer-reviewed Earth and environmental science literature, to ensure that civil society organizations, researchers, and the public can fully utilize the standardized data [4], [5].

4.  **Cost Assessment Requirement:**
    *   EPA should explicitly require that entities submitting 'expected costs' data under the Cost Assessment Requirement also provide, to the extent feasible, data on avoided costs — including avoided health expenditures, productivity losses, and ecosystem service losses — associated with the environmental outcomes the regulation is designed to achieve. The agency should further commit to monetizing health co-benefits (including PM2.5 and ozone reductions) in its own accompanying analysis, reversing the troubling precedent identified in recent rulemakings [5]. This symmetry is essential to producing a cost record that is analytically complete and legally defensible.
    *   In response to the agency's specific question about 'what are the expected costs of the proposed compliance requirements,' EPA should require that cost submissions under the Cost Assessment Requirement adhere to the agency's own established cost estimation methodology, as documented in EPA's Cost Reports and Guidance for Air Pollution Regulations and the Guidelines for Preparing Economic Analyses [2], [4]. Submissions should include disclosure of the methodology used, key assumptions, and sensitivity ranges. EPA should also conduct independent verification of a statistically representative sample of submissions, and publish all submitted data in a machine-readable format in the public docket so that third parties — including advocacy organizations like LCV — can audit and analyze the data. This transparency mechanism is essential to the integrity of the cost record.
    *   EPA should treat the Cost Assessment Requirement not merely as a data collection exercise but as an opportunity to build a comprehensive cost record that includes: (1) industry-submitted compliance cost data, standardized per the agency's existing guidance; (2) EPA's own quantified and monetized estimates of the health and environmental benefits of compliance, including PM2.5, ozone, and climate co-benefits consistent with IRA tax credit frameworks and NREL reference case projections [1], [10]; and (3) an explicit comparison of compliance costs against the documented costs of inaction, drawing on peer-reviewed literature such as the RFF analysis of hidden costs from regulatory repeal [3]. This three-part structure would produce a cost record that is analytically complete, legally defensible, and resistant to selective citation in future deregulatory proceedings — directly serving LCV's interest in durable environmental policy.

5.  **EPA's Own IT Infrastructure:** In response to the agency's question about operational impacts of the proposed compliance requirements, EPA should commit to a parallel IT modernization investment timeline that aligns with the new implementation deadlines imposed on regulated entities. Specifically, EPA should reference and respond to the OIG's FY2023 FISMA audit findings — which identified that EPA achieved only a Level 3 'Consistently Implemented' maturity rating, falling short of the Level 4 'Managed and Measurable' benchmark — and publish a remediation roadmap as part of the final rule's implementation plan [6]. Regulated entities and advocacy stakeholders alike need assurance that compliance data will be securely managed before new reporting burdens are imposed.

[CONCLUSION]
LCV strongly supports EPA's proposed compliance modernization framework. This rule represents a critical step in strengthening environmental protection by improving the transparency, consistency, and accessibility of environmental compliance data. The American public overwhelmingly supports strong environmental standards, and this proposal will provide the necessary infrastructure to hold polluters accountable and ensure that environmental protections are effective. We urge EPA to finalize this rule in a timely manner, incorporating the recommendations outlined above to maximize its effectiveness and ensure the integrity of the regulatory record.

### References
[1] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2025-02/epa-2023-reference-case.pdf
[2] https://www.epa.gov/economic-and-cost-analysis-air-pollution-regulations/cost-reports-and-guidance-air-pollution
[3] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2025-03/eoy2024.pdf
[4] https://www.epa.gov/system/files/documents/2025-12/epa-fy-2024-agency-financial-report.pdf
[5] https://www.epa.gov/sites/default/files/oig/documents/_epaoig_20250527-25-p-0033_cert.pdf
[6] https://www.epa.gov/office-inspector-general/report-epa-needs-develop-and-implement-information-technology-processes
[7] https://www.epa.gov/newsreleases/epa-releases-2023-data-collected-under-greenhouse-gas-reporting-program
